subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his delayed speech development and lack of social interactions. She reports that he does not respond when his name is called, avoids making eye contact, and prefers to play alone. The child has a particular interest in lining up his toy cars and becomes extremely upset with any change in routine. His play is repetitive, and he shows little interest in playing with other children. On examination, the pediatrician notes that the child does not follow pointing gestures and does not point at objects to show interest. Based on the clinical presentation, the pediatrician suspects autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Which of the following is a common clinical feature of ASD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hyperactivity and impulsivity - These symptoms are more characteristic of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) than ASD. Although some children with ASD can display hyperactivity and impulsivity, these are not core features of ASD.
• Option B. Intellectual disability - While some individuals with ASD may also have intellectual disability , it is not a defining characteristic of ASD. Intellectual abilities in individuals with ASD can range from superior to severely challenged.
• Option D. Rapid language acquisition - This is not typically associated with ASD . In fact, individuals with ASD often experience delays in language development, and some may not develop significant spoken language.
• Educational Objective:
• ASD is characterized by challenges in social communication , repetitive behaviors , and in some cases, unusual responses to sensory stimuli , such as hypersensitivity or hyposensitivity . Recognizing these features is crucial for early diagnosis and intervention , which can significantly improve outcomes for children with ASD.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother due to concerns about his performance in school. His teachers report that he often seems to be "in his own world" during class, frequently fails to complete assignments, and is easily distracted by minor noises or movements in the classroom. At home, his mother notes that he forgets to do his chores, has difficulty organizing his tasks, and loses his school supplies regularly. During the evaluation, the boy frequently interrupts the conversation, fidgets in his seat, and struggles to wait his turn to speak. After a thorough evaluation, it is determined that the boy meets 7 of the 9 criteria for inattention according to the DSM-5 guidelines and displays several impulsive behaviors. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Give the child a 2-week trial of stimulant medication
Send the child for a complete psychoeducational assessment
Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate

explanation.short: Ans. C) Send the child for a complete psychoeducational assessment
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Give the child a 2-week trial of stimulant medication - While stimulant medications are a cornerstone in the treatment of ADHD , initiating treatment should ideally follow a thorough assessment, including a psychoeducational evaluation, especially in complex cases or when comorbidities are suspected.
• Option B. Arrange for special education placement - Special education placement may be part of the management for a child with ADHD , but it should be based on a comprehensive assessment of the child's educational needs and not as an initial step without a complete evaluation.
• Option D. Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate - This option is inappropriate because the described behaviors significantly impair the child's functioning in multiple settings , suggesting that they are beyond typical age-appropriate behavior.
• Educational Objective:
• The appropriate management steps after an initial ADHD evaluation suggests a comprehensive psychoeducational assessment for confirming the diagnosis , and to rule out other conditions , understand the child's unique needs , and guiding the management plan , which may include educational adjustments, behavioral strategies , and medication .
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 2-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic by her mother, who is concerned about her daughter's developmental progress. The mother reports that her daughter began walking at 18 months, later than most children, and has recently started to lose the ability to use her hands effectively; she often wrings her hands together and has difficulty grasping toys. The child was noted to have microcephaly at her 12-month check-up, and her growth has remained below the 3rd percentile for her age. The pediatrician also notes a history of feeding difficulties, including poor suck and swallow coordination. Given the history of normal early development followed by regression in motor skills, loss of purposeful hand movements, and the presence of stereotypic movements, the pediatrician suspects Rett’s syndrome. Which of the following is a common clinical feature of Rett’s syndrome?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Progressive loss of motor skills
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Delayed language development - While children with Rett’s syndrome may experience delays in language and communication skills , the most distinctive feature of the disorder is the regression in motor skills and the development of stereotypic hand movements.
• Option B. Hyperactivity and impulsivity - These are not hallmark features of Rett’s syndrome and are more commonly associated with other disorders such as ADHD.
• Option C. Abnormalities in facial features - While some genetic syndromes are characterized by distinct facial features , Rett’s syndrome is not typically associated with specific abnormalities in facial features.
• Educational Objective:
• Rett’s syndrome , is a rare genetic neurological and developmental disorder that affects the way the brain develops , resulting in a progressive loss of motor skills and language . Recognizing the pattern of normal development (for the first 6-18 months) followed by regression in motor skills and the presence of stereotypic hand movements is the key to suspecting and diagnosing Rett’s syndrome.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 60

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 13-year-old boy is referred to a psychiatric clinic by his school due to concerns about his behavior. Teachers and his parents report a pattern of aggressive outbursts, physical fights, and deliberate destruction of property. The boy has been caught stealing from classmates and lying about his actions on multiple occasions. His academic performance has declined over the past year, and he has been suspended several times for vandalism and fighting. The boy shows little remorse for his actions and tends to blame others for provoking him. His past medical history is unremarkable, and there is no evidence of a significant learning disorder or intellectual disability. Based on the boy's history of aggressive behavior, violation of social norms, and lack of empathy, the psychiatrist suspects a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by these features. Which of the following is a common clinical feature of the suspected disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. ADHD: Inattention , hyperactivity , and impulsivity are core features of ADHD , along with potential difficulties in executive functions. ADHD does not inherently include aggressive or antisocial behaviors as defining features.
• Option C. Dyslexia: This learning disorder is characterized by difficulties with accurate and/or fluent word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities, not by aggressive or antisocial behavior.
• Option D. Autism spectrum disorder: ASD is characterized by difficulties in social communication and repetitive behaviors , but it does not typically include aggressive or antisocial behaviors as defining features unless in response to sensory overload or extreme stress.
• Educational Objective:
• Conduct disorder , a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by a repetitive and persistent pattern of behavior that violates the rights of others or major societal norms . Recognizing the clinical features of conduct disorder is crucial for early intervention and management, which may include behavioral therapy and family counseling.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his frequent episodes of "daydreaming." She reports that during these episodes, which last several seconds and occur multiple times a day, her son appears completely disengaged and unresponsive to verbal cues. Outside of these episodes, the boy is described as attentive and capable of completing tasks without difficulty. His academic performance is satisfactory, and he is not known to be disruptive in the classroom. The mother is worried these episodes are affecting his learning and social interactions. Which of the following is the best next step in management?
options: Obtain further information from his parents and teachers with the Conners rating scale.
Begin a program of behavioral modification.
Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate.
Send the child for an electroencephalogram.

explanation.short: Ans. D) Send the child for an electroencephalogram.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Obtain further information from his parents and teachers with the Conners rating scale - While the Conners rating scale is useful for assessing symptoms of ADHD, the child's symptoms are more suggestive of absence seizures than attentional issues, making an EEG a more appropriate next step.
• Option B. Begin a program of behavioral modification - Behavioral modification is a common approach for managing ADHD and other behavioral issues , but it would not address the underlying issue if the child's symptoms are due to absence seizures.
• Option C. Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate - Given the frequency and nature of the episodes, reassurance without further investigation could lead to a delay in the diagnosis and treatment of a potential seizure disorder.
• Educational Objective:
• Child with symptoms of frequent , brief episodes of apparent daydreaming and unresponsiveness , are suggestive of absence seizures , and an EEG is the appropriate diagnostic test to confirm this diagnosis and guide further management.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56-59

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the outpatient pediatric department by his mother, who expresses concerns that her son might have Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). She describes him as being extremely restless at home, having a persistent inability to focus on tasks, and frequently misplacing his belongings. Despite these concerns, the child appears cooperative during the visit and his physical examination does not reveal any abnormalities. The mother is eager for advice on how to manage her son's symptoms and improve his ability to concentrate and remain organized. Which of the following is the best next step in management?
options: Give the child a 2-week trial of stimulant medication
Obtain further information from the parents and teachers
Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate
Send the child for psychological assessment

explanation.short: Ans. B) Obtain further information from the parents and teachers.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Give the child a 2-week trial of stimulant medication - Starting medication without a thorough assessment and definitive diagnosis is inappropriate . Stimulant medications are a mainstay in the treatment of ADHD but should only be considered after a comprehensive evaluation confirms the diagnosis.
• Option C . Reassure the child’s mother that this behavior is age appropriate - While some level of inattention and restlessness can be normal for children , the mother's concerns and the description of the child's behavior warrant a thorough evaluation to rule out ADHD or other disorders.
• Option D. Send the child for psychological assessment - While a psychological assessment may be part of the evaluation process for ADHD , the initial step should involve gathering detailed information from those who observe the child in various settings. If the initial information supports the possibility of ADHD, a more in-depth psychological assessment might then be warranted.
• Educational Objective:
• The initial approach to evaluate a child suspected of having ADHD - Comprehensive evaluation including obtaining a detailed information from parents and teachers , is essential before considering a diagnosis of ADHD and discussing treatment options. This ensures that any intervention is based on a thorough understanding of the child's behaviors across different environments.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56-59

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 10-year-old child to a neurology clinic concerned about her child's sudden, involuntary movements. She reports that these movements involve the face and limbs and have been occurring for more than a year. Additionally, she mentions that her child occasionally makes unexpected vocal sounds, such as grunts or throat clearing, which seem involuntary. The child's academic performance is good, and there are no concerns regarding cognitive development. However, the repetitive nature of the movements and sounds, along with their involuntary appearance, prompts the neurologist to consider a diagnosis of Tourette's syndrome, a type of tic disorder. Which of the following is a common clinical feature of tic disorders?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Impaired coordination and balance - This is not a defining feature of tic disorders , although some individuals with tic disorders might have motor tics that temporarily disrupt their coordination or balance.
• Option B. Hyperactivity and impulsivity - While ADHD is a common comorbidity with tic disorders , hyperactivity and impulsivity are not clinical features of tic disorders themselves but rather of ADHD.
• Option D. Difficulty with memory and learning - Tic disorders do not inherently involve difficulties with memory and learning . Any such difficulties are more likely to be related to comorbid conditions or the social and academic impact of the tics.
• Educational Objective
• Obsessive-compulsive symptoms are common in individuals with tic disorders , especially those with Tourette's syndrome . These symptoms can include repetitive behaviors, intrusive thoughts, and compulsions.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56-59

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her child to the pediatric clinic concerned about developmental delays. The child has been previously diagnosed with an autosomal recessive disorder resulting from a deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase. The child presents with intellectual disability, a common manifestation of this metabolic condition. The pediatrician recognizes this as a classic presentation of Phenylketonuria (PKU), a disorder characterized by an inability to metabolize phenylalanine, leading to its accumulation and subsequent neurotoxic effects. Given the context of genetic disorders and their association with intellectual disability, the pediatrician considers other conditions that might present similarly. All of the following genetic disorders can lead to intellectual disability except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Galactosemia - This is a metabolic disorder that can lead to intellectual disability if untreated . It is caused by the inability to metabolize galactose properly.
• Option B. Phenylketonuria - PKU is directly related to the case described and is known to cause intellectual disability if not managed with a strict dietary regimen from an early age.
• Option D. Neurofibromatosis - This genetic disorder can affect the nervous system, including the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. Neurofibromatosis can lead to learning disabilities and intellectual disabilities in some cases.
• Educational Objective
• Common Genetic Disorders associated with Intellectual disability are –
• Galactosemia Phenylketonuria Homocystinuria Argininemia Lesch-nyhan disease Maple syrup urine disease Tyrosinemia Tuberous sclerosis Neurofibromatosis
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 8205

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-year-old child is referred to a psychologist by his school due to persistent academic difficulties, particularly in reading and writing. His teacher reports that despite average intelligence and effort, the child struggles to recognize words, confuses letters that look similar, and has difficulty understanding the sounds letters make. The child's mother adds that he had delayed speech milestones and has always found rhyming games challenging, unlike his peers. The psychologist observes the child's reading session and notes significant difficulty in decoding simple words, which impacts his reading fluency and comprehension. Given the child's history of delayed speech, difficulty with phonological awareness, and specific challenges with reading and writing, dyslexia is suspected. Which of the following is a common clinical feature of dyslexia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Impaired motor coordination - While some individuals with dyslexia may experience challenges with motor coordination , it is not a defining feature of dyslexia and is more commonly associated with dyspraxia or developmental coordination disorder.
• Option C. Difficulty with math - Known as dyscalculia, difficulty with math is a distinct condition from dyslexia , which primarily affects reading and related language-based processing skills.
• Option D. Rapid language acquisition - This is not associated with dyslexia ; in fact, individuals with dyslexia often experience delayed language acquisition, particularly in the areas of speech and phonological awareness.
• Educational Objective:
• Dyslexia , a specific learning disorder that involves difficulty in reading due to problems identifying speech sounds and learning how they relate to letters and words (decoding). Recognizing the signs of dyslexia, such as difficulty with spelling and word recognition, is crucial for early intervention and support, which can significantly improve reading skills and academic performance.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 1446

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 13-year-old adolescent male is brought to a child psychiatrist by his parents due to concerns about his academic performance and behavior both at home and in school. He has been described as frequently distracted, forgetful in daily activities, and often fails to follow through on tasks and schoolwork. The boy also exhibits fidgetiness, has difficulty remaining seated, and often interrupts others. After a comprehensive evaluation, he has been diagnosed with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Given the high comorbidity rates with ADHD, the psychiatrist decides to evaluate the adolescent for coexisting psychiatric disorders to provide a holistic treatment plan. Which of the following psychiatric disorders is the adolescent most likely to have in conjunction with ADHD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bipolar disorder - While ADHD can coexist with bipolar disorder , it is less common than ODD in individuals with ADHD . Bipolar disorder involves episodes of mood swings ranging from depressive lows to manic highs, which is different from the behavioral issues seen in ODD.
• Option C. Pervasive developmental disorder - This category, primarily associated with autism spectrum disorders , represents a different set of developmental issues than those typically seen in ADHD. The core symptoms involve significant challenges in social interaction and communication.
• Option D. Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) - PTSD can occur in children and adolescents who have experienced or witnessed a traumatic event . While it can coexist with ADHD, it is not as commonly comorbid with ADHD as ODD.
• Educational Objective:
• Common coexisting psychiatric conditions with ADHD, includes :
• Oppositional defiant disorder (35.2%), Conduct disorder (25.7%), Anxiety disorder (25.8%), and Depressive disorder (18.2%).
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 56-59

subject: Paediatrics
question: A healthcare provider is assessing a postpartum mother with a viral infection for breastfeeding suitability. Given the mother's diagnosis, the provider must decide if breastfeeding should be contraindicated. Which viral infection in the mother commonly requires temporary cessation of breastfeeding?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hepatitis B is not a contraindication for breastfeeding as long as the infant receives the appropriate vaccinations.
• Option B: Hepatitis C is generally not considered a contraindication for breastfeeding . Transmission through breast milk is not common, and breastfeeding may continue unless there is nipple bleeding.
• Option D: Varicella Zoster, responsible for chickenpox , is not a contraindication for breastfeeding if the mother has immunity before pregnancy or if the baby has received varicella zoster immune globulin.
• Educational Objective:
• While most maternal viral infections do not prohibit breastfeeding , certain conditions like active herpes simplex lesions on the breasts can necessitate temporary discontinuation of breastfeeding on the affected side to prevent the risk of neonatal transmission.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A Medical Officer in a Regional healthcare center is addressing concerns of a mother who is worried about her milk supply being inadequate for her infant's needs. The MO assures her that breast milk composition typically provides all necessary nutrients in the right amounts. To demonstrate this point, she quizzes the interns present, about the sodium content of breast milk. What is the sodium content in breast milk measured in mEq/L?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Breast milk has a relatively low sodium content compared to other fluids , which is appropriate for the kidney's filtering capacity of an infant . On average, breast milk contains 7 mEq/L of sodium , which meets the infant's nutritional requirements without overloading their renal system.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother with a recent diagnosis of tuberculosis is being counselled. She is concerned about the risk of transmitting the infection to her newborn through breastfeeding. The Pediatrician advises her on when it would be safe to resume breastfeeding after starting antitubercular therapy. For how many weeks should breastfeeding be avoided after initiating antitubercular therapy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Breastfeeding is generally contraindicated in mothers with tuberculosis until they have been on antitubercular therapy for a sufficient period to minimize the risk of transmitting the infection to the infant . The mother should avoid breastfeeding for the initial 2 weeks of treatment , as this is typically the period necessary for the mother to become non-infectious.
• Educational Objective:
• The recommendations for breastfeeding in the context of maternal tuberculosis and antitubercular therapy . Mothers can often resume breastfeeding safely after the infectious period has passed , which is generally after the first 2 weeks of therapy.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 32-year-old working mother in a multinational company brings her 3-month-old infant to your clinic for a routine pediatric visit. After returning to work postpartum, she expresses a desire to transition her baby from breast milk to cow's milk due to her busy schedule. She inquires about the nutritional differences between human milk and cow's milk, specifically asking which component is present in higher quantities in human milk compared to cow's milk. Which of the following is the correct response?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Calcium: Cow's milk actually contains higher levels of calcium than human milk . However, the bioavailability of calcium in human milk is better, meaning infants can absorb it more efficiently.
• Option C. Protein: Cow's milk contains a higher concentration of total protein than human milk . The protein composition in cow's milk is also different, which can be harder for infants to digest and may lead to an increased risk of allergic reactions.
• Option D. Fat: While the total fat content may be similar in both human and cow's milk , the composition of fat in human milk is uniquely suited to the human infant's needs, including essential fatty acids crucial for brain development that are not present in cow's milk in the same ratios.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Page No- 145.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 30-year-old first-time mother has just given birth to a healthy baby boy and is eager to begin breastfeeding. She consults you for advice on starting breastfeeding. Knowing that breastfeeding can typically commence immediately following a normal vaginal delivery, you review her medical history and note she has untreated tuberculosis, which is a known contraindication to breastfeeding. Concerned for the well-being of both mother and child, you plan to discuss alternative feeding options. Based on this scenario, which of the following are also considered contraindications to breastfeeding?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Both A and B
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Galactosemia in the infant is a rare genetic metabolic disorder where the infant cannot properly metabolize galactose , a sugar present in milk . Breastfeeding is contraindicated in infants with galactosemia because it can lead to serious complications , including liver damage , sepsis , and intellectual disability .
• Option B: Active HSV lesions on the breast pose a risk of transmitting the virus to the infant during breastfeeding. If the lesions are present on the breast , direct breastfeeding is contraindicated to prevent neonatal herpes , a potentially severe infection in newborns. However, if the lesions are not on the breast, breastfeeding can continue with precautions, including covering the lesions and practicing meticulous hand hygiene.
• Educational Objective:
• Infant with Galactosemia and Active HSV of the breast are the contraindications of Breastfeeding.
• Absolute contraindications to Breastfeeding are
• HIV in the developed world Active HSV of the breast Mother on radiotherapy Galactosemia Chemotherapy drugs Infant galactosemia
• Active untreated TB Active drug use (Meth, Cocaine, Heroin THC controversial)
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 3793-3794.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 28-year-old woman has just given birth to her first child, a healthy baby boy, via uncomplicated vaginal delivery at a local hospital that is known for its adherence to the Baby-Friendly Hospital Initiative (BFHI). She is eager to start breastfeeding and asks for guidance to ensure the best start for her newborn. As her pediatrician, you discuss the BFHI guidelines with her to support her breastfeeding journey. Which of the following pieces of advice would NOT be consistent with the Baby-Friendly Hospital Initiative guidelines?
options: Mothers and infant to be together for 24 hours a day
Mother to initiate breast feeding within 3 hours of normal delivery
Giving newborn infants no food or drink other then breast milk

explanation.short: Ans. B) Mother to initiate breast feeding within 3 hours of normal delivery
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Mothers and infants to be together 24 hours a day: This guideline is part of the BFHI and is critical for promoting bonding , facilitating breastfeeding on demand, and helping both the mother and infant to recognize each other's cues.
• Option C. Giving newborn infants no food or drink other than breast milk, unless medically indicated : This is a core principle of the BFHI . Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first six months of life to achieve optimal growth, development, and health.
• Option D. Encouraging breastfeeding on demand: This guideline aligns with the BFHI's emphasis on feeding infants whenever they show signs of hunger , which supports effective breastfeeding and ensures the infant's needs are met promptly.
• Educational Objective:
• Have a written breastfeeding policy that is routinely communicated to all health care staff Train all health care staff in skills necessary to implement this policy Inform all pregnant women about the benefits and management of breastfeeding Help mothers initiate breastfeeding with 1 hour of birth Show mothers h ow to breastfeed and how to maintain lactation, even if they should be separated from their infants Give newborn infants no food or drink other than breast milk unless medically indicated Practice “rooming in” by allowing mothers and infants to remain together 24h/d Encourage breastfeeding on demand Give no artificial teats, pacifiers, dummies, or soothers to breastfeeding infants Foster the establishment of breastfeeding support groups and refer mothers to them on discharge from the hospital or clinic
• EBF should be initiated as soon as possible (NVD it is 30 minutes)
explanation.ref: Op Ghai 8 th Edition. Page No- From 97.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to a well-baby clinic in a suburban area near Mumbai for a routine check-up. The mother is a first-time parent and is concerned about her baby's nutrition, as she has been exclusively breastfeeding. She reports that the baby is active, achieving developmental milestones appropriately, and has good urine output. However, she is worried if her breast milk alone is sufficient for the baby's nutritional needs, especially considering the energy and nutrient requirements for optimal growth. The pediatrician decides to educate her about the nutritional content of breast milk. Considering the average composition of breast milk, how much energy (in kcal) and what amounts of protein, fat, and carbohydrates (in grams) would you expect to find in 100 ml of breast milk?

explanation.short: Ans. A) 67 kcal, 1.1g protein, 4g fat, 7.4g carbohydrates
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Breast milk is known for its optimal mix of nutrients , vital for the growth and development of infants . On average, each 100 ml of breast milk provides about 67 kcal of energy . It contains approximately 1.1 grams of protein , which is essential for growth and development . The 4 grams of fat in breast milk are crucial for brain development and provide a significant portion of the energy. The 7.4 grams of carbohydrates , primarily lactose , not only supply energy but also aid in the absorption of calcium.
• Educational Objective:
• Breast milk provides a balanced mix of energy , protein, fat, and carbohydrates , making it the ideal food for infants.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 3789

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A pediatrician demonstrates a feeding technique to a mother who is unable to breastfeed due to a recent surgery. Which feeding method is being demonstrated in the below image?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/30/picture1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Breastfeeding typically involves direct latching to the breast without the aid of devices; thus, it is not the correct answer.
• Option D: Cup feeding is another alternative feeding method where a baby is fed using a small cup , which is not what is depicted in the image.
• Educational Objective:
• Supplementary suckling technique helps to maintain the breastfeeding bond and stimulates milk production while ensuring the baby receives adequate nutrition.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 3789

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a clinical session, a pediatrician discusses the nutritional aspects of various milk options. The discussion turns to the topic of Vitamin K, which is crucial for blood clotting and bone development. The students are presented with three different milk sources for infants and are asked to arrange them in descending order of Vitamin K content. Which of the following represents the correct sequence?
options: More in formula milk > cow milk > breast milk
More in cow's milk > breast milk > formula milk
More in cow's milk > buffalo milk > breast milk
More in cow's milk > formula milk > breast milk

explanation.short: Ans. D) More in cow's milk > formula milk > breast milk
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Cow's milk naturally contains higher levels of Vitamin K compared to human breast milk . While infant formula is fortified with various vitamins , the amount of Vitamin K is usually less than that found in cow's milk but more than that in human breast milk. The answer reflects the typical Vitamin K content hierarchy among the given milk sources.
• Educational Objective:
• Breast milk is deficient vitamin d , vitamin k and vitamin b 12 ( if mother is a pure vegetarian)
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a discussion about the prevention of mother-to-child transmission of HIV, a pediatrician is advising a postpartum patient with HIV on feeding practices for her newborn. The pediatrician emphasizes the importance of one feeding method to reduce the risk of transmission. Which feeding method is contraindicated for infants born to HIV-positive mothers due to an increased risk of transmission?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended when replacement feeding (like formula) is not acceptable , feasible , affordable , sustainable , and safe (AFASS criteria). When these conditions are met, exclusive breastfeeding for the first six months, combined with antiretroviral therapy (ART) for the mother, can be a safe option.
• Option B: Replacement feeding with formula or animal milk may be recommended if it meets the AFASS criteria and if the mother has access to a regular supply of nutritionally adequate and safe formula, as well as the means to prepare it safely.
• Educational Objective:
• The key takeaway is that mixed feeding increases the risk of HIV transmission , while exclusive breastfeeding and replacement feeding have their indications based on specific criteria and availability of safe alternatives.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A new mother asks about the differences in the composition of breast milk throughout a feeding session. She is curious about what the "hind milk," produced towards the end of the feeding, is known to be particularly rich in. What is the primary nutrient that hind milk is rich in?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Hind milk , which is the milk at the end of a breastfeeding session , is rich in fat . This higher fat content contributes to its satiety value , helping the infant feel full and satisfied after feeding , and it is essential for the baby's growth and energy .
• Note - Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, and while they are present in all milk, they are not specifically abundant in hind milk compared to the foremilk .
• Educational Objective:
• Foremilk , the initial milk expressed at the start of feeding , is typically higher in vitamins and protein . In contrast, hind milk is rich in fat and provides the calories needed for the infant to grow and feel satiated . This is crucial for advising breastfeeding mothers about the importance of allowing the baby to feed until they naturally release the breast, ensuring they receive both the foremilk and the hind milk.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a clinic, a pediatrician discusses with new parents the complex composition of breast milk and its benefits beyond basic nutrition. She highlights a specific component that plays a crucial role in the development and healing of the infant's digestive system, especially important for premature babies or those with gastrointestinal issues. Which component in breast milk is specifically known for its role in aiding the repair of the infant's intestinal lining?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Secretory IgA is primarily involved in providing immunity to the infant , not in tissue repair.
• Option B: Lactoferrin has antibacterial properties and supports the infant's immune system rather than directly repairing tissues.
• Option C: Cytokines are involved in immune responses and signalling , rather than the direct repair of the intestinal lining.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of EGF in breast milk is support the structural development and healing of the infant's intestinal tract , thereby enhancing overall gastrointestinal health.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother, anxious about her newborn's nutrition, consults a nurse about breastfeeding effectiveness. The nurse reassures her by explaining the natural changes in breast fullness associated with milk production and removal. She mentions a particular postpartum day by which the mother can expect her breasts to feel noticeably emptier after each feed, indicating a well-established feeding pattern and effective milk removal. What is this significant day postpartum?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A, Option B, Option C : These earlier days are part of the initial milk production phase , where the feeling of breast fullness might still be adjusting and does not necessarily indicate the efficiency of breastfeeding.
• Educational Objective:
• The reduction in breast fullness after feeds by day 6 is a positive sign of effective breastfeeding , providing reassurance to new mothers about their breastfeeding journey.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A working mother concerned about maintaining her breast milk supply for her newborn consults a lactation expert. The expert emphasizes the importance of mimicking the baby's natural feeding frequency to maintain milk production, even when separated. She advises a minimum number of daily expressions to ensure lactation is sustained. What is the recommended minimum frequency of milk expression for a mother to maintain an adequate supply?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, Option B, Option C : These frequencies might be insufficient for some mothers to maintain an optimal milk supply, potentially leading to a decrease in lactation over time.
• Educational Objective:
• The necessity of expressing milk 8-10 times a day for working mothers or those unable to breastfeed directly , ensuring the continuation of lactation and the availability of breast milk for the infant.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician is counseling new parents on the nutritional needs of their newborn. While discussing the comprehensive benefits of breast milk, she points out a limitation regarding its Vitamin D content. She explains that breast milk has a certain range of Vitamin D per liter, which is not sufficient to meet the daily requirements of an infant, necessitating supplementation. What is the average Vitamin D content in breast milk?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Breast milk contains between 12-60 units of Vitamin D per liter , which is below the recommended daily intake for infants. Given this deficiency, supplemental Vitamin D is advised for breastfed infants to ensure they receive adequate amounts for healthy bone development.
• Educational Objective:
• Mother’s milk is deficient in vitamin D – it has 12-60 units of vitamin D .
• A term newborn baby is given around 400 international units & preterm 800-1000 IU of vitamin D till 1 year of life
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine checkup, a neonatologist discusses Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) with the parents of a premature infant. The doctor emphasizes the benefits of KMC for both the infant and the parents but notes that there's an ideal weight at which KMC is generally initiated to ensure the infant's safety and well-being. What is the minimum weight recommended for starting KMC with a hemodynamically stable infant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Kangaroo Mother Care is typically recommended for infants weighing at least 1.8 kg who are also hemodynamically stable , as this weight suggests a level of physiological stability conducive to the practice .
• Educational Objective:
• Kangaroo Mother Care , a method beneficial for the development and bonding of infants , especially preterm ones , is typically initiated when an infant reaches a certain weight threshold , ensuring the practice's safety and effectiveness.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a discussion on infant care practices, a pediatrician highlights the importance of post-feeding positioning to reduce the risk of a particular syndrome. She advises against placing the infant in a prone position immediately after feeding to minimize this risk. What condition is primarily being prevented by this recommendation?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The recommendation to avoid the prone position immediately after feeding is primarily aimed at reducing the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), as this position can compromise an infant's airway and breathing post-feeding .
• Educational Objective:
• The aim is to reinforce safe infant care practices , particularly the avoidance of the prone position immediately after feeding , to lower the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS), thereby promoting safer sleep and care environments for infants.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a breastfeeding support group session, a nurse explains the physiological mechanisms facilitating successful breastfeeding. She elaborates on a crucial reflex that enables milk flow from the glands to the nipple, essential for effective feeding. This reflex is triggered by the infant's suckling and is known by a more common term in lactation discussions. What is the widely recognized name for the draught reflex, crucial for the breastfeeding process?
options: Milk Ejection Reflex / Let Down Reflex

explanation.short: Ans. A) Milk Ejection Reflex / Let Down Reflex
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The draught reflex , integral to successful breastfeeding , is more commonly referred to as the Milk Ejection Reflex or the Let Down Reflex . This reflex is crucial for moving milk from the mammary glands to the ducts and then to the nipple , facilitating breastfeeding.
• Educational Objective:
• Milk Ejection Reflex (also known as the Draught Reflex or Let Down Reflex) is a key physiological process in breastfeeding , enabling the transfer of milk from the mother to the infant and ensuring effective lactation.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine pediatric visit, a doctor assesses a mother and her newborn to ensure effective breastfeeding. She observes the breastfeeding technique, focusing on the baby's latch and attachment, which are pivotal for successful feeding. The doctor looks for specific signs indicating a good latch, contributing to an efficient and comfortable breastfeeding experience for both the mother and the infant. Which of the following represents the correct combination of signs indicative of good attachment? Chin touching the breast Mouth wide open with fullness of cheeks Lower lip everted out Upper areola exposed out

explanation.short: Ans. D) Options 1, 2, 3, and 4 are right
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Good attachment is characterized by the infant's chin touching the breast , a wide-open mouth with full cheeks , the lower lip everted outwards , and more of the upper areola being visible , ensuring efficient milk transfer and a comfortable breastfeeding experience .
• Educational Objective:
• The critical signs of good attachment during breastfeeding , including the infant's chin , mouth , cheeks , lips , and areola positioning , which are essential for effective milk transfer and a positive breastfeeding experience.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A common misconception is addressed regarding which hormone is responsible for milk production versus milk ejection. Is it true or false that prolactin oozes out the milk, and oxytocin produces the milk?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The statement is false . Prolactin is the hormone responsible for stimulating milk production within the mammary glands . In contrast, oxytocin triggers the milk ejection reflex , enabling the milk to be released from the nipple .
• Educational Objective:
• Remember P = P
• PROLACTIN PRODUCES THE MILK
• OXYTOCIN OOZES OUT THE MILK
• Role of Prolactin and Oxytocin in lactation: prolactin for milk production and oxytocin for the milk ejection reflex.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a discussion on the nutritional composition of breast milk, a lactation expert emphasizes the importance of understanding the key nutrients that contribute to infant growth and development. She points out that breast milk contains a predominant carbohydrate that not only serves as a crucial energy source for infants but also aids in the absorption of calcium and other minerals. What is the primary carbohydrate found in breast milk, known for its sweet taste and nutritional benefits?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Lactose is the predominant carbohydrate in breast milk , contributing to its sweet taste . It plays a vital role in providing energy to the infant and facilitating the absorption of essential minerals , such as calcium.
• Option B: Galactocerebrosidase is an enzyme , not a carbohydrate.
• Option C: Minerals are a component of breast milk but not carbohydrates.
• Option D: Combining lactose and galactocerebrosidase does not make sense in this context, as the question asks for the predominant carbohydrate.
• Educational Objective:
• Lactose is the primary carbohydrate in breast milk , providing energy and supporting the absorption of minerals , highlighting the nutritional completeness of breast milk for infant growth and development.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a nutrition-based seminar, a speaker discusses the critical components of breast milk that support the neurological development of infants. One particular fatty acid in breast milk is highlighted for its significant role in the growth and development of the central nervous system and visual acuity. Which fatty acid is recognized for its crucial contribution to brain and eye development in infants?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: TGF Beta is involved in immune regulation, not directly related to CNS or eye development.
• Option B: Para Amino Benzoic Acid is not a fatty acid linked to CNS or eye development in the context of breast milk.
• Option D: None of the Above is incorrect as DHA is indeed present in breast milk and crucial for CNS and eye development.
• Educational Objective:
• Docosahexaenoic Acid (DHA) is a key component in breast milk essential for the development of the infant's brain and eyes, underscoring the importance of breast milk in supporting early neurological and visual development.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a pediatric care unit, a nurse educates a concerned mother whose newborn is experiencing jaundice. She explains that a certain component in breast milk can contribute to this condition, known as breast milk jaundice, which typically appears in the first week of life and can persist longer than typical newborn jaundice. This component affects bilirubin metabolism and promotes enterohepatic circulation of bilirubin. What is this component associated with breast milk jaundice?
options: 3 Alpha 20 Beta Diol & Beta Glucuronidase enzyme

explanation.short: Ans. A) 3 Alpha 20 Beta Diol & Beta Glucuronidase enzyme
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Galactokinase is involved in carbohydrate metabolism , not directly related to bilirubin metabolism and jaundice.
• Option C: This option is incorrect as there are known components in breast milk that can contribute to jaundice.
• Option D: 20 Beta Prednione is not a recognized component in breast milk affecting bilirubin metabolism.
• Educational Objective:
• Certain components in breast milk , specifically 3 Alpha 20 Beta Diol and Beta Glucuronidase , can contribute to the development of breast milk jaundice by affecting bilirubin metabolism and promoting its enterohepatic circulation , leading to a prolonged jaundice period in affected infants .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1799

subject: Paediatrics
question:
For each pathogen listed in Column A, select the protective component from Column B that breast milk provides to help guard the infant against that particular pathogen. Match the pathogen with the correct breast milk component:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/22/picture22.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Bifidus Factor is known to help protect infants from intestinal pathogens such as Shigella . Lysozyme has antibacterial properties effective against E. histolytica . Lactoferrin , with its iron-binding capability , inhibits the growth of G. lamblia. Para Amino Benzoic Acid (PABA) is believed to provide protection against malaria.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A dietitian calculates the daily caloric needs of infants based on their weight using the Holiday-Segar Formula. A mother presents with her 7 kg baby, and the dietitian sets out to determine the infant's daily kilocalorie requirement. Using the standard caloric requirements per kilogram of body weight for infants, how many kilocalories per day does this 7 kg baby require?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• According to the Holiday-Segar Formula , infants weighing 1-10 kg require 100 kcal/kg/day . Therefore, a 7 kg baby would require 700 kcal/day (7 kg x 100 kcal/kg/day).
• Educational Objective:
• Holiday-Segar Formula – The kilocalories requirement is
• 1-10 kg babies –100 kcal per kg
• 10-20 kg - 50 kcal / kg
• >20 kg - 20 kcal/ kg
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1780

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician is reviewing metabolic disorders and their clinical presentations with medical residents. The topic of galactosemia and its associated symptoms arises. Based on the latest updates in medical knowledge, the pediatrician poses a question about the correlation between galactosemia and macrocephaly. Does galactosemia cause macrocephaly?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Galactosemia is a metabolic disorder that can lead to macrocephaly , among other symptoms. This is due to the accumulation of galactose in the blood , which can affect multiple organ systems , including the brain.
• Educational Objective:
• The absolute contraindication of breast feeding is galactosemia and it can cause macrocephaly in the baby
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a Neonatal intensive care unit, a neonatologist assesses a newborn delivered at 27 weeks of gestation to determine the appropriate method of nutritional support. Given the infant's premature status, the neonatologist must choose the safest and most effective route of feeding. Which method of nutrition is most appropriate for a newborn at this stage of gestational development?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Breastfeeding is typically not possible for infants born at 27 weeks due to underdeveloped suck-swallow coordination and the high risk of aspiration.
• Option B & C: Paladai Feed and Katori Spoon Feed are methods used for slightly older preterm infants who have more mature gastrointestinal systems.
• Educational Objective:
• < 28 weeks: Intravenous fluids
• 28-32 weeks: nasogastric / orogastric feeding
• 32-34 weeks: paladai feed
• >34 weeks: breast feeding
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a session on infant feeding in areas with high rates of HIV, a public health specialist outlines the conditions that must be met for replacement feeding to be considered a suitable alternative to breastfeeding. These criteria are designed to ensure the safety and health of the infant where breastfeeding is contraindicated. What does AFASS stand for in the context of replacement feeding?
options: Partially Acceptable, Affordable, Feasible, Sustainable & Safe

explanation.short: Ans. D) Acceptable, Affordable, Feasible, Sustainable & Safe
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Replacement feeding options should be Acceptable to the caregiver and family , Affordable within the family's budget , Feasible to implement consistently and correctly, Sustainable over time , and Safe from contaminants and pathogens .
• Educational Objective:
• To comprehend the AFASS criteria for replacement feeding , which ensure that non-breastfeeding options are safe and practical for infants who cannot be breastfed , particularly in contexts where HIV transmission risk must be minimized.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1796

subject: Paediatrics
question: A breastfeeding mother inquiries about nighttime feeds and their impact on milk production. The lactation consultant explains that a certain hormone, which plays a pivotal role in milk synthesis, is secreted in higher amounts during the night. Which hormone increases in secretion during the night, making nighttime breastfeeding beneficial for milk supply?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Prolactin , known as the milk-producing hormone , has higher levels of secretion during the night . This is why nighttime breastfeeding is often recommended to help maintain a robust milk supply .
• Educational Objective:
• The significance of prolactin in lactation , especially its increased nocturnal secretion , which supports the rationale for encouraging nighttime breastfeeding to enhance and maintain milk production .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1799

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician is discussing weaning with a group of parents. She introduces the concept of 'token breastfeeding’. Which of the following best describes token breastfeeding?
options: If an infant mostly has breastfed and less outside food, it is called as token breastfeeding.
If an infant mostly has other food with no breastfeeds, it is called as token breastfeeding.
If an infant mostly has other food, but still breastfeeds occasionally, it is called as token breastfeeding.

explanation.short: Ans. C) If an infant mostly has other food, but still breastfeeds occasionally, it is called as token breastfeeding.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Token breastfeeding occurs when an infant primarily consumes foods other than breast milk but still engages in occasional breastfeeding sessions.
• Educational Objective:
• Token breastfeeding is the practice where infants primarily eat complementary foods but continue to breastfeed on a non-nutritive basis , typically as a means of comfort or routine .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1795

subject: Paediatrics
question: An intern is reviewing contraindications for breastfeeding. He must identify scenarios where breastfeeding would not be advisable for the infant's safety. Which of the following conditions is considered a contraindication to breastfeeding?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Mothers undergoing chemotherapy or radiotherapy may excrete toxic substances in breast milk that could harm the infant.
• Option B: Infants with galactosemia cannot metabolize the galactose in breast milk , leading to serious complications.
• Option C: While true lactose intolerance in infants is rare, if present, it would necessitate an alternative to lactose-containing breast milk.
• Educational Objective:
• The contraindications to breastfeeding , includes maternal treatments such as chemotherapy or radiotherapy and certain metabolic conditions in the infant , such as galactosemia .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 1797

subject: Paediatrics
question: All the following drugs are used for treatment of congestive heart failure except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Nesiritide: Nesiritide is a recombinant B-type natriuretic peptide that has vasodilatory effects and is used in the management of acute decompensated heart failure.
• Option B. Sacubitril: When combined with valsartan (a neprilysin inhibitor and an angiotensin II receptor blocker, respectively), sacubitril is used to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death and hospitalization for heart failure in patients with chronic heart failure.
• Option D. Metoprolol: Metoprolol is a beta-blocker that is used in the management of CHF. It reduces myocardial oxygen demand and can improve survival in patients with heart failure.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Paediatrics
question: Angiotensin receptor blocker which blocks thromboxane A2 receptor is?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Telmisartan: Incorrect because Telmisartan is known as a partial agonist of peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPAR-γ), which differentiates it from Losartan and does not confer the ability to block the thromboxane A2 receptor.
• Option C. Candesartan: Incorrect as Candesartan is an inactive ester prodrug that does not possess the ability to block the thromboxane A2 receptor. It is hydrolyzed to its active form within the body, which acts selectively on the angiotensin receptor.
• Option D. Olmesartan: Incorrect for this question since Olmesartan, while also being an inactive ester prodrug hydrolyzed to its active form , does not block the thromboxane A2 receptor. It is cleared by renal elimination and biliary excretion and does not require dose adjustment in patients with mild-to-moderate renal or hepatic impairment.
• Educational objective:
• Losartan is unique among angiotensin receptor blockers in that it also competitively antagonizes the thromboxane A2 receptor, contributing to its antiplatelet effects.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A patient is taking atorvastatin for dyslipidemia. Which of these drugs should be avoided in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Oxycodone: Incorrect because oxycodone, an opioid analgesic, does not significantly interact with the CYP3A4 enzyme in a way that would affect the metabolism of atorvastatin to a clinically relevant extent.
• Option C. Rivaroxaban: Incorrect as rivaroxaban, an anticoagulant , is not metabolized by CYP3A4 in a way that would interfere with atorvastatin metabolism.
• Option D. Adalimumab: Incorrect because adalimumab , an immunosuppressive drug used to treat autoimmune diseases, does not interact with the CYP3A4 enzyme and thus would not affect atorvastatin levels.
• Educational objective:
• When prescribing statins, avoid concomitant use with drugs that inhibit CYP3A4, such as clarithromycin, to prevent an increase in their plasma levels which could lead to myopathy. Other notable CYP3A4 inhibitors to avoid include amiodarone, gemfibrozil, HIV protease inhibitors (such as saquinavir, ritonavir), and itraconazole.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following statement is True regarding Sacubitril?
options: It is a drug that can act by inhibiting angiotensin converting enzyme
It is a drug used in combination with valsartan for congestive heart failure
Hypokalemia is an adverse effect of sacubitril
Sacubitril is combined with ramipril for treatment of hypertension

explanation.short: Ans. B) It is a drug used in combination with valsartan for congestive heart failure
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Incorrect because sacubitril is not an angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor , which typically have names ending in -pril.
• Option C. Incorrect as hypokalemia is not a side effect associated with sacubitril. It is more commonly associated with traditional diuretics rather than neprilysin inhibitors.
• Option D. Incorrect because sacubitril is combined with valsartan, not ramipril , and the combination is used for heart failure, not specifically for hypertension.
• Educational objective:
• Sacubitril is a neprilysin inhibitor used in combination with valsartan as an ARNI for the treatment of heart failure to reduce the risk of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization , particularly in patients with reduced ejection fraction.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following drugs is an oral renin inhibitor?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Candesartan: Candesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) . It works by blocking the action of angiotensin II at its receptor, preventing angiotensin II from causing vasoconstriction and aldosterone release, which in turn lowers blood pressure. It does not inhibit renin directly.
• Option C. Quinapril: Quinapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor . It inhibits the enzyme responsible for converting angiotensin I to angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor. By reducing the levels of angiotensin II, it lowers blood pressure. Quinapril acts later in the RAAS pathway compared to aliskiren and does not directly inhibit renin.
• Option D. Benazepril: Benazepril is also an ACE inhibitor, similar to quinapril. It lowers blood pressure by inhibiting the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated volume expansion . Like quinapril, benazepril does not act by inhibiting renin.
• Educational Objective:
• The objective is to understand the mechanisms of action of various drugs used in the management of hypertension, focusing on their roles within the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Recognizing the unique action of aliskiren as a renin inhibitor, in contrast to ARBs and ACE inhibitors that act downstream, is essential for effective hypertension management.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following is the drug of choice for digoxin induced ventricular tachyarrhythmias?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Quinidine: Quinidine is an antiarrhythmic that can increase serum digoxin levels and is contraindicated in digoxin-induced arrhythmias because it may exacerbate toxicity.
• Option B. Amiodarone: While amiodarone is a versatile antiarrhythmic agent used in various types of arrhythmias , it can interact with digoxin and increase its levels , potentially worsening toxicity.
• Option D. Phenytoin: Phenytoin is effective for ventricular arrhythmias, especially in the context of acute myocardial infarction, but it is not the first choice for digoxin-induced arrhythmias. It can be used if lignocaine is not available or contraindicated.
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to understand the appropriate management of digoxin-induced ventricular tachyarrhythmias . Recognizing lignocaine as the drug of choice in this scenario is critical for effectively addressing the cardiac toxicity associated with digoxin overdose , minimizing the risk of further cardiac complications.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Ivabradine is a drug that has been used in clinical practice for few years now. It is indicated for the management of which of the following diseases?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Prerenal azotemia: Incorrect as prerenal azotemia is related to kidney function and ivabradine does not have a role in its treatment.
• Option C. Alzheimer’s disease: Incorrect because Alzheimer’s disease is a neurodegenerative condition and ivabradine is not indicated for its treatment.
• Option D. Smoking cessation: Incorrect as ivabradine is not used in smoking cessation ; it is a cardiac medication used for heart rate control.
• Educational objective:
• Ivabradine is indicated for the management of stable angina and congestive heart failure . It works by inhibiting the funny current in the SA node , leading to a reduction in heart rate, which is a key therapeutic target in managing these conditions.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following drug is used in CHF for relief of congestive symptoms and restoration of cardiac performance but does not possess inotropic action?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Digoxin: Digoxin has a positive inotropic effect , increasing the force of cardiac contractions , and is used in CHF to improve symptoms and cardiac performance. It does not fit the criteria as it possesses inotropic action.
• Option B. Dobutamine: Dobutamine is a beta-1 adrenergic agonist with positive inotropic effects , used primarily in acute heart failure to increase cardiac output. It directly increases the force of heart contractions.
• Option C. Amrinone: Amrinone is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor with positive inotropic and vasodilatory effects , used to improve cardiac output in heart failure patients. Like dobutamine and digoxin, it increases the force of cardiac contractions.
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to understand the role of different classes of drugs in the management of CHF, particularly distinguishing those with and without inotropic action. Metoprolol's use in CHF illustrates the importance of managing heart rate and reducing myocardial oxygen demand as part of a strategy to improve cardiac performance without directly increasing the force of contraction

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following drug can be used for treatment of euvolemic hyponatremia in patient with advanced congestive heart failure?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Nesiritide: Incorrect as nesiritide is a recombinant B-type natriuretic peptide used as a b decompensated heart failure, not specifically for euvolemic hyponatremia.
• Option C. Hydrocortisone: Incorrect because hydrocortisone is a corticosteroid and does not address the underlying mechanisms of euvolemic hyponatremia in CHF.
• Option D. Metoprolol: Incorrect as metoprolol is a beta-blocker used in the management of CHF and hypertension , but it is not used to directly treat euvolemic hyponatremia.
• Educational objective:
• In patients with advanced congestive heart failure presenting with euvolemic hyponatremia, tolvaptan is used to correct water retention and hyponatremia associated with SIADH by antagonizing the vasopressin V2 receptors and increasing water excretion.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Propranolol can be used in all of the following conditions except.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Thyrotoxicosis: Propranolol is used in thyrotoxicosis to provide symptomatic relief from hyperthyroid symptoms such as tachycardia, anxiety, and tremors . It can also inhibit peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.
• Option C. Migraine: Propranolol is used as a prophylactic treatment for migraine . It helps reduce the frequency and severity of migraine attacks.
• Option D. Hypertension: Propranolol can be used in the management of hypertension . It lowers blood pressure by reducing cardiac output and inhibiting renin release from the kidneys.
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to understand the therapeutic uses and contraindications of propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocke r. Recognizing that propranolol is contraindicated in variant angina due to its potential to worsen coronary artery spasms highlights the importance of considering the specific clinical context when selecting beta-blockers for treatment.

subject: Paediatrics
question: All the following are adverse effects of amiodarone except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pulmonary fibrosis: This is a serious and well-recognized side effect of amiodarone, especially with long-term use.
• Option B. Corneal microdeposits: Common and typically reversible upon discontinuation of the drug.
• Option C. Photosensitivity: This adverse effect can occur in patients taking amiodarone , leading to increased risk of sunburn and skin pigmentation.
• Educational objective:
• Amiodarone is a class III anti-arrhythmic drug associated with several adverse effects including pulmonary fibrosis, corneal microdeposits, and photosensitivity, but not tachycardia; in fact, it more commonly causes bradycardia.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following is not true about the mechanism of action of digitalis?
options: It binds to the intracellular face of Na + K + ATPase enzyme
There is rise in intracellular Na +
Inotropic action of digoxin is independent of cardiac innervation

explanation.short: Ans. A) It binds to the intracellular face of Na+ K+ ATPase enzyme.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. There is a rise in intracellular Na+: This statement is true; the inhibition of Na+/K+ ATPase by digitalis leads to increased intracellular sodium.
• Option C. It has positive inotropic action: This is true; digitalis increases the force of cardiac muscle contraction.
• Option D. Inotropic action of digoxin is independent of cardiac innervation: This is also true; digitalis exerts its effects directly on the cardiac muscle cells, not through the nervous system. Though, it can decrease the AV conduction via vagus like action but this action is responsible for its use in atrial fibrillation , not as an inotropic drug.
• Educational objective:
• Digitalis binds to the extracellular face of the Na+/K+ ATPase enzyme , leading to increased intracellular calcium, and in turn, positive inotropic action, which enhances cardiac contractility.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Fenoldopam is used in the management of?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Congestive heart failure: Fenoldopam is not typically used in the management of congestive heart failure (CHF). Drugs commonly used in CHF include ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, and aldosterone antagonists , which work by different mechanisms to reduce the workload on the heart and improve cardiac output .
• Option C. Migraine prophylaxis: Fenoldopam is not used for migraine prophylaxis . Medications for migraine prevention include beta-blockers (such as propranolol), calcium channel blockers, antidepressants, and antiepileptic drugs , which have different mechanisms of action aimed at reducing the frequency and severity of migraine attacks .
• Option D. Tachyarrhythmias: Fenoldopam is not indicated for the treatment of tachyarrhythmias. Drugs used to manage tachyarrhythmias include beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, antiarrhythmic agents, and sometimes digoxin , depending on the type and cause of the arrhythmia. These medications work by modulating cardiac electrical activity to normalize heart rhythm .
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to understand the specific indications for fenoldopam, particularly its role in managing hypertensive emergencies due to its vasodilatory effects and positive impact on renal perfusion . Additionally, recognizing that fenoldopam is not indicated for congestive heart failure, migraine prophylaxis, or tachyarrhythmias underscores the importance of selecting appropriate medications based on the clinical condition being treated.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A new antiarrhythmic drug is found to be effective against both atrial and ventricular arrhythmias. Its effect on action potential is shown in the Figure. The effect of this new drug is most like:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/08/25/image-2-min.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lignocaine: Lignocaine (Lidocaine) is a Class IB antiarrhythmic drug that primarily works by shortening the action potential duration by blocking sodium channels , particularly in ischemic or depolarized cells . It does not prolong the action potential plateau.
• Option B. Propranolol: Propranolol is a Class II antiarrhythmic , which is a beta-blocker that reduces sympathetic nervous system activity. It does not have a direct effect on the action potential duration or plateau phase.
• Option C. Encainide: Encainide , which is no longer widely used, was a Class IC antiarrhythmic drug that significantly slows conduction by blocking sodium channels but does not typically prolong the action potential duration.
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to recognize the electrophysiologic effects of antiarrhythmic drugs on the cardiac action potential. Understanding how these drugs affect the action potential can provide insights into their therapeutic uses and potential arrhythmogenic risks. Quinidine's effect of prolonging the action potential duration is useful in the treatment of both atrial and ventricular arrhythmias, but it also carries the risk of proarrhythmia, underlining the need for careful patient selection and monitoring.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 45-year-old man presents with NYHA Class III breathlessness with a serum creatinine of 2.5 mg/dL and potassium level of 5.5 mEq/L. Which of the following drug is contraindicated?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Carvedilol: Incorrect as beta blockers like carvedilol are indicated in CHF and can reduce mortality. They should be started at a very low dose and gradually titrated to the target dose.
• Option C. Nitroglycerine: Incorrect because nitroglycerine is a vasodilator that can help reduce the workload of the heart and provide symptomatic relief in CHF.
• Option D. Digoxin: Incorrect as digoxin is an oral inotropic agent that can be used in chronic CHF to improve symptoms and possibly exercise capacity. Although it is excreted by kidneys but it can be used in renal failure after dose adjustment.
• Educational objective:
• In patients with CHF and compromised renal function or elevated serum potassium levels , potassium-sparing diuretics like spironolactone should b e avoided due to the increased risk of severe hyperkalemia.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Trimetazidine is a drug approved for the treatment of angina pectoris. Its mechanism of action is assumed to be:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Incorrect because ivabradine is the bradycardiac agent used for angina , which acts by blocking the funny current in the SA node , not trimetazidine.
• Option B. Incorrect as fasudil is the rho kinase inhibitor utilized for angina pectoris , not trimetazidine.
• Option C. Incorrect because nicorandil is the K channel opener (which also has nitric oxide releasing properties ) that is used for angina pectoris, rather than trimetazidine.
• Educational objective:
• Trimetazidine is an antianginal medication that reduces the frequency of angina attacks and increases exercise capacity through a mechanism that likely involves the inhibition of mitochondrial LC3-KAT, leading to a shift from fatty acid to glucose metabolism in the myocardium and resulting in a reduced oxygen demand.

subject: Paediatrics
question: All the following are seen in digitalis toxicity except:
options: Paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with fast ventricular rate

explanation.short: Ans. B) Paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with fast ventricular rate
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ventricular bigeminy: Seen with digitalis toxicity as it causes increased ventricular ectopy.
• Option C. Regularization of atrial fibrillation: Suggestive of digitalis toxicity where there is an AV block with a regular ventricular rate.
• Option D. Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia: Associated with digitalis toxicity and can manifest in the context of severe structural heart disease.
• Educational objective:
• In digitalis toxicity, you would expect to see arrhythmias such as ventricular bigeminy, regularization of AF due to AV block, and bidirectional ventricular tachycardia, but not paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with a fast ventricular rate

subject: Paediatrics
question: All are true about starting beta-blocker therapy in a patient with congestive heart failure except:
options: They should be started with optimum doses
They should gradually increase over the weeks
Special precautions should be taken in cases of NYHA class III and IV
Carvedilol and metoprolol are the preferred drugs

explanation.short: Ans. A) They should be started with optimum doses.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Gradually increasing the dose over weeks is correct , as this allows for the patient’s tolerance to the drug to build and minimizes the risk of adverse effects.
• Option C. Special precautions with NYHA class III and IV patients are necessary due to their increased risk of fluid overload and worsening heart failure.
• Option D. Carvedilol and metoprolol are indeed the preferred beta-blockers for treating congestive heart failure due to their proven benefits in this patient population.
• Educational objective:
• When initiating beta-blocker therapy in congestive heart failure , it is essential to start with very low doses and titrate gradually to minimize risks , especially in patients with more advanced disease (NYHA class III and IV).

subject: Paediatrics
question: All the following statements about nesiritide are true except?
options: It can be used in decompensated CHF
It causes loss of Na + in the urine

explanation.short: Ans. C) It can be administered orally
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It is a BNP analogue: This statement is true. Nesiritide is a synthetic version of BNP that is used in the management of acute decompensated heart failure.
• Option B. It can be used in decompensated CHF: This statement is true . Nesiritide is indicated for the treatment of acute decompensated congestive heart failure (CHF), where it works by causing vasodilation and natriuresis.
• Option D. It causes loss of Na+ in the urine: This statement is true . Nesiritide promotes natriuresis, which is the excretion of sodium in the urine , contributing to its beneficial effects in heart failure.
• Educational Objective:
• The educational goal is to understand the administration and effects of nesiritide in the treatment of acute decompensated heart failure . Nesiritide must be administered intravenously due to its peptide nature and the gastrointestinal degradation of oral peptides

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following statements about diazoxide is false?
options: It acts by causing prolonged opening of ATP dependent K + channels in beta cells
It can be used to treat patients with insulinoma
It is used as an antihypertensive agent
