• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1812-1814.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1812-1814.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A worried mother brings her 3-year-old son, who has no significant medical history, to the pediatric clinic after discovering bright red blood in his diaper. The child seems relatively comfortable, except for when his right lower quadrant (RLQ) is palpated, causing him to cry. An ultrasound reveals a tubular blind-ending structure originating from an ileal loop, and a technetium-99m scan confirms the presence of Meckel's diverticulum. Considering the diagnosis of Meckel's diverticulum, which of the following statements is NOT true about its features?
options: It is due to persistence of omphalomesenteric duct.
May have ectopic acid-secreting gastric mucosa.
Least common congenital anomaly of gastrointestinal tract.

explanation.short: Ans. D) Least common congenital anomaly of gastrointestinal tract
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. True Diverticulum: Correct . Meckel's diverticulum involves all layers of the bowel wall.
• Option B. Persistence of Omphalomesenteric Duct: Correct , as Meckel's diverticulum results from the failure of the omphalomesenteric duct to completely obliterate during development.
• Option C. Ectopic Acid-Secreting Gastric Mucosa: Correct . The presence of ectopic gastric mucosa can lead to acid secretion and subsequent ulceration, causing bleeding, which might explain the rectal bleeding in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Most common congenital anomaly of gastrointestinal tract - Meckel’s diverticulum
• Affects 2% of population 2 feet from ileocecal valve 2 inches long 2 types of epithelia tissue: gastric and pancreatic Male: female ratio 2:1 Patients < 2 years old 2% symptomatic
• Nelson 21 st Edition Chapter 357 – Page No- 7762
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition Chapter 357 – Page No- 7762

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned father rushes his 13-year-old son to the emergency department from school due to 2 days of persistent nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The boy is in evident distress, experiencing severe pain upon palpation of the abdomen, particularly in the right lower quadrant (RLQ). He describes the pain as initially starting around the periumbilical area before localizing to the RLQ. The boy is tachycardic, febrile, and presents with mild hypertension. Physical examination reveals diffuse abdominal tenderness with notable rebound tenderness in the RLQ, leading to a clinical diagnosis of appendicitis. Among the following statements about appendicitis, which one is correct?
options: The most common site of appendix is Retro iliac
Obturator sign has a significance in appendicitis.
Contrast Enhanced CT is the initial investigation in children
Psoas sign has no significance in appendicitis.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Obturator sign has a significance in appendicitis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Retroiliac: The most common location of the appendix is not retroiliac but rather in the RLQ of the abdomen, specifically at the McBurney's point.
• Option C. Contrast-Enhanced CT: In children , ultrasound is often the initial imaging modality of choice for suspected appendicitis to avoid radiation exposure . CT may be used subsequently if ultrasound findings are inconclusive and the clinical suspicion remains high.
• Option D. Psoas Sign: The psoas sign is actually significant in appendicitis , especially when the inflamed appendix is positioned against the iliopsoas muscle . The sign is elicited by extending the hip, with the patient lying on their left side, which can cause pain in cases of appendicitis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical signs significant in diagnosing appendicitis , are the obturator sign and psoas sign , and to recognize the appropriate initial imaging modality in children suspected of having appendicitis, is an ultrasound to avoid unnecessary radiation exposure.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter- 370, – Page No- 8106.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During pediatric rounds, you're reviewing a case involving hyperbilirubinemia. The attending consultant quizzes the team, asking, "In pediatric patients, what is the foremost reason for undergoing liver transplantation?" How should you respond?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Weil’s disease: This condition, caused by Leptospira bacteria , can lead to severe liver damage but is not the leading cause of liver transplantation in children.
• Option B. Fulminant hepatic failure: While this acute liver failure could necessitate urgent liver transplantation , it is not as common as biliary atresia in pediatric patients requiring this intervention.
• Option D. Wilson's disease: A genetic disorder that causes copper accumulation in organs , including the liver . Although it can lead to liver failure, biliary atresia is more frequently the cause of liver transplantation in the pediatric population.
• Educational Objective:
• Biliary atresia is the most prevalent indication for liver transplantation in the pediatric demographic , underscoring the significance of early detection and intervention to avert the progression to irreversible liver damage.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-383. Page No 8277.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-383. Page No 8277.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
An anxious mother consults about her 2-year-old child, who exhibits delayed developmental milestones, signs of intellectual disability, and sandal gap toes. The child, recently diagnosed with a heart condition, is also identified to have a trisomy disorder. Accompanying this information is an image depicting the child's eye findings characteristic of the trisomy disorder. Given this clinical background, what is the most common cause of intestinal obstruction in this child?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture14_E9y44hu.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Meckel's Diverticulum : While Meckel's diverticulum is a common congenital anomaly , it is not particularly associated with Down syndrome more than duodenal atresia.
• Option C . Hirschsprung Disease : This condition involves the absence of ganglion cells in the bowel leading to functional obstruction . It is not specifically associated with Down syndrome.
• Option D . Jejunal Atresia : Though a possible cause of intestinal obstruction in neonates , jejunal atresia does not have a specific association with Down syndrome like duodenal atresia does.
• Educational Objective:
• The most common cause of intestinal obstruction in the Down Syndrome child is duodenal atresia .
• Duodenal atresia is notably associated with Down syndrome , with up to 30% of infants with duodenal atresia having Down syndrome.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 98- Page No 3011.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 98- Page No 3011.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine pediatric visit, an 8-month-old girl named Saheli is brought in by her mother, who is concerned about her daughter's chronic diarrhea lasting over a month and failure to thrive. Saheli presents with abdominal distension and facial pallor, and a routine complete blood count (CBC) reveals severe anemia. The mother reports that these symptoms began after weaning Saheli off breast milk and introducing supplementary feeds. Further evaluation indicates Saheli has a hypersensitivity to gliadin in her diet. Given this clinical picture, which specific antibody is most likely to be present in Saheli?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibody (ASCA): This antibody is more commonly associated with Crohn's disease , an inflammatory bowel disease , rather than with celiac disease.
• Option C. Antinuclear antibody (ANA): ANA is a marker of autoimmune diseases in general and is not specific to celiac disease.
• Option D. Antineutrophil Cytoplasmic Antibodies (ANCA): ANCA is associated with vasculitis and some inflammatory bowel diseases like ulcerative colitis, not celiac disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Anti-endomysial antibodies are highly specific for celiac disease , particularly in the context of symptoms like chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive , and onset after the introduction of gluten-containing foods in infants.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 387- Page No 8386.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 14-year-old boy presents with right-sided abdominal discomfort. His father, who has a history of a similar condition in his teenage years and is currently being treated for a gastrointestinal malignancy, accompanies him. The pediatrician's examination and ultrasound (USG) do not reveal any significant abnormalities. Given the family history, the boy undergoes a colonoscopy, and the findings from this procedure are provided. Based on the diagnosis made from the colonoscopy results, which associated syndromes could be related to this patient's condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture15_8BTnCU7.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Attenuated FAP: A variant of FAP that presents with fewer polyps (often less than 100) and a later onset of cancer compared to classic FAP.
• Option B. Turcot Syndrome: Characterized by the presence of colorectal polyps and a predisposition to brain tumors.
• Option C. Gardner Syndrome: A variant of FAP that, in addition to colorectal polyps, includes extracolonic manifestations such as osteomas , dental anomalies , and soft tissue tumors .
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize the association between familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) syndromes and the development of gastrointestinal malignancies , highlighting the importance of early screening and surveillance in individuals with a family history suggestive of hereditary cancer syndromes .
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 372 Page No-8164 -8166.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 372 Page No-8164 -8166.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
An anxious mother presents her 10-year-old child to the clinic, concerned about a lesion in his mouth, shown in an accompanying image. The child's uncle was recently diagnosed with a gastrointestinal malignancy, heightening her concern about her son's risk for developing cancer. Upon examination, you note the oral lesion and identify a hamartomatous polyp. Given these clinical findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and which gene is implicated?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture16_eYi22rC.jpg]
options: Familial adenomatous polyposis and APC gene.
Familial adenomatous polyposis and STK 1 gene.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Peutz-Jeghers syndrome and STK 11 gene.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) and APC gene: FAP is characterized by the development of hundreds to thousands of adenomatous polyps in the colon and rectum , with an increased risk of colorectal cancer. It is associated with mutations in the APC gene, not with mucocutaneous pigmentation or hamartomatous polyps.
• Option B. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome and STK 1 gene: This option is incorrect because Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is associated with the STK11 gene , not STK1.
• Option D. Familial Adenomatous Polyposis and STK 1 gene: This option is incorrect on both counts; FAP is associated with the APC gene , and STK1 is not the gene involved in FAP or PJS.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome are mucocutaneous pigmentation and hamartomatous polyps , and it is associated with mutations in the STK11 gene . Early diagnosis is crucial for managing the increased cancer risk associated with the syndrome.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 372 Page No-8163-8164.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 372 Page No-8163-8164.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A child exhibiting HLA-DQ8 positivity presents with chronic diarrhea, fatigue, pallor, and failure to thrive. To confirm the diagnosis, a biopsy is conducted, revealing crypt hyperplasia and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, leading to a diagnosis of celiac disease. Considering what is known about celiac disease, which of the following statements is NOT true?
options: Celiac disease is most commonly confused with IBS
10% of adults with iron deficiency anemia have undiagnosed celiac disease
On histology Increase in thickness of the mucosa
Celiac disease not responding to gluten free diet has poor prognosis

explanation.short: Ans. C) On histology Increase in thickness of the mucosa
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Misdiagnosis as IBS: It is true that celiac disease can be confused with IBS due to overlapping gastrointestinal symptoms, making this statement correct.
• Option B . Iron deficiency anemia: It is also true that a significant proportion of adults with iron deficiency anemia may have undiagnosed celiac disease, as celiac disease can lead to malabsorption of nutrients, including iron.
• Option D . Non-responsive celiac disease: Celiac disease that does not respond to a strict gluten-free diet, known as refractory celiac disease, does indeed have a poorer prognosis and may require further medical intervention.
• Educational Objective:
• The histological hallmark of celiac disease involves villous atrophy and not an increase in mucosal thickness.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 387- Page No 8386.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 387- Page No 8386.

question:

question:
Match the following most common findings in children and their associated conditions.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old child is brought in by their mother due to acute onset watery diarrhea, having experienced six episodes in the past 24 hours. The child presents without fever, abdominal pain, or vomiting and appears alert and well-hydrated. Physical examination reveals a soft, non-tender abdomen. Considering the clinical presentation, what is the most common infectious cause of diarrhea in children?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rhinovirus: Primarily associated with the common cold , rhinovirus mainly affects the respiratory system , causing symptoms like runny nose, sore throat, and cough, rather than gastrointestinal symptoms like diarrhea.
• Option C. Norovirus: While norovirus is a common cause of gastroenteritis and can lead to outbreaks , especially in semi-closed environments such as schools and cruise ships , it is not as commonly associated with acute gastroenteritis in children as rotavirus. Norovirus tends to affect people of all ages and is characterized by sudden onset of vomiting and watery diarrhea.
• Option D. Zika virus: Known for its association with outbreaks in tropical regions and its link to birth defects when infections occur during pregnancy , Zika virus is primarily transmitted by mosquitoes and does not commonly cause diarrhea as a primary symptom.
• Educational Objective:
• Rotavirus is the most common cause of acute gastroenteritis in children , characterized by watery diarrhea. Rhino virus is the most common cause of upper respiratory tract infection in children Noro virus is the most common cause of diarrhoea in adults Zika virus will results in microcephaly.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 292 Page No- 7022.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric department due to experiencing five episodes of loose stools. Upon examination, the child shows a keen interest in drinking water and has normal skin turgor. Following WHO guidelines, you initiate Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) therapy and counsel the mother accordingly. What is the recommended dosage of zinc supplementation for this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• For infants and children with acute diarrhea , the WHO recommends zinc supplementation as part of the management . The recommended dosage for infants under 6 months of age is 10 mg of zinc per day for 10-14 days . Zinc supplementation during diarrhea episodes has been shown to reduce the duration and severity of the episode and may also have a prophylactic effect against future episodes for 2-3 months.
• Educational Objective:
• The dosage of Zinc given in the child is
• <6 months of age – 10 mg/day .
• >6 months of age – 20 mg/day .
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother brings her 5-month-old infant to the clinic due to three episodes of vomiting and diarrhea over the past three days. Examination reveals a perianal rash and the presence of acidic stools. The mother shares that she recently returned to work and began substituting breast milk with cow's milk. You advise her on the benefits of expressing and storing breast milk for feeding. Considering the clinical presentation and history, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Both primary and secondary Lactose Intolerance

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Primary Lactose Intolerance : Rare in infants , it's a genetic condition where babies are born with low levels of lactase enzyme . Symptoms would likely have appeared earlier if this were the case.
• Option C. Cow's Milk Protein Allergy: While similar in presentation, the immediate onset of symptoms after introducing cow's milk suggests intolerance to lactose rather than an allergic reaction to milk proteins.
• Option D. Gastroenteritis: Typically associated with infectious agents , it could present with vomiting and diarrhea but less likely to be directly linked to the introduction of cow's milk.
• Educational Objective:
• Lactose intolerance can either be primary (lactase activity that gradually declines with aging) or secondary (an infection or enteropathy damages the villi, resulting in lactose intolerance).
• Many patients who have lactose intolerance diagnosed can tolerate Yoghurt.
• Other products, such as whole or skim milk, ice cream, or buttermilk, have more lactose (approximately 10 g/serving) and are less likely to be tolerated.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874. O P Ghai – Page No-293

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her 2 months old child with complaints of recurrent infections and the skin findings as shown in the image. On examination the following skin findings are noted. What is the micro element that is deficient in this child and its dosage for the treatment of diarrhoea in his cousin who is 8 months old?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/04/29/picture10_FOi3yL4.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B. Zinc deficiency and 20 mg
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A and C : Copper deficiency doesn’t manifest as the features mentioned above.
• Option D: Dose of zinc in a 8 month old child is 20mg/kg for 14 days not 10mg/kg.
• Ref ; Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother presents her 6-year-old son to the clinic, reporting dysphagia. Upon examination, the child shows no signs of respiratory distress. A chest X-ray reveals radiopaque findings suggestive of a foreign body aspiration. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture22.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Immediate Tracheoscopic removal: Tracheoscopic removal is indicated if there are signs of severe airway obstruction or respiratory distress . However, in stable patients without such signs, immediate intervention may not be necessary.
• Option B. Immediate Esophagoscopic removal: Esophagoscopic removal is appropriate if the foreign body is confirmed to be lodged in the esophagus and causing symptoms. However, if the location is uncertain or if the patient is asymptomatic, immediate intervention may not be warranted.
• Option D. Immediate Laryngoscopic removal: Laryngoscopic removal is indicated if the foreign body is visible in the larynx or upper airway and causing significant obstruction. However, if there are no signs of upper airway compromise, immediate intervention may not be required.
• Educational Objective:
• Foreign body in the esophagus - Since it doesn’t produce any major symptoms and it will dislodge into stomach and will be removed out itself. So, the best management is to wait and watch.
• In case of Coin in the trachea immediate removal is advised.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 360Page No 7818-7821.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 360Page No 7818-7821.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 3-year-old daughter to the clinic due to 6 episodes of loose stools. Upon examination, the child is alert and exhibits normal skin turgor. When offered water, the child drinks adequately. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In the child , he is alert , and his skin pinch test skin goes back quickly and on offering water the child drink , these are the features of no dehydration . So, to treat this case we will use Plan-A.
• Plan A - ORS per loose stool (10cc/Kg or stool volume)
• Plan B- B is used for some dehydration - ORS 75ml/kg over 4 hrs
• Plan C- is used for severe dehydration
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother rushes her 2-year-old child to the emergency department, concerned about severe dehydration. Upon examination, the child appears lethargic and weak. The healthcare provider notices several physical signs. Which of the following features is least likely to be observed in cases of severe dehydration?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Drinks Eagerly on offering water
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Very sunken eyes: This is a typical sign of severe dehydration due to decreased fluid volume.
• Option B. Skin goes back slowly after a skin pinch test : Also characteristic of severe dehydration , indicating decreased skin turgor.
• Option C. Very dry buccal mucosa : Another hallmark sign of severe dehydration due to decreased saliva production and fluid loss.
• Educational Objective:
• In case of severe dehydration, the child will be unable to drink the offered water.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her child with a history of episodes of loose stools for 1 day along with features of irritability and restlessness. There is no history of fever or blood in stools. On examination, the child's eyes are sunken, and skin pinch goes back slowly within 2 seconds. On giving water, the child accepts and drinks readily. What will be the best management for this child?
options: Start intravenous fluids Immediately and immediately refer to a higher center.
Give Zinc supplementation and ORS only and ask mother to come back if there is development of any danger signs.
Administer intravenous antibiotic and immediately refer to a higher center
Start oral antibiotics and send the child home

explanation.short: Ans. B) Give Zinc supplementation and oral rehydration and ask mother to come back if some danger signs develop.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Start intravenous fluids immediately and immediately refer to a higher center: Intravenous fluids are typically reserved for cases of severe dehydration or if the child cannot tolerate oral fluids . Immediate referral to a higher center may not be necessary if the child responds well to oral rehydration therapy.
• Option C. Administer intravenous antibiotics and immediately refer to a higher center: Antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated cases of acute diarrhea without specific indications such as dysentery. Immediate referral may not be necessary unless there are signs of severe illness or complications.
• Option D. Start oral antibiotics and send the child home : Antibiotics are not routinely recommended for the management of acute diarrhea unless specific bacterial etiology like dysentery is suspected. Starting oral antibiotics without proper indication can contribute to antibiotic resistance and is not appropriate in this scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• OP Ghai 8 th Edition, Page No-290-295.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 8 th Edition, Page No-290-295.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 1-year-old infant with a history of 10–12 episodes of watery stools per day for the last 2 days. Recognizing the severity of the condition, you decide to initiate zinc supplementation along with additional measures to manage the diarrhea effectively. Considering the comprehensive approach to management, what else should be prescribed to the 1-year-old child?
options: ORS with low-lactose diet and probiotics

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. ORS with antibiotics: Antibiotics are not routinely recommended for acute watery diarrhea unless there is evidence of bacterial infection or specific indications for antibiotic therapy.
• Option C. ORS with low-lactose diet: While a low-lactose diet may be beneficial in cases of lactose intolerance , it is not the primary intervention for managing acute diarrhea, especially in the initial stages of treatment.
• Option D. ORS with low-lactose diet and probiotics: While these interventions may have potential benefits , they are not the initial management priority in acute diarrhea, which requires prompt rehydration with ORS.
• Educational Objective:
• In managing acute diarrhea in infants , oral rehydration solution (ORS) plays a central role in restoring hydration and electrolyte balance , which are essential for recovery. ORS contains the optimal balance of electrolytes and glucose to replace fluid losses due to diarrhea and prevent dehydration . It is the cornerstone of treatment recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO) for managing acute diarrhea in children.
• While zinc supplementation , low-lactose diet , and probiotics can be beneficial in certain cases, the initial management priority is to ensure adequate rehydration with ORS . Antibiotics are not routinely indicated in cases of acute watery diarrhea unless there is evidence of bacterial etiology or severe illness requiring targeted treatment. A low-lactose diet and probiotics may be considered in cases of lactose intolerance or persistent diarrhea, but they do not replace the primary role of ORS in managing acute diarrhea.
• OP Ghai 8 th Edition, Page No-290-295.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 8 th Edition, Page No-290-295.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 7-month-old child Rajni with a few episodes of loose stools suggestive of acute gastroenteritis. On examination, the child weighs 6 kg, with sunken eyes and skin pinch that goes back slowly in 2 seconds. What will be your next step in the management of this child?
options: RL infusion 180mL in the first hour followed by 420 mL in the next 5 hours
RL infusion 180 mL in the first hour followed by 480 mL in the next 2 hours
RL infusion 240 mL in the first hour followed by 360 mL in the next 5 hours
RL infusion 120 mL in the first hour followed by 360 mL in the next 5 hours

explanation.short: Ans. A) RL infusion 180 mL in the first hour followed by 420 mL in the next 5 hours
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Assessment of dehydration in a patient with diarrhoea
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No-289
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No-289

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 2-year-old baby Somu to a pediatrician with a history of multiple episodes of loose, watery stools for 2 days. His mother mentions that he has been restless and irritated since yesterday. However, he can drink water when offered. Physical examination reveals an irritated and thirsty baby with a heart rate of 140/min and a respiratory rate of 39/min. The pediatrician pinches the abdominal skin, which returns within 2 seconds. Based on the history and physical examination findings, which of the following is the best management plan in this case?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A - Plan A is the treatment of No dehydration .
• Option B - Plan C is the treatment of Severe dehydration.
• Option C- Plan D is not a recommended plan for the treatment of dehydration .
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No-290.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No-290.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following Plan of treatment with level of dehydration:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture33.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. A) A2 B3 C4 D1

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-year-old girl is brought to a clinic by her mother in a small town in India, with a history of intermittent diarrhea, periumbilical pain, and bloating that have persisted for the last three years. The symptoms occasionally exacerbate after the intake of milk and dairy products. On physical examination, rashes around the umbilicus are noted. A Benedict's test performed during the visit yields positive results, indicating the presence of reducing sugars in the urine. Given that an enzyme deficiency affecting lactose metabolism is suspected, which of the following food items is the girl most likely to tolerate?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Whole milk: Whole milk contains a significant amount of lactose and is therefore unlikely to be tolerated by someone with lactose intolerance.
• Option C. Buttermilk: Buttermilk generally contains less lactose than whole milk but still may contain enough to cause symptoms in someone with lactose intolerance.
• Option D. Skimmed milk: Skimmed milk contains a similar amount of lactose as whole milk and is therefore unlikely to be tolerated by someone with lactose intolerance.
• Educational objective:
• Yoghurt , is the most likely food that will be tolerated due to its lower lactose content compared to whole milk and skimmed milk , making it a suitable alternative for individuals with lactose intolerance.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874. O P Ghai – Page No-293
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 1854-1874. O P Ghai – Page No-293

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following signs along with the levels of dehydration:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/27/picture35.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) A3 B1 C3 D2

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned father brings his 8-year-old son, Dev, to the pediatric clinic after noticing his frequent complaints of bone pain and a recent incident where a minor fall resulted in a fracture. During the examination, the pediatrician observes multiple healing fractures of varying ages on the X-rays, along with noticeable bruises on the skin. Initially, there is a suspicion of non-accidental injury, but further assessment reveals Dev has a short stature, distinctive blue sclera, and visible bone deformities in his limbs and spine. These findings suggest a genetic condition affecting bone strength rather than external trauma. What is the pattern of inheritance for Dev's diagnosed condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The diagnosis based on the David’s history is Osteogenesis imperfecta and it is Autosomal Dominant in nature.
• The clinical features include
• Multiple fractures: recurrent fractures, which can occur with minimal or no trauma. Can cause deformities or shortening of limbs. Blue sclera : Its due to the underlying connective tissue abnormalities. Dental problems : Brittle teeth that break easily or wear down quickly. ( Dentinogenesis imperfecta - The bluish, opalescent sheen on several teeth results from genetically defective dentin ). Hearing loss: Hearing loss occurs in approximately 20% of young children and as many as 50% of adults by the age of 50 Skeletal deformities : scoliosis (curvature of the spine), kyphosis (hunchback), or pectus excavatum (sunken chest). Short stature: due to the impact of skeletal abnormalities on bone growth. Easy bruising : Osteogenesis imperfecta can cause easy bruising or bleeding, particularly in people with severe forms of the condition.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of frequent fractures from minimal trauma , blue sclera , short stature , and bone deformities , strongly suggests Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI). OI is a genetic disorder primarily affecting collagen , leading to brittle bones and a susceptibility to fractures. The majority of OI cases are inherited in an autosomal dominant manner , meaning a single copy of the defective gene from either parent can cause the disorder.
• Note - Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) (brittle bone disease) is the most common genetic cause of osteoporosis.
explanation.ref: nelson 21 st Edition – Page No-14415-14427

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 3-year-old child is brought in by their mother, who is distressed due to criticism from her mother-in-law regarding the child's health. The child has developmental delays, intellectual disability, and distinctive physical features including sandal gap toes. Recently, the child has been diagnosed with a congenital heart disease. Given the child's clinical features, as shown in the images, what is the most common congenital heart disease associated with this presentation ?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture14.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The clinical features of the child is pointing towards the diagnosis of Downs Syndrome. The most common congenital heart disease in case of Downs syndrome is Endocardial Cushion defects (AVSD)
• The clinical features of Downs syndrome (trisomy 21) are.
• Flat facial profile and an upward slant to the eye.
• Short neck Abnormally shaped ears White spots on the iris of the eye (Brushfield spots) Single, deep transverse crease on the palm of the hand. Brachycephaly Protrudin  g tongue Presenile Alzheimer’s disease.
• Option A, C and D aren’t the most common congenital heart disease in Downs Syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Downs syndrome - Approximately 40% children have congenital heart disease . Endocardial cushion defects account for about 40-60% cases . Presence of heart disease is the most significant factor in determining survival. All children should have a cardiac evaluation before 9 months of age, including echocardiography.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A concerned mother visits your clinic with her 3-year-old child, worried about the child's inadequate growth. The child's medical history reveals a low birth weight, along with ongoing feeding difficulties and a poor appetite. Upon physical examination, you note several distinctive features: a prominent forehead, a small jaw, a narrow chin, and clinodactyly. These clinical findings, combined with the child's growth issues, lead you to consider a specific diagnosis. Based on the information provided, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture7.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Most cases occur sporadically , chromosome 11p15 , maternal disomy of chromosome 7 .
• Metabolic syndrome. Uterine and vaginal dysgenesis Gonadal hypofunction or testicular cancer in males. Low muscle mass or low bone mineral density. Myoclonus dystonia (in people with chromosome 7-related RSS).
• Educational Objective:
• Uniparental disomy (UPD) is when a child gets both copies of a chromosome from one parent . This can lead to conditions like Prader-Willi syndrome (if both chromosome 15s come from the mother) and Angelman syndrome.
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 1696, 2987
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 1696, 2987

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine OPD session on Holi, a police officer brings in an unidentified child for age verification. To determine the child's age, you order a wrist X-ray. Upon reviewing the X-ray, you identify the age of the child to be approximately 2 months. Which of the following carpal bones did you primarily rely on to estimate the age of the child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Scaphoid will ossify by 4-6 years of age .
• Option C. Lunate will ossify by 2-4 years of age
• Option D. Trapezoid will ossify by 4-6 years of age.
• Educational Objective:
• Although there is great individual variability, approximate ossification times are as follows:
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 1127
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 1127

subject: Paediatrics
question: In the pediatric clinic, a concerned mother presents her infant, worried about the child's weight gain. She compares her baby to her sister-in-law's seemingly healthier child. Upon examination, the infant appears active and alert. The mother reports that the birth weight was 2600 grams, and currently, the scale reads 5300 grams, indicating that the infant's weight has doubled. As an intern, you recall the standard growth milestones and reassure the mother. Based on standard pediatric growth patterns, at what age is an infant's weight expected to double from birth?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The weight of the baby triples by 1 year of age and doubles by 5 months .
• Option B. The weight of the baby becomes 4 times the birth weight by 2 years of age and doubles by 5-6 months .
• Option C. The weight of the baby becomes 5 times the birth weight by 3 years of age and doubles by 5-6 months .
• Educational objective :
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 6-year-old child is brought to the pediatric clinic by their mother, who is concerned about the child's noticeably shorter stature compared to peers. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes that the child's short stature appears disproportionate, with a more significant discrepancy between the upper and lower body segments. Considering a potential diagnosis of a condition affecting growth, the pediatrician decides to assess the child's body proportions more closely by measuring the upper segment and lower segment ratios. Understanding the normal progression of body proportions from childhood to adulthood is crucial in this assessment. At what age do the upper and lower segments of the body typically reach adult proportions ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A - By the age of 2 years the ratio is 1.4:1
• Option C - By the age of 7 years the ratio is 1:1 whereas the adult ratio is 0.9:1
• Educational Objective:
• The upper-to-lower body segment ratio (U/L ratio) provides an assessment of truncal growth relative to limb growth . The lower-body segment is defined as the length from the top of the symphysis pubis to the floor , and the upper-body segment is the total height minus the lower-body segment.
• The U/L ratio equals approximately 1.7 at birth, 1.3 at 3 year, and 1.0 after 7 year. Higher U/L ratios are characteristic of short-limb dwarfism, as occurs with Turner syndrome or bone disorders, whereas lower ratios suggest hypogonadism or Marfan syndrome.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9th Edition, Page No- 43-51; Nelson 21 st Ed Pg 1183

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 13-year-old boy presented to the OPD with complaints of fatigue, shortness of breath and fainting episode. On further evaluation features of short stature and webbed neck were noted. He was subjected to cardiological examination where he is diagnosed with pulmonary stenosis. A genetic testing was done in which chromosome 12 is found to be abnormal. On compiling all the history and clinical features he is diagnosed as a case of pseudo turner syndrome known as Noonan Syndrome. What is the gene responsible for his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CLCN 7 gene mutation will results in osteopetrosis , a condition characterized by increased bone density and abnormal bone growth.
• Option C. CFTR gene mutation will result in Cystic Fibrosis , affecting the lungs, pancreas, and other organs.
• Option D. ATM gene mutation will results in Ataxia Telangiectasia , rare inherited childhood neurological disorder that affects the part of the brain that controls motor movement (intended movement of muscles) and speech. The triad of symptoms includes progressive ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias and sinopulmonary disease.
• Educational objective:
• Turner Syndrome vs Noonan Syndrome
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 601– Page No 11714-11716
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 601– Page No 11714-11716

subject: Paediatrics
question:
During rounds with your consultant, several cases of genetic diso rders in the pediatric ward were discussed. Your consultant asks about the most common chromosomal disorder, its complication, and its associated disorders. Match the following columns correctly to demonstrate your understanding and facilitate further discussion:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/screenshot-2024-03-16-101903_YbJIS5O.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Nondisjunction - Common aneuploidies seen in live born babies include Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Edward syndrome (trisomy 18), Patau syndrome (trisomy 13) and Turner syndrome (monosorny X). Chromosome deletion - William syndrome (7qll.23); retinoblastoma with mental retardation and dysmorphic facies (13q14.1); Prader-Willi syndrome (hypotonia, mental retardation and obesity, 15qll); Rubinstein Taybi syndrome (microcephaly, broad thumbs and big toes, dysmorphism and mental retardation; 16q13); and DiGeorge syndrome (congenital heart defect, hypoplasia of parathyroid and thymus, facial and palate anomalies; 22qll). Genomic imprinting - Prader-Willi syndrome (microdeletion on paternal side or inheritance of both copies from maternal side) and Angelman syndrome (microdeletion on maternal side or inheritance of both copies from paternal side).
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 3023-24

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine clinic visit, a mother brings her 6-year-old daughter for evaluation. The mother reports that the child can climb stairs using alternate feet, ride a tricycle, remember her name and age, but is unable to narrate stories. Given this information, what is the child's most likely developmental age ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B- A 4-year-old child can narrate stories where as a 3-year-old can only ask questions.
• Option C- A 5-year-old child asks meaning of the words where as a 3-year-old can only ask questions.
• Option D- A 6-year-old child not only ask questions but also can narrate the daily things in a better manner.
• Educational Objective:
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a developmental assessment in the pediatric clinic, you observe a child who runs adeptly and climbs stairs with one foot per step. Recalling the lecture from your pediatric mentor on milestone development, you consider what the next stage of stair climbing development is. At what age is a child expected to develop the ability to climb steps using alternate feet?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . By the age of 2 years the child can run well , climb upstairs and downstairs with one step at a time
• Option C. The child can start hopping and goes downstairs with alternate feet by the age of 4 years.
• Option D. The child can start Skipping by the age of 5 years .
• Educational objective
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Arrange the following milestones in the correct order of their attainment; I. Build tower of 4 cubes II. Make simple sentences III. Drawing a circle IV. Drawing a rectangle

explanation.short: Ans.  B) I, II, III, IV
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The age of attainment of the milestones is: -
• Builds a tower of 4 cubes- 18 months Makes simple sentences- 2 years Drawing a circle- 3 years Drawing a rectangle- 4 years
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 1080
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 1080

subject: Paediatrics
question: In the pediatric clinic, a mother presents her 10-month-old child for a routine check-up. During the examination, you find the child to be both active and alert. You decide to evaluate the child's developmental milestones. Based on what you know about the normal development of children at this age, which of the following milestones would you NOT expect to find in a 10-month-old child?
options: Sign of Waving bye – bye while leaving OPD.
Standing without support on the corridors of hospital.
Plays a peek –a – boo with mother and siblings.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Standing without support on the corridors of hospital.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, B and D are true regarding the milestones achieved in a 10months old child.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Identify the sequence in which the following developmental milestones are typically attained?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture6.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) D → C → A → B
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• A → Walks with support (11 months)
• B → Creeps upstairs (15 months)
• C → Sits with support (6 months)
• D → Feet to mouth (5 months)
• So, the correct sequence is: D → C → A → B
• Educational objective:
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 45-49.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother visits your clinic for a routine evaluation of her 3-month-old child. Upon examination, you note that the baby is alert and active. The child weighs 4000 grams and has a height of 53 cm. To complete the assessment, you decide to measure the head circumference, a crucial indicator of neurological development. Recalling the typical growth patterns of infants, by how much does the head circumference usually increases per month during the first 3 months of life?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The normal head circumference of a newborn is around 35 cm.
• Head circumference is measured by placing a soft non-stretchable tape around the head just above the eyebrows and finding the largest circumference over the occiput . This is 33-37 cm at birth in term babies.
• Method of recording head circumference. Note the crossed tape method
• Note – At birth, Chest circumference is about 3 cm lesser than head circumference and if the difference is more than 3 cm it is an indication of intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR).
• Head circumference in the first 3 months of life increases by 2 cm per month , at 3-6 months of life : 1 cm per month , at 6-12 months of life : 0.5 cm per month.
• Educational objective:
• Ref OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 13
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 13

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A pediatrician in a rural healthcare center is approached by a mother carrying her newborn for an initial health check-up. The infant, as observed in the image provided, exhibits significant limb abnormalities. The mother reports that during her pregnancy, she had taken medication from a local healer, which you suspect may have contained Thalidomide due to the timing and nature of the birth defects. Considering the history of maternal medication intake and the clinical presentation of the infant, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture10.jpg]
options: Failure of development of flat bones
Failure of development of long bones

explanation.short: Ans. D) Failure of development of long bones
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Phocomelia refers to Failure of development of long bones.
• Rare birth defects that cause severe birth defects , especially of the upper limbs . The bones of the arms may be extremely shortened and even absent . The fingers of the hands may be fused . Risk factors: Maternal thalidomide therapy. Genetic inheritance. Roberts’s syndrome. An extreme case results in the absence of the upper bones of both the arms and legs so that the hands and feet appear attached directly to the body . This is called tetraphocomelia Other associated anomalies: Encephalocele. Hydrocephalus. Bicornuate uterus. Short neck. Thrombocytopenia. Renal and cardiac anomalies.
• Other 3 options are incorrect.
• Educational Objective:
• Phocomelia Syndrome is a rare birth defect characterized by underdeveloped long bones , often related to maternal thalidomide exposure , leading to shortened or absent limbs and potentially associated with other anomalies like Encephalocele , Hydrocephalus, Bicornuate uterus , Short neck , thrombocytopenia , renal and cardiac anomalies.
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 1698
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 1698

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her infant for vaccination. During the examination, you observe that the infant is capable of moving their head from side to side while tracking a moving object, can lift their head from a prone position approximately 45° off the examining table, smiles responsively when encouraged, and makes cooing sounds. However, the infant is unable to maintain a seated position. Based on these developmental milestones, what is the most likely age of the infant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . A 1-month-old child cannot hold his neck.
• Option B . A 6-month-old child can sit with support.
• Option C . A 2-month-old child cannot hold his neck and make cooing sounds.
• Educational Objective:
• Developmental milestones of an infant
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician is conducting routine health check-ups in the outpatient department for children of various ages. While performing anthropometric measurements, a newly joined pediatric intern observes the process and later engages in a discussion about the significance and technical aspects of these measurements in assessing child health and nutritional status. The intern makes several statements about common practices in pediatric anthropometry. Which of the following statements made by the intern is NOT true regarding standard anthropometric measurements in pediatrics?
options: Skin fold thickness is measured in sub scapular region
Weight is measured to the nearest 100 gram
Standing height is about 1.7 cm less than recumbent length

explanation.short: Ans. D) Standing height is about 1.7 cm less than recumbent length.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Birth weight tripling by 1 year of age is a general growth milestone , indicating adequate nutrition and health during the infant's first year.
• Option B. Skin fold thickness measurements at the subscapular region , along with other sites, are used to estimate body fat composition , which is crucial for assessing nutritional status.
• Option C. Weighing infants and children to the nearest 100 grams is a standard practice in to ensure accurate monitoring of growth and early detection of any deviations from expected growth patterns.
• Educational Objective:
• If a child is less than 2 years old, measure recumbent length. If the child is aged 2 years or older and able to stand, measure standing height. In general, standing height is about 0.7 cm less than recumbent length.
• Birth weight of Indian children 2.8 Kg . Term neonate regains lost birth weight by 7 to 10 days, preterm by 10 to 14 days Birth weight doubles by 6 months, triples by 1 year, quadruples by 2 years and increases 5 times by 3 years.
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 4-month-old infant is brought in by their mother for a routine pediatric evaluation. The child appears alert and active, and according to the mother, the infant has been gaining weight and height steadily since birth. The pediatrician proceeds with a thorough physical examination and notes the following finding. The pediatrician considers various differential diagnoses for this finding. Which of the following conditions is least likely to be associated with this clinical feature?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture12.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. PKU - Infants with PKU can present with microcephaly if the condition is untreated due to the toxic effects of elevated phenylalanine levels on brain development.
• Option B. Down's syndrome - Children with Down's syndrome often have brachycephaly , which may give the appearance of a smaller head circumference and can sometimes be associated with actual microcephaly.
• Option D. Fetal Hydantoin syndrome - This syndrome can result from exposure to antiepileptic drugs in utero and is associated with a range of congenital defects , including microcephaly.
• Educational Objective:
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 40.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 40.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 12-month-old child to the OPD department for a routine pediatric evaluation. On examination the child has adequate progression in height and weight. Upon evaluating the child's development, the pediatrician finds that the gross and fine motor milestones are being met as expected for the child's age. The pediatrician then moves on to assess the child's language development. What is the expected level of vocabulary development for a 12-month-old child?
options: Limited to a bi-syllable sound like baba, mama

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The child used Bisyllables like Baba
• Option C. At 18 months , the child can use 10 words with meaning.
• Option D. The child will start speaking sentences by 2 years of age.
• Educational Objective:
• The development of Language mile stones are as follows.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 52-53.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 16-year-old girl presents to the pediatric clinic with her mother, who is concerned about her daughter's emotional well-being following incidents of bullying at school. The mother reports that her daughter has been ridiculed for her lack of secondary sexual characteristics and absence of menarche. After a thorough history and physical examination, the pediatrician orders an ultrasound, which reveals an infantile uterus and streak ovaries. Further genetic workup indicates a ring chromosome abnormality involving one of the X chromosomes. The pediatrician begins a discussion about starting hormonal therapy to induce secondary sexual characteristic development. During the consultation, the mother inquires about any associated cardiac anomalies that might be present with her daughter's condition. As a pediatrician, which cardiac anomaly would you inform the mother is most commonly associated with this condition ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The diagnosis based on the history and the Image given is the Turners Syndrome.
• In Turners Syndrome CVS abnormalities like coarctation of aorta , bicuspid aortic valve can be seen.
• Short stature Shield chest Webbed neck Wide spread nipples and Short fourth metacarpal
• CVS : coarctation of aorta , bicuspid aortic valve Renal : horseshoe kidney. Gonads : gonadal dysgenesis, gonadoblastoma. Strabismus Cataract.
• Ultrasound pelvis reveals hypoplastic uterus and streak ovaries. Karyotype is indicated in all turner syndrome to exclude presence of Y chromosome (associated with gonadoblastoma). Echocardiography and ultrasound for kidney for screening of cardiac and renal abnormalities. Managements: GH therapy (0.1-0.15unit/kg/day)
• Option A, B and C are not the most common CVS abnormality in case of Turner syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Turner syndrome is a condition characterized by the presence of a single X chromosome (45, X karyotype) or a structural abnormality of one of the X chromosomes , such as a ring chromosome. Coarctation of the aorta is a common cardiac anomaly associated with Turner syndrome , occurring in approximately 10-20% of individuals with the condition. It involves a narrowing of the aorta , which can lead to hypertension and an increased workload on the left ventricle.
explanation.ref: Essential pediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No-637

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric clinic by a mother who is concerned about her child's poor growth and difficulty feeding. The physical examination reveals microcephaly (a small head circumference), a cleft lip and palate, and polydactyly. Based on these findings, the pediatrician considers a diagnosis of Patau Syndrome. Which of the following is NOT a feature of Patau Syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Holoprosencephaly is a common neurological anomaly in Patau Syndrome , resulting from incomplete brain (MC forebrain) division into right and left hemispheres . HPE ranges in severity and often causes facial development issues .
• Option B. Cutis Aplasia , a congenital absence of a portion of the skin , is also observed in some cases of Patau Syndrome .
• Option C. Polycystic kidney disease and other renal anomalies can be part of the spectrum of abnormalities seen in Patau Syndrome .
• Educational objective:
• Patau Syndrome – Trisomy of 13
• Clinical features of Patau Syndrome include,
• Cleft lip and palate Polydactyly Microcephalus Holoprosencephaly Cutis aplasia Poly cystic kidney disease and Developmental delay
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother presents her 5-year-old son to the pediatric clinic. She reports that her child has an insatiable appetite (hyperphagia), significant weight gain leading to obesity, and developmental delays. Upon further discussion, the mother reveals that her son had low muscle tone (hypotonia) as an infant and currently suffers from sleep disturbances and behavioral issues. Based on the clinical presentation, the pediatrician suspects a genetic disorder and considers Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) as a differential diagnosis. What additional clinical feature is commonly associated with Prader-Willi syndrome, and with which type of genomic imprinting is the syndrome related?

explanation.short: Ans. D. Hypogonadism and Paternal deletion
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Prader-Willi Syndrome is seen as a result of maternal imprinting and Paternal deletion.
• Clinical features of Prader-Willi Syndrome are;
• Hypotonia : weak muscle tone and poor motor development in infants. Hyperphagia : an insatiable appetite and overeating leading to obesity. Developmental delays: motor, speech, and cognitive development may be delayed. Short stature: Individuals with Prader-Willi Syndrome are usually shorter than average. Behavioral problems : temper tantrums, stubbornness, obsessive-compulsive behaviors, skin-picking, and difficulty with transitions. Hypogonadism: underdeveloped sexual organs and delayed or absent puberty. Sleep disturbances: daytime sleepiness, sleep apnea, and disrupted sleep patterns. Altered pain perception
• Educational objective:
• Prader-Willi syndrome is characterized by a variety of clinical features , commonly include hypogonadism, which manifests as genital hypoplasia , incomplete pubertal development , and infertility in later life . PWS is a complex genetic disorder that is typically associated with the loss of function of genes in a particular region of chromosome 15 (15q11-q13) that are normally expressed from the paternal chromosome . This loss of function is most commonly due to a deletion of this region on the paternal chromosome.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 625.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 625.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old female presents to the pediatric OPD with complaints of frequent joint pain, easy bruising, and hypermobility. While explaining she reports she has some magical skills and she can stretch her skin easily and that she has had several episodes of gastrointestinal bleeding. As she was accompanied by her father you ask for the family history, he admits the same that he has also experienced the same and he is being treated by the physiotherapist, What is the diagnosis based her clinical features and family history, what is the mode of inheritance in this case?
options: Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and Autosomal Dominant
Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and Autosomal Recessive

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Marfan syndrome and Autosomal Dominant - Marfan syndrome is associated with connective tissue abnormalities and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern , but it is typically characterized by tall stature, long extremities, and cardiovascular anomalies, not the skin and joint manifestations described.
• Option C. Marfan syndrome and Autosomal Recessive – Incorrect.
• Option D. Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and Autosomal Recessive - Incorrect . E hlers Danlos Syndrome is an Autosomal Dominant inheritance.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features of joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, easy bruising, and a positive family history suggest a diagnosis of Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS). EDS is a group of connective tissue disorders characterized by varying degrees of skin, joint, and blood vessel involvement . The most common type of EDS is the hypermobility type , which is typically inherited in an autosomal dominant manner . This means that only one copy of the altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 618-619 and 644-645.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 618-619 and 644-645.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old girl presents to the pediatric department with her parents, who are concerned about her delayed developmental milestones. They report that their daughter has experienced multiple joint dislocations, particularly in the shoulders and knees, and seems to have unusually flexible joints. On physical examination, the pediatrician notes the child's hypermobility and conducts an eye exam, which reveals decreased visual acuity and a dislocated lens. Given the constellation of symptoms and the findings on examination, the pediatrician suspects a disorder of a fibrillary protein with autosomal dominant inheritance. Interestingly, a similar pattern of inheritance is observed in Osteogenesis Imperfecta, where not all individuals with the mutation exhibit symptoms. This variability in the clinical presentation of an autosomal dominant condition is best described by which of the following genetic concepts?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Pseudodominance ; Pseudodominance is the situation in which the inheritance of a recessive trait mimics a dominant pattern.
• Example: Hemophilia and red-green color blindness are recessive, X-linked, pseudodominant genetic disorders,
• Option C. Complete Penetrance means the gene or genes for a trait are expressed in all the population who have the genes. “
• Option D. Loss of heterozygosity. LOH (short for “loss of heterozygosity”) refers to a type of mutation that results in the loss of one copy of a segment of DNA
• Educational objective:
• Incomplete penetrance refers to the phenomenon where not all individuals with a mutant genotype show the mutant phenotype . In other words, some individuals who carry the dominant allele for a disorder may not exhibit any of the clinical symptoms. This could be due to a variety of factors, including interactions with other genes , environmental influences , or variations in the expression of the gene .
• In a pedigree this can appear as a skipped generation
explanation.ref: Nelson 21st edition page 2967-68

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 9-year-old girl is being evaluated for short stature. During the evaluation, several distinct physical features are noted: a webbed neck, downward-slanting palpebral fissures, cubitus valgus, pectus carinatum superiorly, and a shield-like chest. An echocardiogram reveals cardiovascular abnormalities. Interestingly, similar clinical features are observed in her brother. What is the most probable diagnosis considering the clinical features and the fact that both siblings are affected?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Turner syndrome : Primarily affects females and is characterized by the presence of a single X chromosome . The features described fit Turner syndrome, but its occurrence in a male sibling (as in the brother mentioned) rules out this condition.
• Option B. Klinefelter syndrome : Affects males and is characterized by an extra X chromosome (47, XXY). The clinical features mentioned do not align with Klinefelter syndrome, and it would not explain the sister's presentation.
• Option D. Fragile X syndrome : Primarily associated with intellectual disability (low IQ), behavioural challenges , and certain physical traits , but not typically presenting with the cardiovascular and skeletal features described in the question.
• Educational Objective:
• Noonan syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by Noonan facies (hypertelorism, ptosis, short neck, low-set ears), short stature , congenital heart disease , and multiple skeletal and hematologic abnormalities.
• Noonan syndrome can affect both males and females , which is consistent with the brother also exhibiting similar features.
• It is primarily an autosomal dominant disorder composed of multiple genetic types . Heterozygous mutations in PTPN11 cause about 50% of Noonan cases . Other cases are caused by mutations in other RAS pathway associated genes such as KRAS, SOS1 , and NRAS . Autosomal recessive forms have also been identified due to mutations of SHOC2 or CBL.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 601– Page No 11714-11716
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 601– Page No 11714-11716

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric outpatient department by his mother due to concerns about his unusual height; he is taller than his classmates and even his class teacher. Further evaluation reveals language defects, learning disabilities, and decreased IQ. Physical examination notes significant gynecomastia (large breasts), delayed secondary sexual characteristics, and small testes. Based on the clinical history, what is the most probable diagnosis, and what karyotype is associated with this condition?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Klinefelter syndrome and 47 XXY
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fragile x syndrome is seen as a result of trinucleotide repeats (mutations in the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome) and they will have normal karyotype with features of low IQ, long face, larger ears and gonads.
• Option B. In case of Klinefelter syndrome the abnormality of karyotype seen is 47XXY not the 45 XO karyotype . 45,XO karyotype is associated with Turner syndrome
• Option D. Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder characterized by tall stature, long limbs, and cardiovascular abnormalities , but it does not typically include the cognitive or gonadal features seen in Klinefelter syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Klinefelter syndrome is characterized by the presence of an extra X chromosome in males , leading to a 47,XXY karyotype. This genetic anomaly results in a phenotype that includes taller than average height , gynecomastia , small testes , delayed or absent secondary sexual characteristics , and often language and learning difficulties.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 601– Page No 117161-1721
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 601– Page No 117161-1721

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric outpatient department due to concerns about his unusually tall stature and intellectual disability. The pediatrician notes several features suggestive of a genetic condition, including a long face, large ears, and hyperextensible joints. After a thorough examination, the possibility of fragile X syndrome is considered. The doctor explains to the mother that the condition might present more severely in future generations, a phenomenon attributed to the increase in the number of trinucleotide repeats. Which concept is the doctor referring to?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture4.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Both A and B
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option C. Complete penetrance is seen in case of Autosomal disorders not in case of trinucleotide repeats.
• Educational Objective:
• Anticipation describes the phenomenon where a genetic disorder becomes more severe or has an earlier onset as it is passed down through generations . This is due to the expansion of trinucleotide repeats (in this case, CGG repeats in the FMR1 gene) in each successive generation (a/k/a Sherman's paradox)
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 85– Page No 3050
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 85– Page No 3050

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A newborn baby boy is brought to the attention of a pediatrician immediately after birth. The initial observation reveals a cleft lip and palate, a skin defect on the scalp, holoprosencephaly, postaxial polydactyly, and malformed ears, as shown in the accompanying image. Considering these congenital anomalies, along with the potential for an associated ventricular septal defect (VSD) and undescended testis, the pediatrician suspects a specific syndrome. Based on the clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture7.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Edward syndrome : Also known as trisomy 18 , may have some similar feature of Cleft lip and palate and congenital VSD whereas, it is also associated with a different set of malformations , such as clenched hands with overlapping fingers, rocker-bottom feet, and low birth weight.
• Option B. DiGeorge syndrome : Caused by a deletion on chromosome 22q11.2 , it presents with congenital heart defects , immune deficiencies , and distinctive facial features but does not typically include holoprosencephaly or postaxial polydactyly.
• Option D. Williams syndrome : Characterized by cardiovascular disease , distinctive facial features , and developmental delays , Williams syndrome does not encompass the range of anomalies described in the question, such as holoprosencephaly or postaxial polydactyly.
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A worried mother brought her 5-year-old girl child to the pediatric OPD with complaints of her constant feeling of hunger. Her mother mentions that she is obese than everyone in her class. On evaluation she has a short stature, intellectual disability and the following appearance. While asking her history in the past, her mother reminded that she had a feeding difficulty at birth as she was hypotonic and she had this voracious appetite from age of 2. Her mother was concerned about her future and asks you to diagnose her. What is the most probable diagnosis you will make and convey to her mother?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture10.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Angelman syndrome : Characterized by severe intellectual disability , lack of speech , seizures , and a happy demeanor , but not typically associated with obesity or the described feeding pattern.
• Option C. DiGeorge syndrome : Associated with congenital heart defects, immune deficiencies, and cleft palate , among other symptoms, but not typically presenting with obesity and excessive hunger.
• Option D. Williams syndrome : Known for hypercalcemia , cardiovascular issues , unique cognitive profile , and very social personality , Williams syndrome does not typically involve the described pattern of early-life feeding difficulties followed by excessive appetite and obesity.
• Educational objective:
• The history of hypotonia , feeding difficulties in infancy , followed by excessive appetite and obesity , alongside intellectual challenges (mental retardation), hypogonadotropic hypogonadism , aligns with Prader-Willi syndrome. (15q11 deletion)
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 625, 3058

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-year-old child, accompanied by his parents, visits your clinic for a consultation. Upon observation, it's noted that all family members share a similar short stature, which has been a characteristic trait since birth. The parents express their past experiences of discrimination due to their stature but also acknowledge a shift towards acceptance in society. They wish to prevent their child from facing similar challenges and seek your advice. After conducting a thorough examination, you identify limb shortening and disproportionate short stature in the child, indicative of an inherited defect in enchondral ossification linked to an FGFR3 mutation. You inform the parents that the condition is genetic and explain the limitations regarding its alteration. Based on the family history and clinical evaluation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteogenesis imperfecta – will result in short stature but the history will suggest the multiple fractures, blue sclera, skin and skeletal abnormalities.
• Option C and D. Both of them will result in proportionate short stature and is less common in familial cases of AD inheritance.
• Educational objective:
• Achondroplasia is the most common form of dwarfism characterized by disproportionate short stature , particularly noticeable in the limbs . It is caused by mutations in the FGFR3 gene , which leads to abnormal cartilage formation and enchondral ossification during bone development . This condition is typically inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 721 Page No 14374-79
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 721 Page No 14374-79

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother presents to the clinic with her 3-year-old son, concerned about his unusual amount of body hair, which she has not observed in her daughter. She mentions that the child's father and paternal grandfather also exhibited this trait. After evaluating both children, the physician diagnoses the son with hypertrichosis, reassuring the mother that it is not associated with congenital anomalies. Given the family history and the gender-specific occurrence of the condition, which mode of inheritance is most likely responsible for this pattern?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture14_Mue00h0.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. X-Linked Recessive – Ex: Duchenne or Becker muscular dystrophy , hemophilia , mucopolysaccharidosis type II (Hunter syndrome), Color blindness , Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, etc
• Option B. Autosomal Recessive – Ex: Tay-Sachs disease , Gaucher disease , Niemann-Pick disease , Ataxia-telangiectasia , Crigler-Najjar syndrome (CN) type I, Dubin-Johnson syndrome, Rotor syndrome, thalassemia, Sickle Cell Anemia, etc
• Option C. Mitochondrial inheritance – Ex: Leigh disease and mitochondrial encephalopathy , lactic acidosis and stroke (MELAS) like syndrome, etc
• Educational objective:
• Examples of Y linked diseases - Hypertrichosis pinnae, webbed feet, color blindness, etc
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 97, page no 2974
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 97, page no 2974

subject: Paediatrics
question: An anxious mother presents with her child who has been complaining of a toothache. After a thorough examination, you refer the child to a dentist. The dental reports indicate no abnormalities. Following the dental consultation, the mother seeks your advice to understand the difference between temporary (deciduous) and permanent teeth. She is particularly interested in knowing at what age the first permanent tooth typically appears in a child. Based on standard dental developmental milestones, how would you answer her question?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A and B. Permanent teeth will not erupt by 2 and 4 years.
• Option D . Permanent teeth will start erupt by 6 years, by the age of 8 years the permanent teeth will be already erupted.
• Educational Objective
• Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
On your first day in the pediatric department, you conduct a routine examination of a newborn in the labor ward. The baby is active and alert. An OBGy consultant comes to assess the newborn, and as she is about to leave, the infant spontaneously grasps her finger. At what age is the grasp reflex, where a baby instinctively holds onto a finger, developed?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Palmar grasp is fully develop by 32 weeks
• Option C. By 3-4 months it start to disappear
• Option D. This is the incorrect option.
• Educational Objective:
• Palmar grasp onset starts by 28 weeks
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 42.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 42.

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-month-old infant named Ramya is brought in by her mother due to concerns about delayed tooth eruption. Ramya's mother compares her to a cousin of the same age who already has several teeth, while Ramya has yet to have any teeth appear. Understanding the mother's concerns, you explain the typical timeline for tooth eruption and the age at which delayed tooth eruption is considered. At what age should the absence of any erupted teeth be a cause for considering a diagnosis of delayed eruption?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Delayed eruption of teeth
• Delayed eruption is usually considered when there are no teeth by approximately 13 months of age (mean + 3 SD). Common causes of delayed eruption of teeth include :- (MNEMONIC – FRIED CAP)
• Familial Rickets Idiopathic (Most common). Endocrine (hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism) Down syndrome Cleidocranial dysostosis Progeria Individual teeth may fail to erupt because of mechanical block-age (crowding, gum fibrosis).
• Causes of early exfoliation include:- Histiocytosis X, Leukemia. Idiopathic factors. Cyclic neutropenia. Trauma. Nutritional and metabolic disturbances, prolonged illness, and certain medications (tetracycline) commonly result in discoloration or malformations of the dental enamel.
• A discrete line of pitting on the enamel suggests a time-limited insult.
• discrete line of pitting on the enamel suggests a time-limited insult.
• Educational objective
• Delayed eruption is usually considered when no teeth have erupted by
• approximately 13 months of age . Common causes include congenital
• or genetic disorders, endocrine disorders (e.g., hypothyroidism,
• hypoparathyroidism), familial conditions, and (the most common) idiopathic
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
