A pediatrician is reviewing a 3-month-old child during a routine health maintenance visit. The child appears phenotypically normal without any apparent abnormalities. The mother expresses concern about the child's risk of developing a familial disease that is present in the father's side of the family. She provides a detail indicating the presence of the disease in previous generations. The family Pedigree is given below and the child is marked in the arrow identify the status of the child?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/18/07.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Child with disease is seen in male child of X-linked Recessive disorder, as it is a female child it will be a carrier.
• Option B. Normal child is not seen in case of X-linked Recessive disorder , males will be affected, and females will be as a carrier.
• Educational Objective:
• Pedigree symbols and abbreviations –
• Squares represent males , circles represent females and a diamond represents one whose sex is not known . Shaded symbols indicate individuals who are affected by the disease. For >2 conditions the symbols are partitioned correspondingly, each quadrant with different fillings/ patterns representing different features Half-shaded symbols represent carriers of the disease if the condition is autosomal recessive. Unshaded symbols represent individuals who are unaffected and presumably do not carry the disease allele if the condition is autosomal dominant. Symbols with a dot in the center can sometimes represent carriers for X-linked conditions, or less commonly, for autosomal recessive conditions.
• Nelson 21 st edition page 11523
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition page 11523

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 14-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric outpatient department by her mother due to concerns about her short stature. During the evaluation, the physician notes several distinctive features: a webbed neck, a low posterior hairline, and a dislocated patella. Given the clinical presentation suggestive of a genetic anomaly, karyotyping is performed, and the findings are shown in the accompanying image. Based on the history and karyotyping results, what is the most probable diagnosis, and what management options should be considered?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture3.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A and C . Klinefelter syndrome is seen in boys with karyotype 47XXY and they will have tall stature instead of short stature in turner’s syndrome.
• Option D. The diagnosis based on the history and the karyotyping findings of 45 XO is Turner syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Turner syndrome is characterized by the presence of a single X chromosome and the absence of all or part of a second sex chromosome in females , leading to various clinical manifestations, including short stature, webbed neck, low posterior hairline , and potential skeletal abnormalities such as a dislocated patella .
• The recommendation for laparoscopic gonadectomy in Turner syndrome is specific to patients who have Y chromosome material, as they are at an increased risk for gonadoblastoma, a type of tumor that can develop in the dysgenetic gonads. Gonadectomy is an important preventive measure.
explanation.ref: Essential pediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No-536-537

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother from a rural area brings her 4-day-old baby to the clinic for vaccination. As the attending physician, you notice abnormalities in the child's appearance as depicted in the provided image, prompting a thorough examination. The neonate exhibits overlapping fingers, an elongated occiput, curved feet, hypoplastic nails, and cleft lip and palate. Given these findings, you suspect a chromosomal anomaly and recommend an echocardiography, which reveals a ventricular septal defect. Based on the neonate's history, clinical presentation and echo findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture5.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Patau syndrome: Also known as trisomy 13 , this condition presents with severe intellectual disability and physical abnormalities , including polydactyly, cleft lip and palate , and microphthalmia , but the specific combination of features mentioned does not align as closely with Patau syndrome as with Edward syndrome.
• Option C. DiGeorge syndrome: This condition is characterized by congenital heart defects, specific facial features, and immune system deficiencies, resulting from a deletion on chromosome 22q11.2. The described features do not match the typical presentation of DiGeorge syndrome.
• Option D. Williams syndrome: Known for distinctive facial features , cardiovascular disease (especially supravalvular aortic stenosis), and a unique cognitive profile with strong verbal skills , Williams syndrome does not present with the combination of features described in the question.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brought her 5-year-old child with complaints of not being regular to the school as he is gets infections very often. When he attends the school the teacher always complaints about him that he isn’t studying well like his fellow classmates. As you start looking at the child, the child’s face appears to be the same as in the image. On further evaluation the child had notched ears, hypertelorism, thin upper lip, and heart murmurs. You start investigating with blood analysis. RBC and WBC counts were normal and the electrolyte level of serum calcium is found to be 6.5mg/dl. What is the most probable diagnosis based on his clinical features and examination findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture8.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Williams syndrome : Known for its "elfin" facial features , cardiovascular disease , and unique cognitive profile , Williams syndrome typically does not present with the immune deficiencies or hypocalcemia that are indicative of DiGeorge syndrome.
• Option C. Patau syndrome : Also known as trisomy 13 , this condition is associated with severe intellectual disability and a range of physical abnormalities , but the specific features and low calcium level described are more characteristic of DiGeorge syndrome.
• Option D. Prader-Willi syndrome : A genetic disorder characterized by obesity, intellectual disability , and hypogonadism , but not typically associated with the facial features, heart murmurs, or hypocalcemia described in the scenario.
• Educational objective:
• DiGeorge syndrome - Congenital heart defect , hypoplasia of parathyroid and thymus , facial and palate anomalies (22q11.2 deletion)
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 3031
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 3031

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric department by her mother due to concerns about constipation, easy fatigability, muscle soreness, and weakness. The mother also expresses frustration with her daughter's academic performance, noting that she does not study as well as her brother. The pediatrician observes that the child has a very friendly personality and a broad forehead. Initial blood tests, including a complete blood count, do not reveal any significant abnormalities. Suspecting Williams syndrome, the pediatrician decides to check the serum calcium levels. What would be the expected serum calcium level in a case of Williams syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In case of William syndrome there will be increased Serum calcium.
• William syndrome (deletion of chromosome 7q11.23)
• Round face with full cheeks, wide mouth, epicanthal fold, upturned nose, large ears, small jaw Intellectual disability Friendly personality Broad forehead
• Hypercalcemia > 11mg/dl (leads to constipation, muscle weakness, lethargy, depression etc)
• CVS: supravalvular aortic stenosis.
• Note - Serum calcium 6.5 mg/dl is more commonly seen in Digeorge syndrome
• Educational Objective:
• William syndrome – Hypercalcemia, supravalvular aortic stenosis, abnormal facies (7q11.23 deletion)
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 4242.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 4242.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 7-year-old son, William, to the pediatric outpatient department, concerned about his inability to speak and ongoing sleep disturbances. Initially deemed normal developmental variations, the family sought advice from various sources, including a monk, but saw no improvement in William's condition. An intern at a nearby primary health center noticed something amiss during a routine vaccination visit and referred the family for further evaluation. Upon thorough examination, the pediatrician observes delayed development, frequent unprovoked laughter, and an ataxic gait in William. These findings, along with the specific inheritance pattern, suggest a diagnosis of a paternal imprinting disorder. What is the most likely diagnosis given the child's clinical presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Prader-Willi syndrome : Also involves imprinting abnormalities of chromosome 15 but presents with hypotonia, feeding difficulties in infancy followed by excessive eating and obesity , and mild to moderate intellectual impairment. It results from the loss of function of genes in the paternal chromosome 15.
• Option C. DiGeorge syndrome: Caused by a deletion on chromosome 22q11.2 , it presents with congenital heart defects, immune deficiencies, and facial anomalies , not specifically related to paternal imprinting.
• Option D. Williams syndrome : Characterized by cardiovascular disease , unique cognitive profile , and distinctive facial features , is not associated with the imprinting issues.
• Educational objective:
• Overview of the common clinical and molecular findings in the 8 known imprinting disorders , including specific features, frequencies, and major chromosomal localization.
• Essential paediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No-639-641
explanation.ref: Essential paediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No-639-641

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 24-month-old child is brought in by their mother for a routine pediatric check-up. The child appears to be developing normally and has recently started to engage in scribbling activities. The mother is curious about her child's developmental milestones and asks you, the pediatrician, about the age at which the child will be able to copy the figure shown to you, which is a circle. Based on your knowledge of childhood development, at what age can a child typically copy a circle?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture2.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 1 year - The child’s pincer Grasp develops by 1 year .
• Option B. 2 years - The child starts making vertical and circular strokes by 2 years of age.
• Educational Objective:
• The fine motor milestones development includes the following,
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a pediatric evaluation, a mother presents her 5-year-old child who is notably shorter than his peers. Upon examination, you determine that the child's skeletal age is younger than his chronological age, which suggests a delay in bone maturation. However, after reviewing the child's growth records, you find that the growth velocity has remained within the normal range. With this information, you consider the probable final adult height for this child. Which of the following best describes the anticipated outcome for this child's final adult height ?
options: Less because of epiphyseal closure due to accelerated growth velocity

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Less because of small bones - This option is incorrect because short stature with normal growth velocity and a delayed skeletal age does not necessarily indicate that the child will have small bones as an adult.
• Option C. More than expected - While delayed skeletal age can indicate a potential for continued growth beyond the typical age , it does not necessarily mean the child will grow to be taller than expected.
• Option D. Less because of epiphyseal closure due to accelerated growth velocity - This is incorrect as the child has a normal growth velocity , not accelerated, and therefore epiphyseal closure is not happening earlier than expected.
• Educational Objective
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 506.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 506.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 3-year-old child, Charan, to the pediatric clinic for a routine evaluation. It is noted that Charan is the second child in the family, and the mother mentions that her first child, who is now 6 years old, has started getting permanent teeth. Considering this information, how many teeth would you expect to find in Charan during your examination?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• At the age of 3, 20 temporary or milk teeth are seen.
• Primary (temporary) dentition begins in 6 months. Secondary (permanent) dentition begins 6 years.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.

subject: Paediatrics
question: On your first day as an intern in the pediatric department, you're eager to learn and observe. As you walk through the outpatient department, you notice that the walls are filled with various IEC material including growth charts and every mother in the waiting area has a growth chart in hand for her child. This piques your interest in the growth monitoring process. Your attending physician quizzes you on the unique aspects of the WHO growth charts from 2006 to test your knowledge. Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of these WHO growth charts?
options: Breast fed infant taken as normative growth model
Includes new indicators such as skin fold thickness
Sample collected from six States in the India
Reiterates children grow similarly across the World

explanation.short: Ans. C) Sample collected from six States in the India.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, B and D are true regarding the WHO 2006 growth chart.
• A. The children included in the study were raised in environments that minimized constraints to growth such as poor nutrition and infection . In addition, their mothers followed healthy practices such as breastfeeding their children and did not smoke during and after pregnancy . The new standards make breastfeeding the biological norm and establishes the breastfed infant as the normative growth model.
• B. Includes new indicators such as skin fold thickness - This is also correct . These standards also include new growth indicators beyond height and weight that are particularly useful for monitoring the increasing epidemic of childhood obesity, such as skinfold thickness, so to provide a more comprehensive assessment of nutritional status.
• D. Reiterates that children grow similarly across the world - This is true as well. The pooled sample from the six participating countries makes it a truly international standard (in contrast to the previous international reference based on children from a single country) and reiterates the fact that child populations grow similarly across the world's major regions when their needs for health and care are met.
• Educational Objective:
• The WHO's 2006 growth charts , based on the MGRS from six countries , define optimal growth under ideal health conditions , highlight breastfeeding as the norm , and include new measures like skinfold thickness for a global growth standard.
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 14-15.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 14-15.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 36 years old pregnant female presents to the Pediatrics department for a regular visit for her 3 years old child with a trisomy which is responsible for the presenile Alzheimer’s disease. You noticed that she is pregnant again and she replied that it is her 2 nd pregnancy and she is in her 15 th week. As she has the risk of Trisomy again, you advised her to undergo a test for karyotyping. What is the test you advise her considering the gestational age?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cordocentesis is done between 18 th to 20 th weeks.
• Option C . Chorionic Villous Sampling is done between 11 th to 14 th weeks.
• Option D. Mothers urine examination does not have a significant importance whereas mothers’ blood can be used by the cell free DNA testing .
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-614
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-614

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 7-year-old child is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his recurring joint pain and backache. She describes her son's history of hypermobility and frequent dislocations, specifically mentioning his shoulders and knees. Additionally, she notes that there is a history of heart problems on the paternal side of the family. Upon examination, the pediatrician observes an eye finding consistent with the condition suspected based on the systemic features reported. What is the likely diagnosis based on these clinical features, and what protein is defective in this disorder?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture15.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) Marfan syndrome and Fibrillin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Marfan syndrome is due to defect in the fibrillin not the Collagen .
• Option B. Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and Collagen - Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a group of connective tissue disorders often related to defects in collagen ; however, the eye finding described is not typically associated with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
• Option D. Ehlers Danlos Syndrome and Fibrillin - Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is primarily associated with collagen defects, not fibrillin .
• Educational objective:
• Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder characterized by hypermobility, frequent dislocations , and cardiovascular complications , such as aortic root dilatation. The eye finding often associated with Marfan syndrome is lens subluxation . The defective protein in Marfan syndrome is fibrillin-1 , which is crucial for the integrity and function of connective tissue throughout the body.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 618-619 and 644-645.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 618-619 and 644-645.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother presents to the pediatric department with her 3-month-old infant, seeking evaluation for potential abnormalities. She reports that the child has been the subject of admiration in their community due to his fair skin, blue eyes, and blond hair, but a recent encounter with a 3 rd year MBBS student has raised concerns about his health. The students’ inquiries prompted the mother to seek a professional opinion. Upon examination, the pediatrician observes an eczematous rash on the child's skin and notes a distinct musty odor. Based on the clinical presentation and findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Albinism is characterized by a complete or partial absence of pigment in the skin, hair, and eyes due to a defect in melanin production but does not typically present with a musty odor or eczematous rash.
• Option B. Vitiligo involves the loss of skin color in patches and does not account for the musty odor or systemic symptoms seen in this patient.
• Option D. Alkaptonuria is a metabolic disorder characterized by the accumulation of homogentisic acid , leading to darkening of the urine and possibly arthritis in adulthood, but it does not explain the clinical findings in this infant.
• Educational objective:
• Phenylketonuria - Phenylalanine Hydroxylase enzyme deficiency
• Infants are normal at birth Mental retardation Blond, blue eyed, with fair skin Mental retardation Eczema Musty body odor of phenylacetic acid Vomiting is common and is often mistaken for pyloric stenosis
• Physical examination - Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes.
• Decreased phenylalanine diet Early institution is essential to prevent the development of brain damage Increase tyrosine in diet.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3131

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 6-year-old girl presents with a history of multiple craniofacial anomalies, including downward slanting eyes, and small jaw. The mother informs that the child had recurrent middle ear infections and has a history of hearing loss. Examination reveals a high-arched palate and respiratory difficulty due to upper airway obstruction. Imaging studies demonstrate hypoplasia of mandible with malocclusion and absence of the zygomatic arch. What is the most likely diagnosis based on the clinical features, radiological findings and the image given below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Down Syndrome: Characterized by intellectual disability , distinct facial features (flat facial profile, slanted eyes, small ears), and other systemic manifestations . The specific craniofacial and radiological findings described do not typically align with Down Syndrome.
• Option C. Seckel Syndrome: Known for primordial dwarfism , microcephaly , and a "bird-like" face , which does not specifically include the craniofacial anomalies or hearing issues described in the question.
• Option D. Turner Syndrome: Affects females and involves a missing or partially missing X chromosome , leading to short stature, gonadal dysgenesis , and other physical abnormalities, but not typically the craniofacial anomalies described for TCS.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 th Edition, Page No 7613

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 24-year-old mother, Mala, recently gave birth to a neonate presenting with epicanthal folds, low-set ears, a small nose, and a protruding tongue. Additionally, Brushfield spots were observed in the eyes. Echocardiography revealed endocardial cushion defects. Upon feeding, the neonate vomited milk with a bilious tinge, leading to a suspicion of duodenal atresia alongside the clinical suspicion of Down syndrome. Considering these findings, what is the most common genetic cause of this syndrome?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Maternal meiotic non- dysjunction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Germline mosaicism : This occurs when some of the body's cells have a different genetic makeup from the rest , which can lead to genetic conditions but it is only responsible for only 1% cases of Down syndrome (least common).
• Option B. Paternal meiotic nondisjunction : Paternal meiotic is not evident in case of down syndrome .
• Option D. Translocation : Down syndrome can also result from a Robertsonian translocation , where a significant portion of chromosome 21 is attached to another chromosome , typically chromosome 14. However, this accounts for least common causes for down syndrome.
• Educational Objective
• Remember - Maternal meiotic nondisjunction is the most common cause of Down Syndrome . It also aims to highlight the association between Down and various congenital anomalies , such as duodenal atresia and cardiac defects , which require prompt recognition and management in affected neonates.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a developmental assessment session, a pediatrician observes a 6-month-old infant's ability to transfer objects from one hand to the other. This developmental milestone is a complex task involving various aspects of an infant's cognitive and motor development. The pediatrician uses this observation to discuss with a medical student the significance of this milestone. What does the ability to transfer objects from one hand to the other at 6 months of age primarily imply about a child's developmental progress?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Visual motor coordination - While transferring objects does involve visual motor integration , this milestone is more directly associated with the child's ability to coordinate vision and fine motor skills , such as reaching for and grasping objects, rather than the transfer itself.
• Option B. Voluntary object release - Voluntary object release is indeed a part of the skill involved in transferring objects from one hand to the other , but it is not the primary developmental achievement indicated by this milestone.
• Educational Objective:
• Transferring objects between hands at 6 months is significant not just for the motor skill it demonstrates but also for the cognitive development it implies , mainly the infant's ability to compare different objects.
• nelson 21 st edition chapter 22, pg 1124-25.
explanation.ref: nelson 21 st edition chapter 22, pg 1124-25.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother presents her 2-year-old daughter, Poonguzhali, to the clinic, concerned about her delayed developmental milestones. Upon a thorough examination, you notice not only a delay but also a regression in previously attained milestones. Considering the child's age and the regression observed, you ponder the possible causes. Which of the following conditions is known to potentially result in the regression of developmental milestones?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rett’s syndrome - Characterized by early normal development followed by a decrease in head growth rate, loss of purposeful hand skills , and the development of stereotypic hand movements , among other features.
• Option B. Autism - Some individuals with autism can experience regression , often in language and social skills , after a period of seemingly normal development.
• Option C. Neuromuscular diseases - These disorders can lead to muscle weakness over time , which might result in the loss of motor skills that a child had previously mastered.
• Educational Objective:
• Regression of milestones –
• The hallmark of many degenerative disorders is neurological regression. Degenerative diseases of the brain generally cause regression of both motor and cognitive function (global regression). History of only loss of motor milestones — progressive neuromuscular disease — most likely cause. Loss of only language skills — autism — suspected. Regression of both language and motor milestones Rett’s syndrome. A child older than 3 or 4 years of age: Deterioration of gray matter regression with seizures. Deterioration of white matter regression with cognitive slowing and spasticity.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No- 12430.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No- 12430.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
In the pediatric clinic, a mother brings her infant for routine vaccinations. As an intern, you engage in conversation with her, and during the chat, the baby grasps a toy and brings it to their mouth, demonstrating a developmental milestone. The mother, cautious about hygiene, quickly removes the toy from the baby's grasp. This event leads you to reflect on the developmental milestones pertaining to fine motor skills. At what age does an infant typically develop a mature pincer grasp, which allows them to pick up small objects between the thumb and forefinger?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. At 7 months of age : baby develop unidextrous approach by 6 months
• Option A. At 5-6 months of age mouthing develops
• Option C. At 9 months of age , immature pincer grasp develops.
• Educational Objective
• Birth - Grasp reflex present 3 months - Grasp reflex disappears 4 months - Bidextrous approach 6 months - Unidextrous approach 9 months – Immature pincer grasp 12 — 13 months – Mature Pincer Grasp, Casting appear . 15 months - Feeds himself with cup, slight spillage 18 months - Use of Spoon, slight spillage Scribbles 2 years - Vertical stroke, Turn one page of book at a time 3, 4, 5 years - Universal milestones: -
• 3 years — draws circle
• 4 years — draws ‘+’ and square/rectangle
• 5 years — draws ‘x’ and triangle
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question: As part of a new initiative, you are tasked with training Anganwadi workers on accurately measuring children's height and weight. During the session, you discuss various aspects of a child's growth, including increments in weight and height. One of the workers asks about specific growth milestones. You plan to inform them about the age at which a newborn's height is typically expected to double from their height at birth. At what age does this doubling in height usually occur?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The height of the newborn is doubled by 4 years.
• The average birth height is 50 cm . The height gain in the first year is around 25 cm (50% of birth height). The height of a child becomes double the birth height at around 4 years and becomes triple by 12 years of age . After the age of 4 years , a child grows at a rate of 6 cm/year .
• Educational objective:
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 33.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 33.

subject: Paediatrics
question: In the outpatient department, a mother presents her 15-month-old child, Mayura, with concerns about the child's lack of teeth. Upon examination, you observe that Mayura has exhibited normal growth patterns, but her oral cavity shows no signs of tooth eruption. Considering the child's age and lack of dental development, you consider the potential causes for this delayed eruption of teeth. What is the most common cause of delayed tooth eruption in children?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Idiopathic is the most common cause of delayed eruption of teeth .
• Delayed eruption is usually considered when there are no teeth by approximately 13 months of age (mean + 3 SD). Common causes of delayed eruption of teeth include: Idiopathic (Most common). Hypothyroidism (Option B) Hypoparathyroidism (Option C) Familial
• (Syphilis (Option D) can lead to the formation of prenatal teeth not the delayed eruption of teeth).
• Individual teeth may fail to erupt because of mechanical block-age (crowding, gum fibrosis).
• Educational objective:
• Delayed eruption is usually considered when no teeth have erupted by approximately 13 months of age . Common causes include congenital or genetic disorders , endocrine disorders (e.g., hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism), familial conditions , and (the most common) idiopathic conditions.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.
• Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No- From 1198.

subject: Paediatrics
question: While shadowing a pediatric consultant in the outpatient department (OPD), you observe him palpating the head of a young child during the examination. After the clinic session, you inquire with a junior resident (JR) about the examination technique. The JR explains that the consultant was assessing the closure of the child's fontanelles. Prompted by this discussion, the JR quizzes you on your knowledge of fontanelle closure. Which fontanelle is typically the last to close in a child ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sphenoidal fontanelle closes by 6 months after birth .
• Option C. Mastoid fontanelle closes by 6-18 months after birth .
• Option D. Posterior fontanelle closes by 2-3 months after birth .
• Educational objective:
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 03
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 03

subject: Paediatrics
question: An anxious mother presents to the pediatric clinic with her 6-month-old son, concerned because he is not speaking monosyllables like his cousin of the same age. The pediatrician listens to the mother's concerns and then proceeds to educate her about the normal developmental milestones and red flag signs that would warrant further evaluation. Which of the following developmental milestones, if not achieved by the specified age, should be considered a red flag and may indicate a need for further developmental assessment?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Social smile at 6 months
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pincer grasp at 9 months - The pincer grasp usually develops between 9 to 10 months of age . Not achieving this milestone right at 9 months is not necessarily a red flag, as there may be slight variations in development.
• Option B. Walking at 12 months - It is common for children to start walking independently between 9 to 15 months of age . Not walking precisely at 12 months is not immediately concerning and often does not indicate a developmental problem.
• Option C. Babbling at 6 months - Babbling typically starts between 4 to 6 months of age . If a child is not babbling by 6 months, it can be a concern, but it is not as significant a red flag.
• Educational Objective:
• Important red flag signs in development:
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 43-51.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her 7-months-old child to the clinic with a history of developmental delays, Failure to thrive, and anomaly shown in the image. On further evaluation he has a small head circumference, low-set ears, small mouth and clenched hands with overlapping fingers. Additional findings of heart murmur and scoliosis were also noted. The physician suspects it to be case of Trisomy based on the clinical findings, What is the Karyotype finding is seen in this case?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/15/picture17.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Trisomy 21 (Down Syndrome) - This condition typically presents with distinct facial features , a single palmar crease , hypotonia , and intellectual disability , which do not completely match the features described in the question.
• Option C. Trisomy 18 (Patau Syndrome) - This is an incorrect combination of the syndrome's name and trisomy number ; Trisomy 18 is Edward Syndrome, not Patau Syndrome.
• Option D. Trisomy 13 (Patau Syndrome) - While Trisomy 13, or Patau Syndrome , also presents with severe congenital anomalies , the specific features mentioned in the scenario are more characteristic of Trisomy 18.
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter – 98 Page No 3024

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A working mother brings her 3-year-old child to the pediatrician’s office for a routine check-up. On evaluation child is small for her age with a beak-like nose, narrow face, and widely spaced eyes as shown in the image. The mother reports that the child has difficulty feeding due to a small jaw and mouth, and has been slow to reach developmental milestones. The pediatrician diagnoses this as a case of Seckel syndrome. All of the following are features of Seckel syndrome, except?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture2.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, C and D are the features of Seckel syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Seckel (SCKL) syndrome , sometimes called “bird-headed dwarfism,” is an autosomal recessive developmental disorder characterized by marked growth failure and mental deficiency, microcephaly , a hypoplastic face with a prominent nose , and low-set and/or malformed ears. Approximately 25% of patients have aplastic anemia or malignancies.
• Note - There is broad genetic heterogeneity comprising at least 8 classifiable types: SCKL1, ATR mutation; SCKL2, RBBP8 mutation; SCKL3, maps to 14q21-q22; SCKL4, CENPJ mutation; SCKL5, CEP152 mutation; SCKL6, CEP63 mutation; SCKL7, NIN mutation; and SCKL8, ATRIP mutation.
• Nelson 21 th Edition, Page No 10133
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 th Edition, Page No 10133

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 2-year-old child named Mia was brought to the Pediatrician by her mother, who is concerned about her daughter's unusual behavior and developmental patterns. The mother reports that Mia frequently plays with cats and has a cry that is reminiscent of a cat's meow. Upon examination, the Pediatrician observes several notable features: a high-pitched, cat-like cry, developmental delays, hyperactivity, aggression, short attention span, and some distinct facial characteristics. Based on these findings, the Pediatrician suspects Cri du Chat syndrome. Considering the typical manifestations of Cri du Chat syndrome, which of the following additional findings is most likely to be observed in Mia?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/16/picture13_HbjKDvS.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Microcephaly is seen in case of Cri du Chat syndrome.
• (Microcephaly is defined as an occipitofrontal circumference more than 3 standard deviations (SD) below the mean for given age, sex and gestation.)
• Cri du Chat syndrome is seen due to deletion of chromosome 5p
• High-pitched cry : cry that sounds like a cat's meow, which is due to abnormalities in the larynx and nervous system. Microcephaly Developmental delays: Distinctive facial features: variety of facial abnormalities, such as a small head, widely-spaced eyes, a small jaw, and low-set ears. Intellectual disability: Speech and language delay: Behaviour problems: exhibit hyperactivity, aggression, or a short attention span. They may also have social and emotional difficulties.
• Educational Objective:
• Syndromic causes of Microcephaly
• Essential paediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No- 26; Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 3034.
explanation.ref: Essential paediatrics by OP Ghai 9 Th Edition – Page No- 26; Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No 3034.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old boy in India is brought to the hospital with a history of recurrent bacterial infections, including otitis media, sinusitis, and pneumonia. He has no significant family history of immunodeficiency disorders. Physical examination is unremarkable except for mild otitis media. Laboratory tests reveal markedly low levels of IgG, IgA, and IgM. Which condition is most likely to be diagnosed based on the clinical features and investigation findings in this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) : CVID can present with similar symptoms but usually has a later onset , and patients may have some immunoglobulins present, albeit at reduced levels.
• Option B. Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) : SCID is characterized by a profound deficiency of T- and B-cell function , leading to severe infections early in life , often within the first few months. The child's age and the type of infections described make SCID less likely.
• Option D. Hyper-IgM Syndrome : This condition involves normal or elevated levels of IgM with low levels of IgG, IgA, and IgE , which does not match the laboratory findings in this case.
• Educational Objective
• X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA), a primary immunodeficiency disorder , should be suspected in male children presenting with recurrent bacterial infections , particularly otitis media , sinusitis , and pneumonia , combined with significantly low levels of immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, and IgM).
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition. page 4627

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who is concerned about the child's history of recurrent, severe infections including pneumonia, oral thrush, and a past episode of sepsis. Laboratory tests reveal markedly low levels of T cells and absent or low B cells. Which condition is the most likely diagnosis for this infant based on the clinical history and laboratory findings?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) : CVID typically presents later in childhood or adolescence with hypogammaglobulinemia and normal T-cell numbers.
• C. X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA) : XLA typically presents with low or absent B cells and normal T-cell counts , not the T-cell deficiency observed here.
• Option D. Hyper-IgM syndrome : Characterized by high levels of IgM with low levels of IgG, IgA, and IgE , and typically normal T-cell counts.
• Educational Objective
• SCID is characterized by a lack of T cells, B cells , and natural killer cells , leading to severe, life-threatening infections in infancy. Early diagnosis is crucial for the management and survival of these infants , with interventions such as bone marrow transplant offering potentially curative treatment .
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1022-1024.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 4-year-old boy Raju with complaints of recurrent bacterial infections, including otitis media and pneumonia. Laboratory evaluations show markedly low levels of immunoglobulins across all classes and an absence of mature B cells. Given Raju's clinical presentation and lab results, which condition is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) : This condition usually presents later in childhood or in adulthood and involves low levels of immunoglobulins but not a complete absence of B cells.
• Option B. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) : While SCID also leads to recurrent infections and can involve low levels of B cells, it is more severe and involves T cells as well.
• Option D. Hyper-IgM syndrome : This condition involves high levels of IgM with low levels of other immunoglobulins , which is not consistent with Raju's lab findings.
• Educational Objective
• X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA) is a rare primary immunodeficiency disorder characterized by a lack of mature B cells and very low levels of immunoglobulins . XLA typically presents in early childhood with recurrent bacterial infections , such as otitis media and pneumonia.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No- 4629.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 6-week-old son to the clinic, concerned that his umbilical cord has not yet detached. The infant, born at term, appears active and well, with no signs of illness or fever. Examination reveals the intact umbilical cord and a shallow, 0.5 cm ulceration at the occiput without discharge or erythema, which has been slowly healing since birth. Given this presentation, which finding is most likely associated with the child's condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Defective humoral response : This would suggest a problem with B cells or antibodies , which is not directly indicated by the delayed umbilical cord detachment or the slow-healing ulcer.
• Option B. Functional leukocyte adherence glycoproteins : This option is contrary to what is expected in LAD , where these glycoproteins are dysfunctional.
• Option C. Normal wound healing : The slow-healing ulcer at the occiput contradicts normal wound healing processes , suggesting an underlying issue like LAD.
• Educational Objective
• Leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD).
• LAD is an inheritable disorder of leukocyte chemotaxis and adherence characterized by recurring sinopulmonary , oropharyngeal , and cutaneous infections with delayed wound healing. Neutrophilia is common with WBC counts of typically more than 50,000 cells/mm 3 . Severe, life-threatening infection is possible with Staphylococcus species, Enterobacteriaceae, and Candida species. Good skin and oral hygiene are important; broad-spectrum antimicrobials and surgical debridement are early considerations with infection.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 999-1001.

subject: Paediatrics
question: You are the resident on duty when a term, 2-hour-old newborn is brought in with temperature instability, feeding difficulties, and a recent seizure. The newborn has distinctive facial features, including wide-set eyes, a prominent nose, a small mandible, and a cleft palate. Auscultation reveals a holosystolic murmur. Post-stabilization, lab tests show significant hypocalcemia, and a chest X-ray indicates a boot-shaped heart with an absent thymus. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ataxia–telangiectasia : This condition is associated with progressive cerebellar ataxia , ocular apraxia , telangiectasia , and immunodeficiency but not typically presenting with the congenital heart disease or facial anomalies seen here.
• Option C. Hyper-IgE syndrome : Characterized by high serum IgE levels , recurrent skin and lung infections , and connective tissue abnormalities. It does not typically present with cardiac anomalies or absent thymus.
• Option D. SCID : A group of disorders characterized by profound immune deficiency leading to severe infections early in life , but without the cardiac anomalies or specific facial features associated with DiGeorge syndrome.
• Educational Objective
explanation.ref: Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 180

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 3-year-old son Raja to the clinic, concerned about his recurrent infections, eczema, and episodes of thrombocytopenia. Physical examination reveals small, circular, scaly patches on the skin. Genetic testing confirms a mutation in the WASP gene. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Atopic dermatitis : While eczema is common in atopic dermatitis , it does not typically present with recurrent infections or thrombocytopenia associated with WASP gene mutations.
• Option B. Pemphigus vulgaris : This is an autoimmune blistering disorder that does not typically present with recurrent infections or thrombocytopenia and has no connection to WASP gene mutations.
• Option C. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia : This type of leukemia occurs mostly in adults and is characterized by the accumulation of functionally incompetent lymphocytes , not typically associated with WASP gene mutations or the triad of symptoms seen in WAS.
• Educational Objective
• WAS is a rare X-linked recessive immunodeficiency disorder characterized by eczema , recurrent infections , and thrombocytopenia. It is associated with WASP gene mutation.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition. Page No-1027.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Which of the following children with Kawasaki Disease is at greatest risk for coronary artery disease?
options: A 3-year-old boy with 4 days of high fever, sterile pyuria, a truncal rash, and strawberry tongue
A 6-month-old boy with 11 days of high fever and a small pericardial effusion on initial echocardiogram
A 5-year-old girl with 6 days of high fever and an initial ESR of 80 mm/h.
A 1-year-old boy with 5 days of high fever, a maculopapular rash and mildly elevated ALT levels.

explanation.short: Ans. B. A 6-month-old boy with 11 days of high fever and a small pericardial effusion on initial echocardiogram.
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Risk factors for development of coronary aneurysms include male gender , fever for more than 10 days , age younger than 12 months , low serum albumin or hemoglobin level , early cardiac findings (eg, mitral regurgitation or pericardial effusion), and thrombocytopenia.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 8 th Edition, 631-632

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother is concerned about her child who has been experiencing stomach ache, joint pain, and a skin rash. Upon examination, the child exhibits joint tenderness and is covered in purpura, yet the platelet count is normal and there is evidence of hematuria. The clinical appearance of the child is given below. What is the most likely diagnosis based on the clinical feature and the image given?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture20.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura : Typically presents with a low platelet count , which is not seen in this case.
• Option C. IgA Nephropathy : While it may cause hematuria, it does not usually present with purpura or joint pain.
• Option D. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) : Characterized by thrombocytopenia , microangiopathic hemolytic anemia , renal failure , fever , and neurological symptoms , and the platelet count would be low, unlike this case.
• Educational Objective
• The clinical presentation of Henoch-Schönlein purpura , is a systemic vasculitis characterized by purpura , abdominal pain , arthralgia , and renal involvement , in the presence of normal platelet count.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition. Page No-1216-1217

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 17-month-old child with a history of coronary artery abnormalities due to Kawasaki Disease is on long-term aspirin therapy. As part of his immunization schedule for school, which additional vaccine should he receive specifically because of his condition and ongoing treatment?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Pneumococcal vaccine : While important in the routine vaccination schedule , it is not specifically indicated due to aspirin therapy.
• Option C. Meningococcal vaccine : This vaccine is recommended for children in certain age groups or with specific health conditions but is not specifically related to aspirin therapy.
• Option D. Oral polio vaccine : The oral polio vaccine is no longer recommended in the United States due to the risk of vaccine-derived poliovirus; the inactivated polio vaccine (IPV) is used instead. Moreover, it is not specifically indicated due to aspirin therapy.
• Educational Objective
• Reye’s syndrome is a rare but serious disease that causes swelling in the liver and brain . It can affect people of any age , but it is most often seen in children and teenagers recovering from a virus such as the flu or chickenpox.
• Studies have found that the main risk factor for Reye’s syndrome is taking aspirin or other related drugs, called salicylates.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 8 th Edition, 631-632

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 4-year-old boy presents with a high-grade fever persisting for over 6 days, bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, a diffuse macular rash on the trunk and extremities, and mucosal changes, including swelling and redness of the hands and feet. Considering these clinical findings, what is a common complication of the suspected disease in this child?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture21_PiGXUly.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Aortic stenosis : This condition involves narrowing of the aortic valve , which is not a typical complication of Kawasaki Disease.
• Option B. Mitral regurgitation : While valvular issues can occur in the context of vasculitis , mitral regurgitation is not a common complication of Kawasaki Disease.
• Option D. Ventricular septal defect : This is a congenital heart defect rather than a complication of Kawasaki Disease.
• Educational Objective
• An acute febrile illness that primarily affects young children . Clinical features include a high-grade fever that has persisted for more than 5 days , bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, a diffuse macular rash on the trunk and extremities , and changes in the lips and oral cavity, including cracked lips and a strawberry tongue. Swelling and redness of the hands and feet, known as "gloves and socks" syndrome, is also a common feature of Kawasaki disease. The most serious complication of Kawasaki disease is the development of coronary artery aneurysms, which can lead to myocardial infarction, heart failure, and sudden death.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No. 5370.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a health camp in a rural area, a paediatrician observes a 5-year-old child with unusual growth patterns. The child, accompanied by his parents, exhibits rapid height increase, thickened skin, and distinct facial features including a pronounced jaw, enlarged tongue, and broad nose. Concerned about the possibility of a hormonal imbalance, specifically gigantism due to excess growth hormone (GH) secretion by a pituitary tumor, the paediatrician decides to initiate a screening process. Which of the following tests should be ordered first to confirm the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. GH levels : GH levels fluctuate significantly throughout the day , making them less reliable for initial screening . GH levels might still be necessary for diagnosis but after initial screening with IGF1 levels. GH levels will be increased in cases of both gigantism and Laron dwarfism.
• Option B. Cortisol levels : Cortisol levels would be more indicative of adrenal function and could be relevant in conditions like Cushing's syndrome, not gigantism.
• Option D. Prolactin levels : Elevated prolactin levels could indicate a prolactinoma , another type of pituitary tumor , but it's not directly related to the symptoms of gigantism.
• Educational Objective:
• The appropriate initial screening test for suspected gigantism in children is Serum IGF-1 levels (Reliable indicator of sustained GH excess, making it the test of choice)
explanation.ref: Ghai Essential Paediatrics 8 th Edition, Page No-513.

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-year-old child from a rural village, recently involved in a road traffic accident, presents with symptoms of increased thirst and urination. Five days post-accident, the child's blood pressure is notably low at 90/50 mmHg, and a random blood sugar test reveals a level of 60 mg/dl. A neurology consult suggests the possibility of panhypopituitarism. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Growth Hormone : While important in panhypopituitarism, it is not the first-line treatment in acute settings due to its lesser role in immediate survival.
• Option B. Thyroid Hormone : Thyroid hormone replacement is crucial in panhypopituitarism but secondary to adrenal replacement therapy due to the acute risk of adrenal crisis.
• Option D. Vasopressin : Used to treat diabetes insipidus , which can be a component of panhypopituitarism. However, the immediate risk of adrenal crisis makes hydrocortisone more critical.
• Educational Objective
• For the treatment of panhypopituitarism , Hydrocortisone is the first hormone to be replaced followed by Growth hormone , thyroid hormone , ADH and gonadotropins depending on their levels.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition Page No-11274.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-month-old infant is brought in with constipation and failure to thrive. Further examination reveals an umbilical hernia, decreased muscle tone, and short stature. The mother mentions that she has been taking regular medication in the morning for an endemic illness. What is the most appropriate next step in managing this infant's condition?
options: Ask mother not to take those morning tablets
Do a TSH and T4 assay in infant.
Do a GH assay in infant.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Since it’s a case of congenital hypothyroidism we should treat the infant not to Reassure.
• Option B . Since mother is also suffering from the endemic hypothyroidism, we shouldn’t ask her to stop the tablets.
• Option D . the cause of the short stature is hypothyroidism not the GH deficiency, there is no need of GH assay.
• Educational Objective
• The signs of congenital hypothyroidism in infants , includes constipation , failure to thrive , umbilical hernia , decreased muscle tone , and short stature , and to understand that the first step in evaluation is to check thyroid function tests , specifically TSH and T4 levels . This approach helps in early diagnosis and treatment, which is crucial for normal development.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question: In the pediatric ward, a 7-month-old baby has been diagnosed with congenital hypothyroidism. As part of an educational exercise, you're discussing the case with a junior colleague, highlighting the clinical features of the condition. To test their understanding, you ask which of the following features is not typically associated with congenital hypothyroidism. How should your colleague respond?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Failure to thrive : This is a common feature in congenital hypothyroidism due to the lack of thyroid hormones , which are crucial for growth and development.
• Option B. Delayed development of milestones : Hypothyroidism can significantly impact neurological development , leading to Mental retardation and delays in achieving other age-appropriate milestones .
• Option C. Constipation : Reduced levels of thyroid hormones can decrease gastrointestinal motility , leading to constipation.
• Educational Objective
• Microglossia , the condition of having an unusually small tongue , is not associated with congenital hypothyroidism . In contrast, congenital hypothyroidism is more commonly linked with macroglossia , where the tongue is enlarged , which can lead to feeding and breathing difficulties , as well as an altered facial appearance .
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the pediatrician by her concerned parents due to the absence of menstruation and lack of secondary sexual characteristics development. Upon further evaluation, the girl also reports experiencing difficulties with her sense of smell. Laboratory tests indicate low levels of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). Based on this clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Mullerian agenesis : Typically presents with primary amenorrhea in females with fully developed secondary sexual characteristics, without affecting the sense of smell or LH/FSH levels.
• Option B. Androgen Insensitivity syndrome : Presents with female external genitalia in a genetically male individual (XY), often with normal breast development but no menstrual cycle ; not associated with anosmia.
• Option D. Klinefelter syndrome : A chromosomal disorder in males (47,XXY) that can lead to hypogonadism and reduced secondary sexual characteristics, but not typically associated with anosmia.
• Educational Objective
• Kallmann syndrome features -
• XLR Delayed puberty because of Hypogonadotropic Hypogonadism Anosmia Decreased FSH and LH levels due to failure of GnRH production by hypothalamus.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 15-month-old child is brought to the emergency department at 6 AM by his mother due to seizures. After the seizure is controlled, the pediatrician's examination reveals deaf mutism and severe intellectual disability in the child. Additionally, a midline neck swelling is noted. During the conversation, the mother recalls that she forgot to take her morning medication for a disease that is common in the area. Based on the child's symptoms and the regional prevalence of a specific condition, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Thyroid dysgenesis : This refers to a congenital malformation or absence of the thyroid gland , leading to hypothyroidism, but it does not typically present with seizures or is associated with maternal medication.
• Option B. Pendred syndrome : This genetic disorder is characterized by sensorineural hearing loss and thyroid goiter , but the link to maternal medication and the acute presentation with seizures are less typical.
• Option C. Maternal autoantibodies : While maternal autoantibodies can lead to transient hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism in the newborn , the severe symptoms and the reference to a common regional disease suggest endemic cretinism is more likely.
• Educational Objective
• Cretinism is a condition of severely stunted physical and mental growth due to untreated congenital deficiency of thyroid hormones (congenital hypothyroidism).
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 15-year-old male visits the pediatric department with symptoms of weight gain, fatigue, increased sensitivity to cold, dry skin, and facial puffiness, leading to a diagnosis of hypothyroidism. During the consultation, the child exhibits difficulties in understanding, prompting an ENT consultation. On Pure Tone Audiometry the following Graph is obtained and there was a mutation in Cl-Iodine transporter. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture11.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hashimoto's thyroiditis : An autoimmune disorder leading to hypothyroidism, but not typically associated with hearing loss or mutations in the Cl-Iodine transporter.
• Option B. Thyroid dyshormonogenesis : Refers to a group of disorders causing hypothyroidism due to enzymatic defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, but not usually linked to hearing loss.
• Option D. Graves' disease : An autoimmune disorder characterized by hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism, and not associated with hearing loss.
• Educational Objective
• Pendred Syndrome is due to mutation in the PDS gene on 7 th chromosome which codes for Chloride/Iodide transporter. The features of Pendred syndrome are Sensory Neural Hearing Loss ( SNHL ) along with goitre and hypothyroidism.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423
• ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 9-year-old girl with chronic kidney disease presents to the pediatric department complaining of bone pain. Her lab results show a serum calcium level of 9.0 mg/dl, phosphate 7.5 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 860 pg/ml, and PTH 760 pg/ml (normal range 35-70 pg/ml), with low vitamin D metabolites. An X-ray of her hand, which was taken, reveals changes consistent with her lab findings and clinical symptoms. Based on the clinical features and X-ray findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture16.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hypoparathyroidism : Characterized by low PTH levels , which would result in hypocalcemia , not the bone changes seen on X-ray in this patient.
• Option B. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism : This typically occurs in patients with long-standing secondary hyperparathyroidism who develop autonomous parathyroid function, and it is less likely in a child of this age with CKD.
• Option D. Primary hyperparathyroidism : Caused by a disorder within the parathyroid glands themselves , leading to hypercalcemia , which is not consistent with the patient's normal calcium level.
• Educational Objective
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition Chapter 529 Page No- 11524-11525.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 16-year-old boy visits the pediatric department with concerns about being unable to gain weight despite normal eating habits. He also reports palpitations, diarrhea, fine tremors, and exophthalmos. Laboratory tests show increased levels of T3 and T4, decreased TSH, and positive TSH receptor stimulating antibodies, leading to a diagnosis of Graves' disease. What is the underlying pathology responsible for his exophthalmos?
options: Accumulation of GAG in muscles of eye.
Accumulation of T3 and T4 in muscles of eye.

explanation.short: Ans. C. Accumulation of GAG in muscles of eye
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Metaplasia of muscles of the eye : Metaplasia refers to the transformation of one differentiated cell type to another , which is not the process causing exophthalmos in Graves' disease.
• Option B. Hyperplasia of muscles of the eye : While there is enlargement of the eye muscles in Graves' disease , it is due to inflammatory infiltration and GAG accumulation, not true hyperplasia.
• Option D. Accumulation of T3 and T4 in muscles of the eye : The hormones T3 and T4 themselves do not accumulate in the eye muscles to cause exophthalmos.
• Educational Objective
• Clinical features of Graves’ disease –
• Anxiety and irritability A fine tremor of the hands or fingers. Heat sensitivity and an increase in perspiration or warm, moist skin Weight loss, despite normal eating habits. Enlargement of the thyroid gland (goitre) Change in menstrual cycles. Erectile dysfunction or reduced libido Frequent bowel movements. Graves' ophthalmopathy Fatigue Graves' dermopathy Palpitations Sleep disturbance.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 583 Page No- From 11440.
• ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 583 Page No- From 11440.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 15-year-old girl is brought in by her mother due to a month-long history of frequent urination without pain and a recent itchy rash under both breasts. Over the past year, she has been gaining weight and often feels tired. Her physical examination shows she has a temperature within normal limits, her weight is above the 99th percentile for her age, and she has an erythematous, macular rash with satellite lesions under both breasts. A urinalysis reveals significant glycosuria without any signs of infection. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Fanconi syndrome : A rare disorder affecting kidney function leading to glucose , amino acids , and phosphate in the urine , but it does not explain the rash or the overall clinical presentation.
• Option C. Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) : While HIV can cause a variety of symptoms, the specific combination of glycosuria , weight changes , and rash is not typical , and there is no mention of risk factors or immunodeficiency.
• Option D. Occult malignancy : While malignancies can cause unexplained weight changes and fatigue , the specific findings of glycosuria and the described rash are not typically associated with hidden cancers.
• Educational Objective
• The clinical presentation of diabetes mellitus includes symptoms like unexplained weight changes , fatigue , frequent urination , and predisposition to infections like candidiasis . Glycosuria in the absence of urinary infection symptoms further supports the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition. page 11814

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 6-month-old infant named Hari has been experiencing a high fever for the past three days. On examination, he presents with a maculopapular diaper rash and swollen red lips. Laboratory tests reveal mild normocytic anemia and a WBC count of 15,000/mm^3 with neutrophil predominance. His urinalysis is normal, but cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows pleocytosis with no bacteria on Gram stain. Despite 24 hours of ceftriaxone treatment, the fever persists, and he has developed foot edema. What should be the next step in managing this patient?
options: Continuing current management and following the culture results.
Addition of vancomycin to the antibiotic regimen
Paediatric cardiology consultation; beginning of IVIG infusions and oral high-dose aspirin

explanation.short: Ans. D. Paediatric cardiology consultation; beginning of IVIG infusions and oral high-dose aspirin.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Nystatin for the diaper rash : While nystatin is an antifungal medication used for candidal diaper rashes , the presence of a maculopapular rash rather than a candidal rash and systemic symptoms suggests a different underlying issue.
• Option B. Continuing current management and following the culture results : Continuing only with antibiotic therapy would not address the underlying vasculitis or prevent coronary complications if the diagnosis is Kawasaki disease.
• Option C. Addition of vancomycin to the antibiotic regimen : While vancomycin may be added to cover resistant bacteria , the clinical presentation suggests a non-bacterial cause that would not respond to antibiotics.
• Educational Objective
• Kawasaki disease (also known as mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome) is a syndrome of unknown cause that results in a fever and mainly affects children under 5 years of age . It is a form of vasculitis , where medium-sized blood vessels become inflamed throughout the body. The fever typically lasts for more than five days and is not affected by usual medications.
• Other common symptoms include large lymph nodes in the neck , a rash in the genital area , lips , palms , or soles of the feet , and red eyes . Within three weeks of the onset , the skin from the hands and feet may peel , after which recovery typically occurs. The disease is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries, which include the formation of coronary artery aneurysms and myocarditis.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition. Page No-1216-1217

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 14-year-old boy from a small town presents at a Medical College with recurrent episodes of severe headaches, heart palpitations, excessive sweating, and dizziness over the past few months. His family history is negative for genetic disorders. Elevated levels of fractionated metanephrines in a 24-hour urine test suggest a diagnosis of pheochromocytoma. Considering possible syndromic associations with pheochromocytoma, which of the following is least likely to be related?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. MEN2A : This syndrome is known to be associated with pheochromocytoma , medullary thyroid carcinoma , and hyperparathyroidism, making it a likely association.
• Option C. MEN2B : Similar to MEN2A, MEN2B is associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma , pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, and a marfanoid habitus, indicating a potential association.
• Option D. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome : This genetic disorder can lead to a variety of tumors and cysts , including pheochromocytomas, in different organs, making it related to the boy's condition.
• Educational Objective
• MEN1 is the least likely syndrome to be associated with pheochromocytoma, in contrast to MEN2A , MEN2B, and Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome , which are known to have such associations.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition Page No-11506.
• ; Nelson 21 st Edition Page No-11506.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother, residing in a region where hypothyroidism is not endemic, brings her child to the clinic due to failure to thrive and delayed developmental milestones. The pediatric examination notes the child's hoarse cry, mottled skin, and distinct facial and abdominal features. Considering these clinical presentations, what is the most common cause of the child's condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture2.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Thyroid dyshormonogenesis is associated with positive family history in Congenital hypothyroidism cases.
• Option C. Hashimoto's thyroiditis : This is an autoimmune condition leading to hypothyroidism, more common in older children and adults , and less likely in very young children presenting with congenital features.
• Option D. Graves' disease : Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism in adolescents and its symptoms would contrast with those described.
• Educational Objective
• Thyroid dysgenesis is the most common cause of congenital hypothyroidism , accounting for approximately 85% of cases . It includes a spectrum of developmental abnormalities such as ectopic thyroid tissue , hypoplastic thyroid gland , or complete absence of the gland . These developmental issues lead to insufficient thyroid hormone production, which is critical for growth and development, thus explaining the child's symptoms.
• Ghai Essential Pediatrics 8 th Edition, Page No-516-519.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 12-month-old son to the pediatric department due to concerns about delayed dentition and a doll-like facial appearance. Despite appearing overweight, the child's weight-for-height ratio is normal, and he exhibits proportionate short stature. He was of average size at birth. Investigations reveal decreased levels of Insulin-like Growth Factor 1 (IGF-1) and Growth Hormone (GH), with GH levels below 8 ng/ml. Based on the clinical presentation and investigation results, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what would be the appropriate treatment? *rhGH- High purity recombinant human growth hormone.

explanation.short: Ans. A. Congenital GH deficiency, rhGH (S/c)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Congenital GH deficiency, rhGH (I/m) : While intramuscular (I/m) administration is possible, subcutaneous (S/c) administration of rhGH is preferred due to better absorption and patient comfort .
• Option C. & Option D. Acquired GH deficiency : Given the child's age and symptoms manifesting early in life , a congenital rather than acquired deficiency is more likely. Acquired GH deficiency usually occurs later in life due to injury , tumor , or radiation therapy affecting the pituitary gland .
• Educational Objective
• Delayed dentition , doll-like facies , proportionate short stature , normal weight for height , along with decreased IGF-1 and low GH levels , strongly suggests congenital growth hormone deficiency . This condition is present from birth and can lead to various growth delays without timely intervention. The recommended treatment is recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH) therapy, typically administered via subcutaneous (S/c) injections, which is the most effective and commonly used route for GH therapy.
• Ref ; Ghai Essential Paediatrics 10 th Edition

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother brings her 24-month-old child to your clinic, worried about his shorter stature compared to his cousin of the same age. Upon examination, you note delayed tooth eruption, though the child's cognitive development appears normal, with no history of trauma. Biochemical tests reveal elevated GH levels but decreased IGF-1. What is the likely underlying cause of this child's short stature given the abnormal GH and IGF-1 levels, and what would be the appropriate treatment?

explanation.short: Ans. B. Mutation in GH receptor, Mecasermine.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Craniopharyngioma : A brain tumor that can impact hormone production including GH ; however, it typically results in GH deficiency , not elevated GH levels.
• Option C. Mutation in GH receptor, rhGH : rhGH would not be effective in this case due to the GH receptor mutation , making the GH unable to exert its effect.
• Option D. Craniopharyngioma, rhGH : Again, as craniopharyngioma typically causes GH deficiency , rhGH could be considered , but the elevated GH levels in this patient suggest a different pathophysiology.
• Educational Objective
• Laron Syndrome or GH insensitivity syndrome, characterized by mutations in the GH receptor that lead to an inability to utilize GH properly , resulting in high circulating levels of GH but low levels of IGF-1 , the latter being crucial for growth. The appropriate treatment for Laron Syndrome is mecasermin , which is recombinant human IGF-1 , bypassing the need for GH receptor activation.
• Ref ; nelson 21 st edition page 11,290
• ; nelson 21 st edition page 11,290

subject: Paediatrics
question: While on pediatric duty, you encounter a friend from the gynecology department in the cafeteria. She mentions that her Senior Resident has tasked her with preparing a presentation on delayed puberty and inquires about its definitions. Which of the following statements regarding delayed puberty should you advise her to exclude from her presentation?
options: Lack of secondary sexual characters in female by the age of 13 years
Absence of menarche in females by the age of 16 years.
Lack of pubertal changes in males by the age of 14 years.
Lack of testosterone in males by the age of 17 years.

explanation.short: Ans. D. Lack of testosterone in males by the age of 17 years.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lack of secondary sexual characteristics in females by the age of 13 years : This is considered a threshold for investigating delayed puberty in girls , as most develop breast buds by this age.
• Option B. Absence of menarche in females by the age of 16 years : This is also a standard benchmark for considering delayed puberty in females , as most have their first menstrual period by this age.
• Option C. Lack of pubertal changes in males by the age of 14 years : This is a common criterion for evaluating delayed puberty in boys , as testicular enlargement typically begins by this age.
• Educational Objective
• Delayed puberty is defined as
• Lack of secondary sexual characters in female by the age of 13 years , Absence of menarche in females by the age of 16 years. Absence of menarche in females within 5 years of onset of puberty . Lack of pubertal changes in males by the age of 14 years.
• Delayed puberty is most common in males.
• The Etiology of delayed puberty is either hypogonadotropic hypogonadism or Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition page 11382

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A newborn is brought to you after the labor room nurse encountered difficulty in identifying the baby's gender, indicating a case of ambiguous genitalia. As you prepare to explain the situation to the parents, you consider the most common causes of this condition. What is the most prevalent cause of ambiguous genitalia in newborns?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture15.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Conn's Syndrome : Characterized by hyperaldosteronism , leading to hypertension and electrolyte imbalances , not ambiguous genitalia.
• Option C. Adrenal Insufficiency : While adrenal insufficiency can present with electrolyte imbalances and possibly hypotension , it does not typically cause ambiguous genitalia.
• Option D. Pheochromocytoma : A condition involving a tumor of the adrenal medulla that produces excess catecholamines . It is not associated with ambiguous genitalia.
• Educational Objective
• Congenital adrenal hyperplasia is the most common cause of the ambiguous genitalia in newborn .
• Autosomal recessive condition, Most common enzyme deficient is 21 Hydroxylase Mutation of chromosome no 6.
• Features of 21 Hydroxylase deficiency are
• Ambiguous genitalia, Salt wasting, Dehydration, Hyperkalaemia, Hypoglycemia and Shock.
• Ref ; OP Ghai Essential Pediatrics 8 th Edition, Page No-526.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old boy is admitted to the PICU with severe bronchopneumonia and is placed on ventilation. Bilateral infiltrates are visible on his chest X-ray, and his urine output has decreased to 0.3 ml/kg/hour. Laboratory tests reveal a serum sodium level of 110 mmol/l, chloride level of 96 mmol/l, with low serum BUN and creatinine. Given the child's condition, which of the following is expected?
options: High serum osmolality and low urine osmolality
Low serum osmolality with high urinary osmolality.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. High serum osmolality and low urine osmolality : This scenario would be more consistent with dehydration , which does not match the child's presentation.
