• Option B. High serum and urine osmolality : This could occur in scenarios of generalized body dehydration , not indicated by the child's symptoms and lab results.
• Option C. Low serum and urine osmolality : This could be seen in conditions leading to overhydration without the selective retention of water over sodium, which does not align with the findings.
• Educational Objective
• Low serum osmolality with high urinary osmolality is seen in case of Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH).
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition.page 2049
• ; Nelson 21 st Edition.page 2049

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a busy city hospital in India, a pediatrician evaluates a 3-year-old with excessive thirst and frequent urination. Lab tests show plasma osmolality at 325 osm/kg and urine osmolality at 490 osm/kg, suggesting diabetes insipidus (DI). To differentiate between nephrogenic and central DI, which test should be conducted next?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . Water deprivation test is used to differentiate psychogenic polydipsia from the diabetes Insipidus.
• Option C . Oral water load test : Generally, not used in DI diagnosis as it can exacerbate symptoms and lead to water intoxication . This is used for diagnosing SIADH.
• Option D . Salt loading test also called as Saline loading test is used for Conn’s syndrome.
• Educational Objective
• The Vasopressin response test , also known as the Desmopressin stimulation test , helps differentiate between nephrogenic and central diabetes insipidus . In central DI , administration of vasopressin leads to a decrease in urine output and an increase in urine osmolality , indicating a deficiency in endogenous vasopressin . In nephrogenic DI , there is little to no response , as the kidneys are unresponsive to vasopressin.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 548, Page No- 10857- 10859

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a pediatric clinic, a 26-month-old child presents with unique facial characteristics such as doll-like facies, frontal bossing, and a depressed nasal bridge. The child, notably shorter than peers, has a proportionate body structure. An evaluation indicates a delayed bone age compared to the chronological age. Given these findings, which condition is least likely to account for the child's proportionate short stature?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Growth Hormone Deficiency : This condition can lead to proportionate short stature due to a uniform reduction in growth velocity but would not typically result in the specific craniofacial features described.
• Option B. Constitutional Delay : This common, benign condition can cause temporary proportionate short stature and delayed bone age , aligning with the child's presentation except for the distinct facial features.
• Option C. Malnutrition : Chronic malnutrition can result in proportionate short stature and delayed bone age , but may not account for the specific facial characteristics observed.
• Educational Objective
• An AD disorder because of mutation in FGFR3 gene. It leads to abnormal enchondral ossification. Rizhomelic limb shortening Disproportionate short stature Champagne glass pelvis.
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition. Page 14368
• ; Nelson 21 st Edition. Page 14368

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 14-year-old boy, who has been managing a condition with the aid of a syringe and medication for the past 5 years, presents with a recent random blood sugar (RBS) value of 158 mg/dl while on medication. Intrigued by an Instagram post about his condition, he inquires about the diagnostic criteria for his illness. Which of the following is not a recognized diagnostic criterion for the disease he is likely managing?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture6.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Fasting blood sugar > 126mg/dl : This is a standard criterion for the diagnosis of diabetes.
• Option B. Random blood sugar > 200mg/dl : A random blood sugar level exceeding 200 mg/dl, especially when accompanied by classic symptoms of diabetes , can be diagnostic.
• Option D. HbA1C > 6.5% : An HbA1C level greater than 6.5% is a well-recognized diagnostic criterion for diabetes , reflecting average blood glucose levels over the past 2-3 months.
• Educational Objective
• The standard diagnostic criteria for diabetes include a fasting blood sugar (FBS) level greater than 126 mg/dl
• Ref ; Ghai Essential Paediatrics 10 th Edition, Page No-546.
• ; Ghai Essential Paediatrics 10 th Edition, Page No-546.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 1-week-old neonate to the clinic, concerned about the baby's lethargy, hoarse cry, coarse facial features, large abdomen, and hypothermia. Laboratory tests reveal decreased levels of T3 and T4, with an elevated TSH level. Skull imaging shows the presence of intra-sutural bones, and a thyroid scan indicates no iodine uptake. Considering these findings, what is the most appropriate next step in the management of this neonate?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. & Option B . Start with oral thyroxine : While initiating thyroxine is a critical part of managing congenital hypothyroidism, determining the precise cause and understanding the anatomical status of the thyroid gland through imaging should precede the treatment to tailor the management appropriately.
• Option D. TSH receptor antibody estimation : This test is more relevant in cases where autoimmune thyroid disease is suspected , such as in Graves' disease, and less so in the neonatal period for congenital hypothyroidism.
• Educational Objective:
• Thyroid scan (i123 >tc99):
• Ectopic uptake – presence of ectopic Thyroid gland. Increase uptake - Dyshormonogenisis (decreased iodine) No uptake –next step is Ultrasound of thyroid gland.
• The reason for no uptake is
• Thyroid agenesis If normal thyroid gland check for TSH receptor antibodies, if they turn positive it’s because of p maternal antibodies, if absent its TSH receptor defect.
• After the investigation the next step is to treat with - oral thyroxine (10-15/kg/day).
• Ref ; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 581 Page No- 11398-11423

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-year-old child from a rural area is brought to the pediatric department with symptoms of vomiting, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor, lethargy, salt craving, and hyperpigmentation of the skin. The child's blood pressure is measured at 100/60mmHg, with a random blood sugar (RBS) level of 60mg/dl. Laboratory tests reveal a serum sodium level of 110 mEq/L and a serum potassium level of 6.0 mEq/L. Based on the provided information, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Conn's syndrome : Characterized by hyperaldosteronism , leading to hypertension and hypokalemia, not consistent with this child's presentation.
• Option B. Pendred Syndrome : A genetic disorder associated with hearing loss and thyroid dysfunction , not related to the adrenal gland or the symptoms described.
• Option D. Pheochromocytoma : A tumor of the adrenal medulla causing episodes of hypertension , headaches , palpitations , and sweating , not primarily presenting with the symptoms described.
• Educational Objective
• The most common cause of adrenal insufficiency is autoimmune or Addison’s disease . The other causes include infection with TB , Waterhouse friderichsen syndrome and Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia .
• Clinical features of adrenal insufficiency are vomiting, dehydration, reduced skin turgor, lethargy, salt craving and hyperpigmentation of skin.
• Lab Investigation findings - Hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia and haemoconcentration is seen.
• Ref ; nelson 21 st edition page 11515

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 15-year-old male is brought to the pediatric department by his parents, who are concerned about his vision problems. Upon further evaluation, it is revealed that the child has a history of multiple health issues, including a diagnosis of retinitis pigmentosa in early childhood, which has led to progressive vision loss. Based on the information provided, which of the following features is not associated with the syndrome affecting this child?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture12_0UYI8YO.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Obesity : Obesity is a common feature in Bardet-Biedl syndrome , often developing in early childhood.
• Option C. Rod-cone dystrophy : This term refers to retinitis pigmentosa , a hallmark of Bardet-Biedl syndrome, characterized by progressive vision loss starting in childhood.
• Option D. Hypogonadism : Hypogonadism, which can manifest as a delay in or absence of puberty , is another potential feature of Bardet-Biedl syndrome.
• Educational Objective
• Retinitis pigmentosa (Rod- cone dystrophy) , Obesity, Postaxial polydactyly, Spastic paresis, Learning disabilities and Hypogonadism in males.
• Ref ; Nelson 20 th Edition Page No – 310.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old girl named Mala is brought to the pediatrician by her mother due to a decrease in school performance linked to frequent infections. Mala has a history of recurrent sinusitis and bacterial pneumonia. Laboratory investigations reveal high levels of IgM with low levels of other immunoglobulin isotypes. Based on these clinical and laboratory findings, which condition is most likely affecting this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) : Typically presents with low levels of IgG and IgA and normal or only slightly elevated levels of IgM , not the high IgM levels seen in this case.
• Option B. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) : This condition usually presents in infancy with severe , life-threatening infections and involves a combined deficiency of both B and T cells, which is more severe than the symptoms presented by Mala.
• Option C. X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (XLA) : Characterized by very low or absent B cells and all immunoglobulin isotypes , except for IgM, which may be normal or low but not elevated as in Hyper-IgM syndrome.
• Educational Objective
• Hyper-IgM syndrome is characterized by elevated levels of IgM and low levels of other immunoglobulin isotypes , leading to recurrent bacterial infections , particularly involving the sinuses, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition.page 5462

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-month-old girl is brought in by her mother due to decreased fluid intake, vomiting, and watery diarrhea over the past 3 days. The father mentions the child's persistent thrush since birth, despite treatment, and two hospitalizations for pneumonia in the last four months. The child's weight has significantly dropped from the 50th to the 5th percentile. Physical examination reveals buccal mucosal exudates and hyperactive bowel sounds, but no dehydration signs, although extremity muscle wasting is noted. Given these findings and the history, severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) is suspected. Which of the following findings is consistent with SCID?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID), characterized by recurrent severe infections , failure to thrive , and chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis , and understand that defective cellular immunity is a key feature of the disease.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1022-1024.

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-month-old boy named Rahul is brought in for a routine check-up. He has a history of hospitalization for Kawasaki Disease, and aspirin therapy was discontinued 6 weeks ago following a normal echocardiogram result. Considering his medical history, which aspect requires special attention during his examination?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. His developmental assessment : While important, it is not uniquely impacted by his history of Kawasaki Disease or recent aspirin therapy.
• Option B. The abdominal examination : Kawasaki Disease can involve gastrointestinal symptoms , but with his Kawasaki Disease resolved and a normal echocardiogram, this would not be the primary concern.
• Option D. Serum hemoglobin evaluation : There's no specific link between recent aspirin therapy cessation post-Kawasaki Disease and a need for serum hemoglobin evaluation unless there were symptoms suggesting anemia or chronic disease.
• Educational Objective
• Live-virus vaccines (measles-mumps-rubella [MMR], varicella) are delayed for 11 months following high-dose IVIG administration ; IVIG potentially interferes with the immune response. The measles vaccine, typically given as MMR at 12 months, is provided if the exposure risk is high, but reimmunization is required unless serologic testing indicates adequate antibody titers.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 8 th Edition, 631-632

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 14-year-old girl visits the pediatric department with concerns about unexplained weight gain despite maintaining a low-calorie diet. She reports an inability to sustain physical activity with her peers due to rapid fatigue. Recently, she observed a swelling in her neck, prompting her visit. Given the described symptoms, which autoimmune antibody is most likely contributing to her current condition?

explanation.short: Ans. B. Anti Thyroid peroxidase antibody
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anti Myeloperoxidase antibody : Typically associated with vasculitis ( Wegener’s granulomatosis ), such as microscopic polyangiitis , not directly related to thyroid function.
• Option C. TSH receptor stimulating antibody : More commonly associated with Graves' disease , which typically presents with symptoms of hyperthyroidism , not weight gain and fatigue.
• Option D. Anti DNAase antibody : Associated with a response to Streptococcal infections (SLE) and not directly linked to thyroid diseases.
• Educational Objective
• The presence of Anti Thyroid peroxidase (Anti-TPO) antibodies suggests autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's thyroiditis), which is consistent with the patient's symptoms of weight gain, fatigue, and neck swelling (due to thyroid gland enlargement). This condition often leads to hypothyroidism , where the immune system attacks the thyroid gland , reducing its ability to produce thyroid hormones.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition Page No-2923.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a 15-day history of polyuria and polydipsia. His mother notes that his clothes no longer fit him as they have become too loose. Laboratory tests reveal a random blood sugar (RBS) level of 320 mg/dl and low insulin levels, leading to a diagnosis of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (DM). Considering the long-term complications of DM, which of the following is not considered a microvascular complication of this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Nephropathy : Diabetes can lead to damage to the small blood vessels in the kidneys , potentially causing chronic kidney disease or end-stage renal disease, which is a microvascular complication.
• Option B. Neuropathy : High blood sugar levels can damage nerves throughout the body , leading to diabetic neuropathy , a common microvascular complication of diabetes.
• Option D. Retinopathy : Diabetic retinopathy involves damage to the small blood vessels in the retina , leading to vision problems and blindness, and is a microvascular complication of diabetes.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Ghai Essential Paediatrics 8 th Edition

subject: Paediatrics
question: An anxious mother brings her 8-year-old to the clinic due to abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding that started yesterday morning. Upon evaluation, the child displays signs of precocious puberty, café au lait spots, and fibrous dysplasia, leading to a diagnosis of McCune-Albright Syndrome (MAS). Considering the diagnosis, which endocrine anomaly is commonly associated with this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hypothyroidism : While thyroid function can be affected in MAS , hypothyroidism is not a hallmark feature of this condition.
• Option B. GH deficiency : Growth hormone deficiency is not typically associated with MAS . Instead, MAS can involve conditions like acromegaly due to growth hormone excess.
• Option C. Delayed puberty : MAS is more commonly associated with precocious puberty rather than delayed puberty.
• Educational Objective
• McCune-Albright Syndrome (MAS) is characterized by a triad of polyostotic fibrous dysplasia , café-au-lait skin pigmentation , and a variety of endocrine disorders , including precocious puberty, rickets, hyperthyroidism, etc .
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No-530

subject: Paediatrics
question: A female child presents with ambiguous genitalia, dry skin, reduced skin turgor, low blood pressure (80/50 mmHg), and hypoglycemia. Further tests reveal elevated potassium levels (6 mmol/L), leading to a diagnosis of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH). Considering the characteristics of CAH, which of the following statements is false?
options: It is an Autosomal recessive condition.
Most common enzyme deficient is 21 Hydroxylase.
17- hydroxy progesterone levels will be decreased.

explanation.short: Ans. D. 17- hydroxy progesterone levels will be decreased.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It is an Autosomal recessive condition : This statement is true . CAH is typically inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
• Option B. The most common enzyme deficiency is 21-Hydroxylase : True . The majority of CAH cases are due to a deficiency in the 21-Hydroxylase enzyme.
• Option C. It involves a mutation on chromosome 6 : This statement is true . The gene responsible for 21-Hydroxylase, CYP21A2, is located on chromosome 6.
• Educational Objective
• Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) is an Autosomal recessive condition ,
• Most common enzyme deficient is 21 Hydroxylase Mutation of chromosome no 6.
• Features of 21 Hydroxylase deficiency are
• Ambiguous genitalia, Salt wasting, Dehydration, Hyperkalemia, Hypoglycemia and Shock.
• Diagnosis : increased 17- hydroxy progesterone levels.
• Treatment : Lifelong Hydrocortisone or fludrocortisone.
• Ref ; OP Ghai Essential Pediatrics  8 th Edition, Page No-526.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A female child presents with ambiguous genitalia, and upon examination, her blood pressure is notably high at 180/110 mmHg, with normal blood sugar levels. Further tests reveal low potassium levels, recorded at 2.80 mmol/L. Based on these findings, a diagnosis of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) is made. Which enzyme deficiency is likely responsible for this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. 21 Hydroxylase deficiency : The most common form of CAH , typically presenting with salt wasting or simple virilizing forms , but not usually associated with hypertension.
• Option C. 3 Beta-hydroxylase deficiency : A rare form of CAH that can present with ambiguous genitalia but is not typically associated with the hypertension and hypokalemia seen in 11 beta-hydroxylase deficiency.
• Option D. 17 Hydroxylase deficiency : This form of CAH leads to decreased sex steroids and cortisol production with increased mineralocorticoids , which could cause hypertension but typically presents with sexual infantilism rather than virilization.
• Educational Objective
• 11 beta hydroxylase deficiency presents with ambiguous genitalia in females , Hypertension and Hypokalemia . There will be no features of Glucocorticoid deficiency because of the excess 11 deoxycorticosterone.
• Ref ; OP Ghai Essential Pediatrics 8 th Edition, Page No-526.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A male child presents with under virilization, and upon examination, his blood pressure is significantly high at 180/110 mmHg, with normal blood sugar levels. Further testing reveals low potassium levels at 2.80 mmol/L. Based on these findings, a diagnosis of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) is made. Which enzyme deficiency is likely responsible for this clinical presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 11 beta-hydroxylase deficiency : This form of CAH can cause hypertension and hypokalemia due to the accumulation of mineralocorticoid precursors, but it typically presents with virilization rather than under virilization.
• Option B. 21 Hydroxylase deficiency : The most common form of CAH, which can lead to virilization in females and salt-wasting crises , but not typically associated with hypertension.
• Option C. 3 beta-hydroxylase deficiency : A rare form of CAH that can lead to ambiguous genitalia due to impaired steroid synthesis but is not commonly associated with the hypertension and hypokalemia seen in 17 hydroxylase deficiency.
• Educational Objective
• 17 hydroxylase deficiency in CAH presents with under virilization in males , along with hypertension and hypokalemia , due to impaired sex steroid and cortisol synthesis and increased mineralocorticoid production .
• Ref ; OP Ghai Essential Pediatrics 8 th Edition, Page No-526.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 2-year-old girl presents with a high-grade fever lasting over a week, bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, cracked lips, cervical lymphadenopathy, and a rash on the palms and soles. Kawasaki disease is diagnosed based on these findings. What is the most appropriate initial management for this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Ibuprofen : While it has anti-inflammatory properties , it is not the primary treatment for the inflammation associated with Kawasaki disease.
• Option Acetaminophen : Primarily used for fever control , it does not have the anti-inflammatory or antiplatelet effects required for treating Kawasaki disease.
• Option D. Antibiotics : Kawasaki disease is not caused by a bacterial infection , so antibiotics are not a part of the initial treatment unless there is evidence of a secondary bacterial infection.
• Educational Objective
• Treatment of Kawasaki disease:
• Immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours Aspirin 30 mg/kg per day (four times per day dosage) reduce to 5 mg/kg per day when fever
• Resolves Continue aspirin until 6 weeks or longer if abnormal echocardiogram.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No. 5370.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 14-year-old girl comes to the clinic with symptoms of weight loss, palpitations, and exophthalmos. Laboratory findings reveal elevated T3 and T4 levels, suppressed TSH, positive TSH receptor stimulating antibodies, and negative anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies. Based on the clinical presentation and lab results, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Follicular carcinoma thyroid : Typically presents with a thyroid mass and can cause hyperthyroidism but is not associated with TSH receptor antibodies.
• Option B. Papillary carcinoma thyroid : The most common thyroid cancer , which may present with a mass and occasionally hyperthyroidism but does not involve TSH receptor antibodies.
• Option D. Hashimoto's thyroiditis : An autoimmune disorder that usually presents with hypothyroidism , not hyperthyroidism, and is associated with positive anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies.
• Educational Objective
• Graves' disease is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the hyperthyroidism , which is commonly associated with symptoms such as weight loss , palpitations , and exophthalmos . The presence of TSH receptor stimulating antibodies is pathognomonic for Graves' disease , as these antibodies stimulate the thyroid gland to produce excessive amounts of thyroid hormones , leading to the suppression of TSH .
• Ref ; OP Ghai 8 th Edition, Page No-520.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 16-year-old phenotypically female patient reports to the clinic with primary amenorrhea. She has a height of 160 cm, weighs 45 kg, and is at Tanner stage B3 PH 1-2. An ultrasound reveals the absence of a uterus. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Müllerian dysgenesis : While this condition also results in the absence of a uterus , individuals with Müllerian dysgenesis typically have normal ovarian function and would not show the androgen insensitivity as with CAIS.
• Option B. Turner’s syndrome : Typically presents with a 45,X karyotype and may involve primary amenorrhea but also includes other features such as short stature, webbed neck, and is not associated with normal breast development or a male karyotype.
• Option D. Kallmann syndrome : It is characterized by the association of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and anosmia or hyposmia due to the defective migration of GnRH-synthesizing neurons, but the uterus is usually present.
• Educational Objective:
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 133.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 6-month-old male infant is brought by his parents to a primary healthcare center in a semi-urban area of India. The parents are concerned about their child's lack of response to stimuli and inability to support his head, which they noticed was significantly different from their older child at this age. They also mention that the infant has had episodes described as "startles" that seemed abnormal and unlike typical infant reflexes. On physical examination, the physician notes the infant's peculiar hair, which is sparse, kinky, and depigmented, along with loose skin folds, microcephaly, and a pronounced forehead. Given these findings and considering the rural Indian healthcare setting where genetic testing facilities are rare, the physician recalls a rare disease of copper metabolism linked to the ATP7A gene mutation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture11_ZaSc03U.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Wilson’s disease (A) involves a different gene (ATP7B) and typically presents later in childhood or adolescence with liver disease and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Dubin-Johnson syndrome (C) and Rotor syndrome (D) are both hereditary hyperbilirubinemia syndromes , primarily affecting the liver, and do not present with the neurological or connective tissue features seen in Menkes disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Menkes disease, also known as kinky hair disease , is a rare X-linked recessive disorder characterized by defective copper transport due to mutations in the ATP7A gene . This leads to a deficiency in copper-dependent enzymes like lysyl oxidase that are crucial for neurodevelopment, connective tissue formation, and hair pigmentation .
• Nelson 21 st edition page 3123
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition page 3123

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-year-old girl is brought in by her mother due to failure to thrive and easy fatigability. Examination reveals severe anemia, splenomegaly, and jaundice. The patient's family history includes an unspecified "blood problem." Hemoglobin electrophoresis is normal, and a complete blood count (CBC) shows normocytic anemia. Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous abnormal erythrocytes, including spiculated cells. Given these findings and the presence of an enzyme deficiency leading to anemia, what is the most common enzyme deficiency associated with this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency will results in anemia with Smear findings of `Bite cells .
• Option B: Glucose-6-Phosphatase deficiency will results in the Type 1 Glycogen storage disorder .
• Option C: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) deficiency will results in the Lactic acidosis , ataxia and retardation.
• Educational objective:
• Pyruvate kinase deficiency is an enzyme disorder that leads to hemolytic anemia by affecting the glycolytic pathway in red blood cells , resulting in insufficient ATP production and decreased cell survival . The clinical presentation can include failure to thrive, severe anemia , splenomegaly, and jaundice . The presence of bizarre erythrocytes , including spiculated cells on the peripheral smear , is consistent with hemolytic anemia .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page No 10062-10065
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page No 10062-10065

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 15-year-old girl presents to the pediatric outpatient department with progressive writhing and jerking movements of the extremities, excessive drooling, and multiple joint pains. On examination, a brown-yellow pigment is noted around the iris. Despite an unremarkable CT scan, an MRI reveals bilateral hypodense areas in the basal ganglia. Laboratory tests show elevated levels of aspartate transaminase (AST) and alanine transaminase (ALT). Based on the clinical presentation and investigative findings, which gene is most likely responsible for her condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. ATP 7A on Chromosome 13: Mutations in the ATP7A gene cause Menkes disease , a condition characterized by copper deficiency , leading to neurological degeneration , kinky hair , and growth failure , which is not consistent with the patient's presentation.
• Option C. ATP 13B on Chromosome 7 & D. ATP 13A on Chromosome 7: There are no well-documented diseases directly associated with mutations in ATP 13B or ATP 13A that match the clinical features described in the question. These options are included as distractors.
• Educational Objective:
• Wilson's disease , an autosomal recessive disorder affecting copper metabolism , leading to copper accumulation in tissues . This condition is characterized by neurological symptoms (such as writhing and jerking movements), psychiatric disturbances , Kayser-Fleischer rings (the brown-yellow pigment around the iris), and liver dysfunction (elevated AST and ALT). The disease is caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene located on Chromosome 13 , which encodes a protein essential for copper transport and excretion.
• Nelson 20 th Edition Page No- 1940.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition Page No- 1940.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-month-old full-term female infant is brought in by her mother due to poor weight gain. The child exhibits failure to thrive, hypotonia, reduced muscle mass, and significant head lag upon physical examination. Laboratory tests reveal markedly elevated levels of lactate, pyruvate, and alanine, with a blood gas analysis showing a pH of 7.0. Despite treatment with thiamine, the infant's lactic acidosis does not improve. Based on the clinical and laboratory findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) deficiency
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Pyruvate kinase deficiency will result in hemolytic anemia along with hepatosplenomegaly.
• Option C: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency will results in hemolytic anemia with Smear findings of `Bite cells .
• Option D: Glucose-6-Phosphatase deficiency will results in the Type 1 Glycogen storage disorder .
• Educational Objective:
• Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) deficiency , a metabolic disorder marked by elevated lactate , pyruvate , and alanine levels , along with failure to thrive and neurological symptoms
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3487-3489

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old girl is brought in by her anxious mother due to a significantly enlarged abdomen and recurrent episodes of weakness, sweating, and pallor, which resolve after eating. The child has a history of delayed developmental milestones and poor academic performance. Physical examination reveals an enlarged, firm liver. Laboratory tests show hypoglycemia, acidosis (low pH), elevated lactate, triglycerides, ketones, and free fatty acids. A liver biopsy indicates high glycogen content with normal structure, and enzyme assays reveal markedly low levels of glucose-6-phosphatase. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Anderson’s disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type IV): Characterized by abnormal glycogen with a branched structure , leading to liver cirrhosis , but not typically presenting with hypoglycemia or the specific metabolic abnormalities seen in Von Gierke’s disease.
• Option C. Cori’s disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type III): Involves a deficiency in the debranching enzyme , leading to abnormal glycogen structure and mild hypoglycemia , but not as severe as in Von Gierke’s disease.
• Option D. Pompe’s disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type II): Caused by a deficiency in the lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase , leading to glycogen accumulation in lysosomes , particularly affecting muscle tissue, not primarily associated with hypoglycemia or liver enlargement.
• Educational Objective:
• Von Gierke’s disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type Ia) through its hallmark features of hypoglycemia , lactic acidosis , hyperlipidemia , and hepatomegaly due to a deficiency in glucose-6-phosphatase .
• Affected tissue : Liver and kidney
• Clinical features: large quantities of glycogen are formed and stored in hepatocytes, renal and intestinal mucosa cells . The liver and kidneys become enlarged. Abnormalities of lipids may lead to xanthoma formation. Uric acid is often elevated and may cause clinical gout. Galactose, fructose, and glycerol are metabolised to lactate. The elevated blood lactate levels cause metabolic acidosis.
• Treatment : Blood loss may require oral iron. Treatment of hyperuricaemia and pyelonephritis protect renal function. Diazoxide to maintain blood glucose has been disappointing. Liver transplantation for primary disease or for hepatocellular carcinoma seems effective.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A newborn becomes lethargic and difficult to feed, developing progressive jaundice with a serum bilirubin level peaking at 13.5 mg/dL. Despite undergoing exchange blood transfusions, the bilirubin levels remain elevated. On the ninth day, vomiting commences, and the infant exhibits hepatomegaly and worsening cerebral symptoms. The accompanying eye findings, consistent with the provided image, and positive urine tests for reducing substances (which are negative when specifically testing for glucose), suggest hereditary galactosemia. Which of the following is NOT a clinical feature of hereditary galactosemia?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture3_UsEmUMc.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hepatosplenomegaly: This is a common feature of hereditary galactosemia due to the accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate in the liver and spleen.
• Option B. Aminoaciduria: This occurs due to the toxic effects of accumulated metabolites on the renal tubules , leading to the excretion of amino acids in the urine .
• Option C. Mental retardation: Can develop as a result of chronic toxicity from the accumulated galactose metabolites if the condition remains untreated.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary galactosemia is a metabolic disorder resulting from a deficiency of one of the enzymes involved in galactose metabolism , most commonly galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase . This leads to the accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate and related metabolites, causing multiple systemic effects . Clinical features include failure to thrive (which is contradictory to weight gain), jaundice, hepatomegaly, vomiting , and in untreated cases, cataracts, aminoaciduria, and mental retardation . Weight gain is not a feature of hereditary galactosemia; instead, these patients often have failure to thrive due to liver dysfunction and feeding difficulties .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No-3473-3476.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No-3473-3476.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-month-old infant presents with failure to thrive and abdominal distension. On examination, the pediatrician notes hepatosplenomegaly. Liver biopsy findings show glycogen with an abnormal, amylopectin-like structure characterized by long outer chains and a lack of branching points. Based on these biopsy findings, which type of Glycogen Storage Disease (GSD) is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cori’s disease (GSD III): Cori's disease is due to a deficiency of the debranching enzyme (amylo-1,6-glucosidase ). This leads to a limit dextrin-like structure in the glycogen, not the long outer chains without adequate branching seen in Anderson's disease .
• Option C. Her’s disease (GSD VI): Her's disease is caused by a deficiency of liver phosphorylase . It does not cause the accumulation of abnormal glycogen but rather an inability to adequately break down normal glycogen .
• Option D. Pompe’s disease (GSD II): Pompe's disease is a lysosomal storage disease due to deficiency of the enzyme acid alpha-glucosidase . It leads to the accumulation of normal glycogen in lysosomes and not the abnormal structure described.
• Educational Objective:
• Anderson's disease, or GSD IV, is caused by a deficiency of the branching enzyme (α-1,4-glucan 6-glycosyltransferase) . This deficiency results in the accumulation of an abnormal glycogen with a structure similar to amylopectin , known as polyglucosan, which has long outer chains and is missing the appropriate branching pattern . The liver biopsy of a patient with Anderson's disease typically shows this abnormal glycogen structure , which aligns with the findings described in the patient.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3458-3463
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3458-3463

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Madhuri, an 11-year-old girl, presents with joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the hips and knees, and reports that her urine turns dark upon air exposure and has a peculiar odor. Physical examination shows pigmented areas on her skin, and laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of homogentisic acid in her urine. Based on these clinical findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and its mode of inheritance?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture6_o8HgSS1.jpg, https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture7_wX4aPp7.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) Alkaptonuria and Autosomal Recessive
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alkaptonuria and Autosomal Dominant: This is incorrect; alkaptonuria is autosomal recessive .
• Option B. Phenylketonuria and Autosomal Dominant: This is incorrect; PKU is autosomal recessive and is not associated with joint pain or urine darkening.
• Option C. Phenylketonuria and Autosomal Recessive: Although PKU is autosomal recessive , it does not cause urine to darken or joint pain , making this option incorrect for the given clinical presentation.
• Educational Objective:
• Alkaptonuria is characterized by a deficiency in the enzyme homogentisate dioxygenase , leading to the accumulation of homogentisic acid , which can cause darkening of the urine upon exposure to air and joint pain due to ochronosis (buildup of homogentisic acid in connective tissues). The pigmentation found on physical examination further supports this diagnosis. Alkaptonuria is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3149-3150
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3149-3150

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 4-month-old infant presents to the pediatric department with a distinct fair complexion, blue eyes, blond hair, an eczematous rash, and a musty odor in the diapers. The mother reports concerns raised by a medical student about the child's unusual features. After evaluating the clinical presentation, the physician suspects Phenylketonuria (PKU). Which of the following statements about Phenylketonuria is incorrect?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture5_sNq2OQo.jpg]
options: It is an Autosomal Recessive disorder
Tendon reflexes in the baby is hyperreactive
Decrease phenylalanine and Tyrosine in diet
Most of the babies are normal during birth

explanation.short: Ans. C) Decrease phenylalanine and Tyrosine in diet
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Autosomal Recessive disorder: PKU is indeed an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning that a child must inherit one defective gene from each parent to manifest the disease.
• Option B. Tendon reflexes in the baby are hyperreactive: Neurological abnormalities, such as hyperactive reflexes , can occur in PKU due to the accumulation of phenylalanine , which can be toxic to the brain .
• Option D. Most babies with PKU appear normal at birth: Newborns with PKU typically appear normal at birth because maternal metabolism compensates for the enzyme deficiency during fetal development . Symptoms and signs develop later if the condition remains untreated .
• Educational Objective:
• Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a metabolic disorder characterized by a deficiency of the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase , which is necessary for converting phenylalanine to tyrosine . Dietary management of PKU involves reducing the intake of phenylalanine , which accumulates due to the enzyme deficiency. However, since tyrosine becomes an essential amino acid in individuals with PKU , it is necessary to ensure adequate tyrosine intake , not decrease it. Therefore, the correct dietary management should be to decrease phenylalanine and ensure sufficient tyrosine in the diet .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3131
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3131

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following disorders with their respective urinary findings:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture10_CWdz8Xi.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) A4 B3 C1 D2
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• A4: Isovaleric Acidemia - Sweaty Feet Odour: isovaleric Acidemia, particularly Type II , is known for producing a distinctive "sweaty feet " odour due to the accumulation of organic acids .
• B3: Maple Syrup Urine Disease - Burned Sugar Smell: This condition is named for the sweet, maple syrup-like smell of the affected infants' urine , which is a result of the accumulation of branched chain amino acids leucine, isoleucine, and valine , due to deficiency of branched chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase enzyme .
• C1: Multiple Carboxylase Deficiency - Tom Cat Urine: This disorder, often presenting with a range of neurological and dermatological symptoms , can produce a urine odor reminiscent of a tom cat , due to the accumulation of various organic acids .
• D2: Phenylketonuria - Mousy or Musty Odour: Phenylketonuria (PKU) leads to a build up of phenylalanine, causing a characteristic "mousy" or "musty" odour in the urine and on the breath .
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 3107
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 3107

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother presents to a pediatric clinic in a suburban area of India with her 4-month-old infant, reporting significant feeding difficulties, recurrent vomiting, diarrhea, and a noticeable lack of weight gain since birth. The infant, who was previously healthy, now appears lethargic and uninterested in feeding. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes jaundice, hepatomegaly, and splenomegaly. A distinctive boiled cabbage odor is also observed, which raises suspicion for a metabolic disorder. Based on the clinical presentation and the specific odor noted, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the treatment of choice?
options: Both a and b are correct

explanation.short: Ans. A) Hereditary tyrosinemia and Nitisinone
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Richner-Hanhart syndrome (tyrosinemia type II) is caused by a deficiency of tyrosine aminotransferase and is characterized by painful palmoplantar keratoderma, corneal ulcers, and sometimes intellectual disability, but not typically by the systemic symptoms seen in hereditary tyrosinemia type I.
• Option C: Incorrect because Richner-Hanhart syndrome does not present with the systemic symptoms described, and its treatment may involve dietary restrictions of phenylalanine and tyrosine rather than Nitisinone.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary tyrosinemia type I is a rare metabolic disorder characterized by the deficient activity of the enzyme fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (characteristic boiled cabbage odor). Nitisinone is the treatment of choice.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3143 -3153
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3143 -3153

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-month-old child is brought to the paediatric clinic by his mother due to concerns about the child's poor feeding, frequent vomiting, and noticeable lack of growth and developmental progress. Physical examination reveals hepatosplenomegaly and generalized muscle weakness. Laboratory investigations identify elevated levels of hawkinsin and other unusual metabolites in both blood and urine samples. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of these findings?

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 4-month-old infant is brought to the paediatric clinic by a concerned mother who reports that her child is not gaining weight adequately, has thin extremities, and appears shorter than expected for his age. Physical examination reveals a protruding abdomen. The child's medical history includes recurrent infections. Laboratory tests show hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, and neutropenia. Based on the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and which enzyme is defective or deficient in this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture15_djiFI6a.jpg]
options: Type1b GSD and glucose 6 phosphate translocase
Type IIIa GSD and liver and muscle debranching enzyme
Type IV GSD and branching enzyme

explanation.short: Ans. B) Type1b GSD and glucose 6 phosphate translocase
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Type 1a GSD and glucose-6-phosphatase: While Type 1a GSD shares many clinical and laboratory features with Type 1b, including hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis , and a protruding abdomen due to hepatomegaly , it does not typically present with neutropenia and recurrent infections.
• Option C. Type IIIa GSD and liver and muscle glycogen debranching enzyme: Type IIIa GSD (Cori or Forbes disease) involves a deficiency in the debranching enzyme , affecting both liver and muscle , but the clinical picture is more commonly associated with myopathy and cardiomyopathy rather than neutropenia.
• Option D: Type IV GSD and glycogen branching enzyme: Type IV GSD (Andersen's disease) is caused by a deficiency in the glycogen branching enzyme , leading to the accumulation of abnormal glycogen structure , but it is more commonly associated with progressive liver disease and does not typically present with neutropenia.
• Educational Objective:
• Type 1b Glycogen Storage Disease (GSD) is characterized by a deficiency in glucose-6-phosphate translocase , leading to impaired glucose production from glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis . This results in the clinical presentation of hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, and hyperuricemia. The neutropenia and recurrent infections are distinctive for Type 1b GSD, differentiating it from Type 1a, which is primarily associated with glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency but does not typically present with neutropenia and recurrent infections.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A paediatric resident is reviewing metabolic disorders for an upcoming exam and is focusing on enzyme deficiencies associated with various paediatric diseases. Match the following paediatric diseases with their corresponding enzyme deficiencies:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture13.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Alkaptonuria is caused by a deficiency in homogentisic acid oxidase (iii), leading to the accumulation of homogentisic acid , which causes darkening of urine and potentially arthritic complications. Phenylketonuria results from a deficiency in phenylalanine hydroxylase (iv), which leads to elevated levels of phenylalanine in the blood and can cause intellectual disability if untreated. Tyrosinemia (Type I) is due to a deficiency in fumarylacetoacetate hydrolase (i), resulting in liver and kidney dysfunction , along with a cabbage-like odor . Richner-Hanhart syndrome (Tyrosinemia Type II) is associated with a deficiency in tyrosine transaminase (ii), leading to painful palmoplantar keratoderma and corneal ulcers .
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 9-month-old boy is brought to the pediatric emergency department due to multiple episodes of loose stools. The child appears dehydrated, with a dry mouth, but drinks water eagerly when offered. Following the administration of oral rehydration solution (ORS) according to protocol, further examination reveals photosensitivity and an ataxic gait. The mother reports a family history of two siblings with similar symptoms, though she and her husband are unaffected. Based on the clinical findings and family history, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture2_fXbtLrQ.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Alkaptonuria results from the deficiency of Homogentisate oxidase and has a clinical feature of changing of urine colour and Ochronosis.
• Option C: Phenylketonuria results from the deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase and has clinical features of blond hair and skin changes.
• Option D: Maple Syrup Urine Disease has a clinical feature of burnt sugar smell of urine.
• Educational Objective:
• Hartnup disease, an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by diarrhea, dehydration, photosensitivity, and neurological symptoms such as ataxia. The family history of similarly affected siblings with unaffected parents supports the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3178

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old boy presents with fever, chills, abdominal pain, and diarrhea in a malaria-prevalent area. Following a positive rapid antigen test for malaria, he is started on Primaquine therapy. Four days into treatment, he develops hematuria, with his hemoglobin level dropping from 11.3 g/dL to 7.12 g/dL, total bilirubin rising from 1.38 mg/dL to 4.52 mg/dL, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) increasing from 236 IU/L to 509 IU/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) deficiency: This metabolic disorder primarily affects the neurological system and is not associated with drug-induced hemolysis.
• Option B. Pyruvate kinase deficiency: While this enzyme deficiency can cause hemolytic anemia, it is less likely to be triggered by Primaquine therapy compared to G6PD deficiency.
• Option D. Glucose-6-Phosphatase deficiency: This condition is related to glycogen storage disease type I and presents with metabolic issues rather than acute hemolytic anemia triggered by medication.
• Educational Objective:
• Primaquine and other oxidative drugs can precipitate hemolysis in individuals with G6PD deficiency, as seen in this case with the sudden drop in hemoglobin, increase in total bilirubin, and rise in LDH levels, which are markers of hemolysis.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page No 10068-10069
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page No 10068-10069

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 9-month-old child presents with recurrent infections, fatigue, and bleeding episodes. The attending physician notes anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia on a complete blood count. Upon examination, the child exhibits features suggestive of Mucopolysaccharidoses. Considering the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, the physician considers diseases for which enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is not enough to treat the condition as a single aid. Which of the following diseases cannot be treated with enzyme replacement therapy alone?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gaucher's disease: Gaucher's disease is treatable with enzyme replacement therapy using imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa, or taliglucerase alfa , which are recombinant forms of the deficient enzyme glucocerebrosidase.
• Option B. Hurler’s disease: Although Hurler's disease also requires more than ERT due to its severe and systemic nature, if addressing specifically enzyme replacement efficacy, it can still be part of the treatment protocol alongside other interventions like hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
• Option D. Fabry’s disease: Fabry’s disease can be treated with enzyme replacement therapy using agalsidase beta or agalsidase alfa .
• Educational Objective:
• Hunter's disease, a form of Mucopolysaccharidosis, requires treatment approaches beyond enzyme replacement therapy due to its neurological involvement, which ERT alone cannot address.
• Enzyme replacement therapy is available for Gauchers disease, Hurler’s disease, Fabry’s disease and Pompes disease .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 107 – Page No 3551
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 107 – Page No 3551

subject: Paediatrics
question:
An 8-month-old child is brought to the pediatric clinic with a history of recurrent infections and easy bruisability. Examination reveals excessive pallor, cloudy cornea, and coarse facial features. A complete blood count (CBC) indicates the presence of anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia. Based on the clinical history and examination findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and the associated enzyme deficiency?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture8_Yqjllww.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans.  D) Hurler’s disease and Alpha-L-iduronidase
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Gaucher’s disease and Glucocerebrosidase: Gaucher’s disease is caused by a deficiency in the enzyme glucocerebrosidase , leading to the accumulation of glucocerebrosides . While it can cause anemia, thrombocytopenia, and bone abnormalities , it does not typically present with cloudy corneas or the coarse facial features seen in Hurler’s disease.
• Option B. Hurler’s disease and Glucocerebrosidase: This is incorrect; Hurler’s disease is associated with a deficiency of alpha-L-iduronidase .
• Option C. Gaucher’s disease and Galactocerebrosidase: This is incorrect; Gaucher’s disease is associated with a deficiency of glucocerebrosidase , not galactocerebrosidase.
• Educational Objective:
• Hurler’s disease is characterized by features such as coarse facial features, cloudy corneas, recurrent infections, and hematological abnormalities , with the underlying enzyme deficiency being alpha-L-iduronidase.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 104 – Page No 3413-3416
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 104 – Page No 3413-3416

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old boy is brought to a pediatric outpatient department in an urban hospital in India with his parents, who are concerned about his recent onset of hand tremors and occasional jerking movements. The boy, previously healthy and an active cricket player, has noticed these symptoms worsening over the past month, affecting his ability to play. An ophthalmological examination reveals Kayser-Fleischer (KF) rings, and an MRI indicates bilateral hypodense lesions in the basal ganglia. Laboratory tests show elevated liver enzymes (AST and ALT), and a liver biopsy reveals increased copper levels. Based on these findings, a diagnosis of Wilson's disease is made. Which of the following statements about Wilson's disease is NOT true?
options: ATP 7B gene mutation is responsible for Wilsons disease
Wing beating tremors can be seen in this disease
D-penicillamine is used in the 1 st line of management
Increased serum ceruloplasmin levels is seen

explanation.short: Ans. D) Increased serum ceruloplasmin levels is seen

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother from a rural community in India brings her 3-year-old son to a local health clinic. She reports that her child has experienced episodes of yellowing skin and eyes since infancy, particularly during episodes of common childhood illnesses like colds and flu. She is worried as these episodes seem to recur with each illness. The child appears well between episodes. On examination, the physician notes a slight enlargement of the liver and spleen. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of indirect bilirubin with normal liver enzyme levels, and there is no evidence of hemolysis. Considering the intermittent nature of the jaundice, especially during infections, and the specific lab findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Crigler-Najjar Syndrome (A): Is a much rarer condition characterized by severe , persistent jaundice due to a complete or severe deficiency in the enzyme that conjugates bilirubin , leading to significantly elevated levels of unconjugated bilirubin .
• Option B: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome (B) and Rotor Syndrome (C): Both involve conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal liver enzyme levels but typically present with dark liver (Dubin-Johnson) or normal liver appearance (Rotor) on imaging, not applicable in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Gilbert Syndrome is a common, mild liver disorder in which the liver doesn't properly process bilirubin . It is characterized by intermittent episodes of jaundice , particularly during periods of illness, fasting, or stress, due to mildly elevated levels of unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin in the absence of liver damage or hemolysis. The key feature of Gilbert Syndrome is the preservation of normal liver enzyme levels and the absence of other signs of liver disease , distinguishing it from other conditions listed.
• Nelson 20 th Edition Page No- 1937-1939.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition Page No- 1937-1939.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-year-old child is brought to the paediatric clinic by their mother, who is concerned about the child's delayed growth and developmental milestones. During the examination, the paediatrician notes the child's breathing difficulties and decreased visual acuity. Fundoscopic examination reveals a cherry-red spot on the macula, and further physical examination shows hepatosplenomegaly. A peripheral blood smear reveals the presence of Zebra bodies. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and which enzyme deficiency is responsible?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Niemann pick disease and sphingomyelinase
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Gaucher disease and glucocerebrosidase: Gaucher disease results from a deficiency in glucocerebrosidase, leading to the accumulation of glucocerebroside . While it can present with hepatosplenomegaly and growth delay , the cherry-red spot is not a typical feature, and Gaucher cells, not Zebra bodies, are seen on peripheral smear.
• Option C. Farber's disease and ceramidase: Farber's disease involves a deficiency in ceramidase , leading to the accumulation of ceramides . It typically presents with joint deformities , subcutaneous nodules , and progressive hoarseness , which are not described in this scenario.
• Option D. Wolman disease and acid lipase: Wolman disease is caused by a deficiency in acid lipase , leading to the accumulation of cholesteryl esters and triglycerides. It presents early in life with severe malabsorption, failure to thrive, and hepatosplenomegaly, but not typically with a cherry-red spot or Zebra bodies.
• Educational Objective:
• Niemann-Pick disease , particularly type A and B , is characterized by a deficiency in the enzyme sphingomyelinase , leading to the accumulation of sphingomyelin in various tissues , including the liver, spleen, lungs, and brain . The clinical presentation of delayed growth , developmental delay , hepatosplenomegaly , and the cherry-red spot on the macula , along with the finding of Zebra bodies on peripheral smear , are classic for Niemann-Pick disease .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 104 – Page No 3413-3416.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 104 – Page No 3413-3416.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-month-old infant is brought to the pediatric clinic by their mother, who is concerned about the child's apparent weakness and poor muscle tone. The mother also reports difficulty with feeding and the child's failure to gain weight appropriately. Physical examination reveals respiratory distress, hepatosplenomegaly, and an enlarged cardiac silhouette on chest X-ray. Given these findings and a suspicion of Glycogen Storage Disease (GSD), what is the most common cause of death in patients with this presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Recurrent infection: While infections can be a concern, especially in immunocompromised patients , they are not the primary cause of death in patients with the type of GSD described in the scenario.
• Option B. Liver failure: Liver involvement is common in several types of GSD, but liver failure is not the primary cause of death in Pompe disease , where cardiac and respiratory involvement is more significant .
• Option D. Respiratory failure: Although respiratory failure is a critical component , it usually occurs in conjunction with cardiac failure in Pompe disease , making cardiorespiratory failure (C) a more comprehensive answer.
• Educational Objective:
• Pompe disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type II), characterized by lysosomal alpha-1, 4-glucosidase (acid maltase) deficiency . This leads to glycogen accumulation in lysosomes , particularly affecting cardiac and skeletal muscles . The early infantile form presents with hypotonia, cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly , and respiratory distress . The most common cause of death in these patients is cardiorespiratory failure due to progressive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and respiratory muscle weakness.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No 3441-3446

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-year-old child is brought in by their mother due to a recurrent rash upon sun exposure and the passage of purple-colored urine. Following an examination, the child is diagnosed with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria, a disorder of heme biosynthesis. Which of the following is NOT a feature of Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Osteodystrophy: Bone abnormalities can occur in CEP due to porphyrin accumulation.
• Option B. Erythrodontia: This refers to reddish-brown discoloration of the teeth, a hallmark sign of CEP, caused by the deposition of porphyrins in dental tissues.
• Option D. Mutilation and Disfigurement: Severe skin damage from photosensitivity reactions can lead to significant mutilation and disfigurement in CEP.
• Educational Objective:
• Enzyme defect: Uroporphyrinogen III cosynthase.
• Characteristic features - It is a rare congenital disorder. Mostly contained in erythropoietic tissues (bone). Clinical features include;
• Severe cutaneous photosensitivity with blistering and increased friability of the skin over light-exposed areas. The first manifestation is often pink-to-dark red discoloration of the urine. Porphyrin deposition may lead to corneal ulcers and scarring, reddish-brown discoloration of the teeth (erythrodontia), and Bone loss and/or expansion of the bone marrow. Mutilation and Disfigurement.
• The diagnosis of CEP in a proband with suggestive clinical and biochemical findings is most commonly established by identification of biallelic pathogenic variants in UROS.
• Treatment of manifestations: There is no specific treatment for the photosensitivity. Currently, the only effective management is prevention of blistering by avoidance of sun and light exposure.
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 110 – Page No 3656-3662
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 110 – Page No 3656-3662

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 8-months-old boy was brought to the emergency department after several episodes of vomiting and lethargy. After a careful evaluation and history, the mother admits that these episodes occur after ingestion of certain types of sweet food which are high in fructose. His blood sugar was extremely low (42 mg/dL). The test for reducing sugar in urine was positive. What is the most likely diagnosis and the enzyme deficient based on the clinical scenario?
options: Hereditary fructose intolerance and aldolase B

explanation.short: Ans. B) Hereditary fructose intolerance and aldolase B
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Galactosemia and GALT: Galactosemia is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme Galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase (GALT). It leads to an accumulation of galactose in the blood , manifesting symptoms when a child is exposed to lactose from milk . The clinical presentation would not be triggered specifically by fructose ingestion.
• Option C. Galactosemia and Aldolase B: This option is incorrect as Galactosemia is associated with a deficiency of GALT , not Aldolase B. Aldolase B deficiency is related to Hereditary Fructose Intolerance .
• Option D. Hereditary Fructose Intolerance and Fructokinase: Fructokinase deficiency leads to essential fructosuria , a benign condition where fructose is excreted in the urine. It does not cause hypoglycemia or other serious symptoms seen in HFI.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary fructose intolerance (HFI) is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme Aldolase B , which is involved in the metabolism of fructose . This condition leads to an accumulation of fructose-1-phosphate in the liver, kidney, and intestines, causing inhibition of glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis , which in turn leads to hypoglycemia after fructose ingestion . The clinical symptoms of HFI, including vomiting, lethargy, and hypoglycemia after ingesting fructose. The presence of reducing sugars in the urine is indicative of an inherited disorder of carbohydrate metabolism, such as HFI .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No-3479-3481
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 105 – Page No-3479-3481

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old child with mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) presents for regular follow-up. Upon evaluation, severe pallor, signs of easy bruisability, coarse facies, and clear cornea are noted. Genetic reports indicate CAG trinucleotide repeats. Suspecting Hunter's disease, what is the pattern of inheritance for this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A, C and D are not appropriate as Hunters disease is a X-Linked Recessive disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Hunter syndrome , also known as mucopolysaccharidosis type II (MPS II), is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by a deficiency of the enzyme iduronate sulfatase (IDS). This deficiency leads to the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) in various tissues.
• Hunter syndrome is X-linked recessive , it primarily affects males , while females may be carriers of the condition.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 107 – Page No 3551
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 107 – Page No 3551

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to a pediatric outpatient department by his mother, who is concerned about her child's eyes, which have been showing excessive tearing, redness, and sensitivity to light for the past few weeks. She also mentions that her baby seems to be in pain when touching his feet. On examination, the pediatrician observes nonpruritic hyperkeratotic plaques on the soles of the infant's feet, a finding that is unusual and concerning. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated levels of tyrosine in the blood (hypertyrosinemia) and increased 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid in the urine. Given these clinical features and laboratory findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Tyrosinemia type 1 is characterized by liver and kidney dysfunction, neurological crises, and a cabbage-like odor , but not typically by the skin and ocular findings seen in Tyrosinemia type II.
• Option C: Tyrosinemia type 3 is very rare and typically presents with neurological symptoms ; the characteristic skin and ocular features of Tyrosinemia type II are absent.
• Option D: Tyrosinemia type 4 is not a recognized classification within the medical community for tyrosinemia, making it an incorrect option.
• Educational Objective:
• Tyrosinemia type II (Richner-Hanhart syndrome) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by tyrosine aminotransferase deficiency , leading to elevated tyrosine and 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid levels . Tyrosinemia type I involves liver and renal issues , while type III is associated with neurological symptoms , distinguishing them from type II. Tyrosinemia type IV is not recognized .
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3143 -3153
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3143 -3153

subject: Paediatrics
question: A worried mother rushes her 1-year-old son to the emergency department of a district hospital in India, reporting that her child has just experienced a convulsive episode. She mentions that the child was healthy at birth and reached early developmental milestones on time, but she has been increasingly concerned about his slow growth and a persistent rash that she describes as similar to eczema. Upon examination, the emergency physician notes the distinctive eczematous rash and also detects a "mousy" odor in the child's urine, raising suspicion for a metabolic disorder. Given the combination of developmental concerns, seizure, eczematoid rash, and characteristic urine odor, which condition is most likely, and how should the diagnosis be confirmed?
options: Alkaptonuria and ferric chloride urine test
Phenylketonuria and ferric chloride urine test

explanation.short: Ans. C) Phenylketonuria and ferric chloride urine test
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Alkaptonuria (Options A and B) is a different metabolic disorder characterized by the accumulation of homogentisic acid , leading to darkening of the urine upon standing and ochronosis (bluish-black discoloration of connective tissue), but it does not present with the clinical features described in the question, such as eczematoid rash and mousy odor urine.
• Educational Objective:
• Phenylketonuria , a treatable metabolic disorder , and the simple diagnostic tests like the ferric chloride urine test is used in initial screening , particularly in areas where more sophisticated diagnostic facilities may not be readily available .
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3178
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 103 – Page No 3178

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 3-year-old child arrives at the pediatric outpatient department in the morning with complaints of recurrent nosebleeds and noticeable fatigue. The mother also reports that the child has gingival hyperplasia. Following a complete blood count (CBC), the results reveal anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukocytosis with 75% blast cells, leading to a diagnosis of acute myeloid leukemia (AML). Considering the specific symptom of gingival hyperplasia alongside the AML diagnosis, which subtype of AML is this child most likely to have?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: AML M3: Also known as acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), is typically associated with a specific chromosomal translocation (t(15;17)) and presents with a high risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), but not specifically with gingival hyperplasia.
• Option C: AML M1: Also known as acute myeloblastic leukemia without maturation , primarily involves immature myeloblasts and does not commonly present with gingival hyperplasia.
• Option D: AML M2: Also known as acute myeloblastic leukemia with maturation , involves myeloblasts with some degree of maturation but is not specifically associated with gingival hyperplasia.
• Educational Objective:
• AML M5 , also known as acute monoblastic/monocytic leukemia , is commonly associated with extramedullary infiltrates , including gingival hyperplasia . This subtype of AML is characterized by a proliferation of monoblasts and monocytes in the bone marrow and can manifest with distinctive clinical features such as gingival enlargement due to leukemic infiltration.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following characteristic findings along with their disease causative agent:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture13_4MdDe3E.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Nodular rash with indentation: This description is characteristic of poxvirus infections, such as chickenpox (varicella) and smallpox, where the lesions are often described as "dewdrop on a rose petal" for chickenpox, and with deep indentations for smallpox.
• Option B: Koplik spots: These are small, white, grain-like spots found on the buccal mucosa opposite the first and second molars, and are pathognomonic for measles (Rubeola).
• Option D : Pastia's lines: These are linear petechial rash found in the creases of the body, particularly in the antecubital fossa , and are associated with s carlet fever.
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 6733
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 6733

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old child is brought to the clinic with symptoms including fever, cough, runny nose, sensitivity to light, and a sore throat. The physical examination reveals a rash that began on the head and subsequently spread to cover the entire body. The rash is characterized by large, flat, red spots that have coalesced. Based on the clinical presentation described, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Varicella or Chickenpox, is a viral infection that typically presents with a rash consisting of small, itchy, fluid-filled blisters. The rash starts on the face and then spreads to the rest of the body.
• Option B: Rubella or German measles , is a viral infection that can cause a rash, fever, and other symptoms. However, the rash in rubella typically starts on the face and then spreads to the rest of the body. The rash consists of smaller, pink or light red spots that may merge together.
• Option D: Scarlet fever , is a bacterial infection that can cause a rash, fever, and other symptoms. The rash consists of small, red bumps that feel like sandpaper a nd appear on the chest and stomach first , then spread to o ther areas.
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of fever, cough, runny nose (the three "C's": cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis), along with a rash that starts on the head and spreads to the rest of the body, sensitivity to light (photophobia), and a sore throat , is highly indicative of Measles (Rubeola).
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1542-1547.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1542-1547.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother consults for her 5-year-old child who has developed multiple small, raised, pink or skin-colored bumps on the skin. These bumps are described as painless, smooth, round or dome-shaped with a dimpled center, and may exhibit a waxy or pearly appearance. The child is otherwise in good health with no additional symptoms. Based on these findings, a diagnosis of molluscum contagiosum is made. Which of the following statements are true regarding the features of molluscum contagiosum?
options: Henderson Patterson bodies are seen in the HPE
Immunocompromised patients are most commonly affected
The causative organism is a brick shaped virus

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Henderson Patterson bodies are seen in the histopathological examination: This is true. Henderson-Patterson bodies, also known as molluscum bodies, are characteristic cytoplasmic inclusion bodies found in the epidermal cells infected with the molluscum contagiosum virus, visible upon histopathological examination.
• Option B: Immunocompromised patients are most commonly affected: While molluscum contagiosum is common in children and can occur in any individual, it is more persistent, widespread , and sometimes more challenging to treat in immunocompromised patients, making them a notably affected group.
• Option C: The causative organism is a brick-shaped virus: Molluscum contagiosum is caused by a member of the Poxviridae family, which are large, complex viruses, and typically described as "brick-shaped" due to its rectangular shape.
• Educational Objective:
• Molluscum contagiosum, a common viral skin infection, especially in pediatric populations. The characteristic Henderson-Patterson bodies found upon histopathological examination, the increased prevalence and severity in immunocompromised patients , and the nature of the causative poxvirus, provides comprehensive insight into the disease, aiding in accurate diagnosis and management.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1580-1581

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following Inclusion bodies and their infectious disease:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture15_Axzh1M9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Cowdry Type A (B4): Cowdry Type A inclusion bodies are typically associated with Herpes Simplex Virus infections. They are eosinophilic, intranuclear inclusions found in infected cells.
• Option C: Cowdry Type B (C3): Though Cowdry Type B inclusions are specifically linked to Polio Virus.
• Option D: Guarnieri Bodies (D1): Guarnieri bodies are pathognomonic of Small Pox infection caused by the Variola virus . They are eosinophilic or basophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions found in the cytoplasm of infected cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Nelson 21 st Edition
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 1-month-old infant is brought to the clinic by their mother due to symptoms of fever and lethargy. Upon examination, the infant is found to have an enlarged liver and spleen. The mother reports having a cat at home and mentions that she consumed undercooked meat during her pregnancy. Given this history and the clinical findings, which congenital infection is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Congenital Syphilis may also present with fever and hepatosplenomegaly, but it’s typically associated with characteristic skin rash, bone abnormalities, and other specific clinical features.
• Option C: Congenital HIV may present with similar symptoms, but it’s usually diagnosed by routine screening of the mother during pregnancy.
