• Option D: Congenital Cytomegalovirus may cause a range of symptoms including fever, hepatosplenomegaly, and neurological abnormalities, but it’s typically associated with hearing loss.
• Educational Objective:
• The history of exposure to a cat and the consumption of undercooked meat during pregnancy, combined with the infant's presentation of fever, lethargy, and hepatosplenomegaly, strongly suggest congenital toxoplasmosis. Cats are known to be definitive hosts for Toxoplasma gondii , and undercooked meat is a common source of infection.
• Congenital toxoplasmosis can result from the transplacental transmission of the parasite when a woman is infected during pregnancy.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1723-1725.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1723-1725.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 2-month-old infant is brought in by their mother due to fever and irritability. Notably, the child has a smaller head circumference than expected for their age. The mother recalls experiencing a mild, flu-like illness during her pregnancy. The histopathology examination of the same virus reveals the following cells. Given this history, clinical presentation and histopathology findings, what is the most probable diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture18_uHmvwD0.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Congenital Rubella Syndrome: While congenital rubella can cause microcephaly, it is typically associated with additional features such as cataracts, heart defects, and hearing loss.
• Option C: Congenital Syphilis: Congenital syphilis can lead to a variety of symptoms, including rash, hepatosplenomegaly, and bone abnormalities, but microcephaly is not as characteristic as in congenital CMV.
• Option D: Congenital Toxoplasmosis: This condition can also cause microcephaly, but the mother's flu-like illness is more suggestive of a viral etiology like CMV.
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of microcephaly, fever, irritability, and the mother's history of a mild flu-like illness during pregnancy is suggestive of Congenital CMV Infection.
• OP Ghai, Page No-264-265.
explanation.ref: OP Ghai, Page No-264-265.

subject: Paediatrics
question: In a rural health center in Uttar Pradesh, India, a 5-year-old child presents with a two-day history of sore throat and low-grade fever. The healthcare provider notes a grayish-white membrane covering the child's tonsils and pharynx, accompanied by significant cervical lymphadenopathy. The child has received only one dose of the diphtheria vaccine due to limited healthcare access. Diphtheria is suspected. To confirm the diagnosis, a throat swab is taken. Which special staining technique should be used to identify the diphtheria bacillus in a laboratory setting?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Ponder’s stain - Ponder's stain are used to demonstrate the metachromatic granules formed in the polar regions
• Option B: Neisser stain - A microbiological stain for visualising the polar bodies in the cytoplasm of gram-positive bacteria . This staining is used to identify diphtheria bacteria. Cells are stained with methylene blue and crystal violet in acidic solution and then counterstained with chrysoidin solution.
• Option C: Albert’s stain - A kind of differential stain used for staining and identifying metachromatic granules. The granules appear purple-black when exposed to Albert's stain and against the light green cytoplasm . Albert stain only acts to stain metachromatic granules and no other granules in the bacteria.
• Educational Objective:
• The presence of a sore throat, low-grade fever, and a grayish-whitemembrane covering the tonsils and throat, that bleeds on peeling - called as pseudomembrane along with enlarged cervical lymph nodes giving a characteristic Bull’s neck appearance are all consistent with diphtheria. Ponder’s stain, Neisser stain and Albert stain are the special stains used for diphtheria. Treatment of diphtheria involves the administration of diphtheria antitoxin and antibiotics - Penicillin/Erythromycin . It is also important to isolate the infected individual to prevent the spread of the disease to others. Diphtheria is a serious and potentially life-threatening bacterial infection that requires immediate medical attention.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1345-1348.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1345-1348.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 5-year-old child in a rural healthcare clinic near Pune, India, presents with a persistent cough that has lasted for 3 weeks. The child, who has a history of missing several scheduled vaccinations due to accessibility issues, also exhibits a fever of 38.5°C and difficulty breathing. The mother reports that the cough is worse at night and the child has had intermittent episodes of wheezing. On examination, crackles are heard upon auscultation in the right upper lobe, and decreased breath sounds are noted. A chest X-ray has been performed, showing an area of infiltration in the right upper lobe, as shown below. Based on the clinical findings and imaging, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture19_m1UtOu8.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Bronchial asthma , typically presents with wheezing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness.
• Option B: Pneumonia , presents with cough, fever, and chest pain, but the chest X-ray findings are typically different from that of tuberculosis. Also, inpneumonia, there won’t be persistent cough for 3 weeks.
• Option D: Congenital heart disease , presents with cyanosis, poor feeding, and failure to thrive, but not with persistent cough and infiltrates on chest X-ray.
• Educational Objective:
• Persistent cough, fever, and signs of respiratory distress, along with a history of incomplete vaccination and specific radiographic findings of infiltration of right upper lobe , in a high TB-prevalence area, pulmonary tuberculosi s is the most likely diagnosis.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1445-1460
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1445-1460 .

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 15-year-old boy is brought to a primary health center in Kerala, India, after experiencing several days of sore throat, significant fatigue, and noticeable swelling in the lymph nodes. The patient's family also reports the development of a rash following administration of ampicillin, which was prescribed by a local practitioner for suspected bacterial pharyngitis. Upon examination, the healthcare provider notes marked hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal atypical lymphocytosis. Given the clinical presentation, history, and laboratory findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Strep throat: Caused by Streptococcus pyogenes, strep throat presents with sore throat and may have swollen lymph nodes but is less likely to cause hepatosplenomegaly, atypical lymphocytosis, or rash following ampicillin administration.
• Option C : Influenza: While influenza can cause sore throat, fatigue, and lymphadenopathy, it typically presents with more systemic symptoms such as fever, body aches, and cough. Hepatosplenomegaly and atypical lymphocytosis are not characteristic findings.
• Option D: Common cold: The common cold usually presents with upper respiratory symptoms such as runny nose, sneezing, and cough, with less emphasis on sore throat, significant fatigue, and without the hepatosplenomegaly and atypical lymphocytosis seen in infectious mononucleosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of infectious mononucleosis, including sore throat, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, hepatosplenomegaly, and atypical lymphocytosis, particularly noting the characteristic rash following ampicillin administration, which differentiates it from other common causes of pharyngitis such as strep throat, influenza, and the common cold.
explanation.ref: O P Ghai 9 th Edition, Page No- 212 .

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the peripheral smear findings and its associated conditions in column A and B:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture26_ZG3HzGC.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) A3 B4 C2 D1
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Macro-ovalocytes and Howell-Jolly bodies are typically found in conditions where there is abnormal red blood cell maturation or after splenectomy , which is often seen in megaloblastic anemia due to folate or vitamin B12 deficiency.
• Option B: Heinz bodies and bite cells are a classic finding in G6PD deficiency , as oxidative stress causes denaturation of hemoglobin , which precipitates within the cell, and portions of these cells are bitten off (particularly by the spleen), leading to 'bite cells'.
• Option C: Target cells are commonly seen in a number of conditions including thalassemia and liver disease . They are named for the bullseye appearance created by an excess of red blood cell membrane.
• Option D: Spherocytes are most commonly associated with hereditary spherocytosis but can also be present in autoimmune hemolyticanemia (AIHA), where antibodies against red blood cells cause them to assume a sphere shape.
• Educational Objective:
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition page page 10,036
• Nelson 21 st Edition page page 10,036

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine visit at a community health center, a mother expresses concern about her 7-year-old child's increased fatigability. Clinical examination reveals significant pallor. Laboratory tests, including a peripheral blood smear, indicate microcytic hypochromic anemia, characterized by smaller than normal and pale red blood cells, along with an elevated red cell distribution width (RDW), suggesting variability in RBC size. Given these findings, which type of microcytic anemia is most commonly encountered in clinical practice?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sickle cell anemia : This is a type of hemolyticanemia caused by a genetic mutation leading to abnormal hemoglobin formation . The RBCs are not typically microcytic but sickle shaped.
• Option C. Thalassemia : Thalassemia also causes microcytic anemia and is prevalent in certain regions of India . However, it is characterized by a normal or low RDW, unlike iron deficiency anemia, which shows a high RDW.
• Option D. Hemolyticanemia : This refers to anemia due to the increased destruction of RBCs . It can present in various forms and is not specifically associated with the microcytic hypochromic picture.
• Educational Objective:
• Iron deficiency anemia is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia , characterized by small, pale RBCs and a high RDW , distinguishing it from other forms of anemia such as sickle cell anemia, thalassemia, and hemolyticanemia.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother from a rural community in Maharashtra brings her 8-year-old child to the regional hospital. The child has been suffering from severe bone pain, fever, and noticeable yellowing of the skin. On examination the child had the following clinical findings. Laboratory analysis, including a peripheral blood smear, indicates microcytic anemia, with red blood cells demonstrating a distinctive crescent shape and the presence of target cells. With regard to the child's condition, which of the following statements is incorrect?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture21_OAjvxMa.jpg]
options: This are the clinical features of sickle cell anemia
The child is at risk for developing acute chest syndrome
Hydroxyurea can’t be used in the management of the disease
The child is at risk for developing stroke

explanation.short: Ans. C) Hydroxyurea is contraindicated in the management of the disease
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. These are the clinical features of sickle cell anemia : True , the presentation of severe bone pain, fever, jaundice, and the peripheral smear findings are indicative of sickle cell anemia.
• Option B. The child is at risk for developing acute chest syndrome : True , patients with sickle cell anemia are at increased risk for acute chest syndrome, a severe and life-threatening complication.
• Option D. The child is at risk for developing stroke : True , children with sickle cell anemia have a higher risk of stroke due to the occlusion of blood vessels in the brain by sickled cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with sickle cell disease commonly present with bone pain , fever , and jaundice due to hemolyticanemia and vaso-occlusive crises . Hydroxyurea is an important drug in the management of sickle cell anemia , as it reduces the frequency of pain episodes and the risk of acute complications .
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her 6-year-old child to a local clinic with concerns about the child's noticeably distended abdomen and a history of recurrent infections. Upon physical examination, the child exhibits notable pallor and splenomegaly. The physical examination findings of the child revealed the following image. Additionally, an X-ray of the skull shows abnormal bone structure with a "crew cut" appearance, indicative of bone marrow expansion. A peripheral blood smear reveals microcytic hypochromic red blood cells and target cells. Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture22.jpg]
options: The child is at risk of developing gallstones
Iron supplements are indicated in the management of the disease
The child may require blood transfusions

explanation.short: Ans. C) Iron supplements are indicated in the management of the disease.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The child may have beta-thalassemia : True , the clinical findings of facial bone deformities, splenomegaly, and a blood smear showing microcytic hypochromic RBCs with target cells are consistent with beta-thalassemia.
• Option B. The child is at risk of developing gallstones : True , chronic hemolysis associated with beta-thalassemia increases the risk of bilirubinate gallstones.
• Option D. The child may require blood transfusions : True , patients with beta-thalassemia major often require regular blood transfusions to manage severe anemia.
• Educational Objective:
• Beta-thalassemia can present with facial bone deformities , splenomegaly , and specific blood smear findings . Management often involves regular blood transfusions , while iron supplementation is not typically indicated due to the risk of iron overload.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-342
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-342

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old child with a known family history of hereditary spherocytosis is brought to the clinic by his mother. The child presents with clinical signs of pallor and jaundice. Laboratory evaluation including a peripheral smear demonstrates the presence of spherocytes and a notably increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC). Given the family history and clinical presentation, which of the following statements is incorrect in relation to the child's condition?
options: The child may require a splenectomy.
The condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern
Spectrin mutation can lead to this condition
The peripheral smear may reveal target cells

explanation.short: Ans. D) The peripheral smear may reveal target cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The child may require a splenectomy : True , splenectomy is a treatment option for hereditary spherocytosis to decrease hemolysis.
• Option B. The condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern: True , although it can also be inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
• Option C. Spectrin mutation can lead to this condition: True , spectrin mutations are a common cause of hereditary spherocytosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The hereditary spherocytosis , often characterized by spherocytes on the peripheral smear and increased MCHC, is commonly inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and may involve spectrin mutations . Splenectomy can be part of the management ; however, target cells are not a feature of this condition and their presence would suggest an alternative or additional diagnosis.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 337
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 337

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 12-year-old child is brought to the clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his yellow-tinted skin and a visibly enlarged abdomen. On physical examination, the physician notes excessive pallor and an enlarged spleen. The peripheral smear reveals the following image with presence of spherocytes and reticulocytosis. Based on the child’s clinical features and the findings on the peripheral smear, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture24_FHrdQtQ.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sickle cell anemia : This is usually characterized by the presence of sickle-shaped red blood cells , which are not noted in the provided peripheral smear image.
• Option C. G6PD deficiency : This condition typically presents with episodic hemolysis triggered by certain drugs or foods , and blood smears might show 'bite cells', not the spherocytes as shown.
• Option D. Thalassemia : It commonly presents with microcytic and hypochromic red cells and target cells , which differ from the spherocytes seen in hereditary spherocytosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary spherocytosis is recognized by the classic triad of jaundice , splenomegaly , and the presence of spherocytes on a peripheral blood smear . The increased reticulocyte count further supports the diagnosis by indicating an increased red blood cell turnover.
• Ref; Nelson 20 th Edition Page no- From 2311.
• Nelson 20 th Edition Page no- From 2311.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A child is brought to the clinic with a familial history of anemia. On examination, the healthcare provider notes the presence of spherocytes in the blood smear. Further genetic analysis reveals a defect in the ankyrin protein of the red blood cells. With this information, a diagnosis of hereditary spherocytosis is confirmed. Regarding hereditary spherocytosis, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Autosomal recessive is the most common type of inheritance
May be diagnosed by incubated osmotic fragility test
Can be diagnosed by flow cytometry
Presence of Spherocytes in PS has a differential diagnosis of AIHA.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Autosomal recessive is the most common type of inheritance
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. May be diagnosed by incubated osmotic fragility test : True, this test can demonstrate increased fragility of red blood cells in a hypotonic solution , a hallmark of hereditary spherocytosis.
• Option C. Can be diagnosed by flow cytometry: True , flow cytometry can be used to diagnose hereditary spherocytosis by detecting deficiencies in membrane proteins.
• Option D. Presence of Spherocytes in PS has a differential diagnosis of AIHA: True , while spherocytes are characteristic of hereditary spherocytosis, they can also be seen in autoimmune hemolyticanemia, making it a part of the differential diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary spherocytosis , typically inherited as an autosomal dominant fashion and can be diagnosed with osmotic fragility tests or flow cytometry . Spherocytes on a peripheral smear can indicate hereditary spherocytosis but also need to be differentiated from autoimmune hemolytic anemia .
• Hereditary Spherocytosis- Ankyrin mutation (M/C-70% cases)- AD
• Herdeitary Spherocytosis- Spectrin mutation (Most severe)- AR
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 337.
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 337.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 6-year-old child is brought to the clinic with sudden onset pallor and dark urine. The child also exhibits jaundice and pallor on physical examination. The mother mentions that the child recently consumed fava beans, which they had not eaten before. Laboratory results, including a peripheral blood smear, as shown in the image. Based on the child's clinical presentation, history of recent dietary change, and the peripheral smear findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture25_CuhkLru.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sickle cell anemia : This condition usually presents with sickle-shaped cells on the smear , which is not depicted in the image.
• Option B. Hereditary spherocytosis : Characterized by spherocytes on blood smear , hereditary spherocytosis is not typically triggered by dietary factors.
• Option D. Thalassemia : This genetic condition causes chronic hemolyticanemia with microcytic and hypochromic cells and target cells, but acute episodes are not usually precipitated by food intake.
• Educational Objective:
• G6PD deficiency presents as hemolytic anemia precipitated by fava bean consumption , and diagnosed by the characteristic 'bite cells' on a peripheral blood smear.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 338
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 338

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician evaluates a 5-year-old child who has been experiencing recurrent nosebleeds and easy bruising. Upon physical examination, the child has noticeable petechiae spread across various skin surfaces. The clinician orders a CBC, which reveals thrombocytopenia. Prolonged BT, while CT, PT, and aPTT are within normal ranges. The child's mother also mentions that the child's elder sister, Sana, exhibits similar symptoms, suggesting a potential hereditary pattern. Which genetic disorder should be considered in the differential diagnosis for this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome: This is a rare autosomal recessive disorder characterized by oculocutaneous albinism , visual impairment , and a bleeding diathesis due to platelet dysfunction ; however, it is typically associated with additional systemic symptoms.
• Option C: Hemophilia A: This is an X-linked recessive disorder that primarily affects males and is characterized by deficiency of clotting factor VIII , which would result in abnormal PT and aPTT, not typically seen in this case.
• Option D: Hemophilia B: Similar to Hemophilia A, Hemophilia B is also an X-linked recessive disorder affecting males with a deficiency of clotting factor IX, which also would not present with normal PT and aPTT levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Bernard-Soulier syndrome is a genetic disorder characterized by recurrent bleeding , thrombocytopenia , and prolonged bleeding time , with normal coagulation studies, in a child with a family history of similar symptoms. This condition should be differentiated from other coagulopathies that present with abnormal clotting factor levels, such as Hemophilia A and B.
• Ref; 21 st edition nelson, page number 10,297

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 8-year-old child is presented to a pediatric clinic by their mother with a history of a recent viral infection, which was shortly followed by the sudden onset of petechiae and purpura. During the clinical evaluation, the physician notes that the petechiae are non-palpable and there is mild splenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal a white blood cell count of 13,000/mm 3 and a platelet count of 78,000/mm 3 . C-reactive protein (CRP) is also found to be elevated. Which of the following findings, if present, would most likely exclude a diagnosis of Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Platelet and WBC counts: Thrombocytopenia is a hallmark of ITP , and a mildly elevated WBC count can be a response to infection or stress, not necessarily ruling out ITP.
• Option C. Non-palpable petechiae: Non-palpable petechiae are consistent with a diagnosis of ITP , as they are a common clinical feature of thrombocytopenia.
• Option D. Elevation of CRP: CRP can be elevated in many conditions , including ITP , as it is an acute-phase reactant and can indicate inflammation or infection .
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of ITP, typically includes isolated thrombocytopenia without other significant physical findings such as splenomegaly . Recognizing that splenomegaly is not a characteristic feature of ITP is important for the differential diagnosis of thrombocytopenia.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition page 3800
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 3800

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old girl is brought to a clinic by her mother due to concerns about the child's failure to thrive and persistent fatigue. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes severe anemia, splenomegaly, and jaundice. The mother mentions a vague family history of a "blood problem" but lacks specific details. Due to the complexity of the case, the child is referred to a hematologist for a more thorough evaluation. Hemoglobin electrophoresis results come back normal, but the CBC shows normocytic anemia, and the peripheral smear reveals the presence of bizarre erythrocytes, including spiculated cells. These findings suggest that the child's anemia may be due to an enzyme deficiency. Given the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, what is the most common enzyme deficiency that could result in this type of anemia?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency: This is the most common enzyme deficiency worldwide , leading to hemolytic anemia often triggered by certain foods , drugs , or infections . However, the peripheral smear typically shows "bite cells" and "blister cells," not the bizarre erythrocytes as described.
• Option B. Glucose-6-Phosphatase: Deficiency in this enzyme is associated with glycogen storage diseases , particularly von Gierke disease, and not typically with hemolyticanemia.
• Option C. Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC): Deficiency affects the ability to convert pyruvate to acetyl-CoA , leading to neurological deficits and lactic acidosis, rather than hemolyticanemia.
• Educational Objective:
• Pyruvate kinase deficiency is a cause of normocytic anemia with bizarre erythrocytes on peripheral smear , leading to symptoms of anemia , splenomegaly , and jaundice . This condition should be distinguished from other enzyme deficiencies like G6PD deficiency, which presents differently on peripheral blood smears.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page  No 10062-10065
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page  No 10062-10065

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-year-old girl is brought to a clinic in a malaria-prevalent region with symptoms of fever, chills, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. Given the local endemic conditions, a rapid antigen test for malaria is conducted, and the result necessitates the initiation of primaquine treatment. Four days into the therapy, the patient develops hematuria, and laboratory tests reveal a significant drop in hemoglobin levels from 11.3 g/dL to 7.12 g/dL, an increase in total bilirubin from 1.38 mg/dL to 4.52 mg/dL, and an elevation in lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) from 236 IU/L to 509 IU/L. Based on the clinical presentation and the onset of symptoms following primaquine administration, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC): PDC deficiency affects the mitochondrial conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA , leading to neurological symptoms and lactic acidosis , not hemolyticanemia.
• Option B: Pyruvate kinase deficiency: This is another cause of hemolyticanemia but is less likely to be triggered specifically by primaquine treatment.
• Option D: Glucose-6-Phosphatase deficiency: This deficiency is associated with glycogen storage disease type I (Von Gierke disease), which presents with hypoglycemia and metabolic abnormalities , not primaquine-induced hemolysis.
• Educational Objective:
• G6PD deficiency, particularly hemolytic anemia triggered by oxidative stressors such as primaquine in patients with malaria .
• Ref; Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page  No 10068-10069
• Nelson 21 st edition, Chapter 490 – Page  No 10068-10069

subject: Paediatrics
question: Manthra, a 13-year-old, is rushed to the emergency department by her mother due to recurrent episodes of nosebleeds (epistaxis), blood in urine (hematuria), and blood in stools (hematochezia). The mother also recalls significant bleeding from the umbilical stump shortly after Manthra's birth. A comprehensive blood panel reveals normal platelet counts and coagulation times, including Prothrombin Time (PT), Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT), Thrombin Time (TT), and fibrinogen levels. However, a urea clot lysis test indicated positive results. Considering these findings, which clotting factor deficiency is most likely present in Manthra?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Factor IX: Deficiency leads to Hemophilia B , which would typically result in prolonged APTT.
• Option B: Factor X: Deficiency would affect both the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways , leading to abnormalities in PT and APTT.
• Option C: Factor VIII: Deficiency causes Hemophilia A , also typically presenting with prolonged APTT.
• Educational Objective:
• Factor XIII deficiencies clinically characterized by normal initial clot formation tests but abnormal clot stability , leading to recurrent and delayed bleeding episodes . The urea clot lysis test can help diagnose this condition by demonstrating the clot's inability to resist lysis, indicating a deficiency in fibrin stabilization.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No-10225.
• Nelson 21 st Edition. Page No-10225.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old child is brought to the clinic by their mother due to fever, swelling, and pain in the leg, particularly with movement. Physical examination reveals signs of inflammation, and a complete blood count (CBC) indicates increased neutrophil levels and microcytic anemia. Suspecting osteomyelitis, further inquiries are made into the child's medical history. The mother mentions a familial history of anemia, which, upon further testing, is attributed to a missense mutation at the beta globin's 6th position - a hallmark of sickle cell anemia. Considering the specific type of anemia diagnosed due to this genetic mutation, which organism is most commonly associated with osteomyelitis in such cases?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Streptococcus pyogenes: While it can cause various infections , it is less commonly associated with osteomyelitis in patients with sickle cell anemia.
• Option B: Staphylococcus aureus: Although it is a common cause of osteomyelitis , in patients with sickle cell anemia, Salmonella species are more prevalent.
• Option C: Pseudomonas: This organism is more commonly associated with osteomyelitis in individuals with specific risk factors such as intravenous drug use or puncture wounds, not specifically with sickle cell anemia.
• Educational Objective:
• The patients with sickle cell anemia , characterized by a missense mutation at the beta globin's 6th position, have an increased risk of developing osteomyelitis due to Salmonella species , differentiating it from the more common causative agent, Staphylococcus aureus, in the general population.
• Ref- Nelson 21 st Edition page 6044
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 6044

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother brings her 3-year-old child to the pediatric department due to recurrent infections and noticeable dilated veins over the chest. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes pallor, petechial hemorrhages, and hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal anemia, thrombocytopenia, and a white blood cell count of less than 50,000/cu.mm. These findings lead to a suspicion of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL). To confirm the diagnosis of ALL, what key feature would the pediatrician expect to find in the bone marrow aspirate of this child?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) is confirmed by the presence of a high percentage of lymphoblasts in the bone marrow aspirate . Specifically, the World Health Organization (WHO) criteria for the diagnosis of ALL include the presence of more than 25% lymphoblasts in the bone marrow. This high percentage of immature lymphoid cells is indicative of ALL, distinguishing it from other hematological disorders.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-days-old baby with intellectual disability, mongoloid slant of the eyes, sandal gap toes, and distinctive facial features consistent with Down syndrome presents to the clinic. The baby exhibits pallor and small petechiae, raising concern. Routine blood tests reveal an increased leukocyte count with the presence of blast cells, and further examination identifies hepatosplenomegaly. Given these findings in a baby with Down syndrome, what is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and Option C. Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) are possible hematolog ical malignancies in children with Down syndrome . However, the transient nature of the symptoms and the age of the child suggest TMD as the most likely initial diagnosis. It is important to monitor the child for potential progression to AML.
• Educational Objective:
• Transient myeloproliferative disorder is characterized by high leukocyte counts, blasts cells in peripheral blood with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and hepatosplenomegaly.
• Resolves within first 3 months. It has GATA 1 mutation. Require monitoring (20% may develop acute myeloid leukemia by 3 years of age).
• Ref; Ghai 9 th edition – Page no – 600.
• Ghai 9 th edition – Page no – 600.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A couple, after 17 years of marriage, welcomes a baby born with features indicative of Down syndrome. Due to residing in a rural area, the mother had not attended any prenatal visits and was unaware of the diagnosis until after the birth. Upon confirming the diagnosis of Down syndrome, you discuss the characteristics of the condition and potential future complications with the mother. Concerned about the association between Down syndrome and increased risk of hematological malignancies, the mother inquires about the most common type of blood cancer her child might be at risk for. How would you respond?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Chronic lymphoblastic leukemia (CLL) is much more common in adults and is rare in children, including those with Down syndrome.
• Option C: Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is also more common in adults and not typically associated with an increased risk in individuals with Down syndrome.
• Option D: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) does occur at a higher rate in individuals with Down syndrome, particularly in the form of transient myeloproliferative disorder in newborns , which can progress to AML. However, ALL is still considered the most common leukemia in children with Down syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• The children with Down syndrome are at an elevated risk for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) compared to the general pediatric population. This knowledge is important for guiding the clinical monitoring and care of children with Down syndrome to promptly identify and treat potential complications.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old child is brought to the clinic by his mother due to a notable decrease in activity level compared to peers, along with easy fatigability and a tendency to bruise. Suspecting anemia, you order a complete blood count (CBC), which reveals an RBC count of 2.0 X 10^12/L, a WBC count of 55,000 per microliter, and a platelet count of 70,000 per microliter. Further examination shows lymphadenopathy and bone pain. Based on these clinical findings and laboratory results, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is a known good prognostic factor for this condition?
options: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia and hyper diploidy
Chronic lymphoblastic leukaemia and female gender
Chronic lymphoblastic leukaemia and male gender

explanation.short: Ans. A) Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia and hyper diploidy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Chronic lymphoblastic leukaemia (CLL) and female gender: CLL is rare in children and typically occurs in older adults. Gender is not a significant prognostic factor in CLL.
• Option C: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL) and hypodiploidy: Hypodiploidy, or fewer chromosomes than normal, is generally associated with a poorer prognosis in ALL.
• Option D: Chronic lymphoblastic leukaemia (CLL) and male gender: As mentioned, CLL is uncommon in children , and male gender is not known to be a significant prognostic factor in CLL.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation and laboratory findings of low RBC count, high WBC count with lymphadenopathy, bone pain, and easy bruising and fatigability are indicative of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia (ALL), a common childhood leukaemia. Hyper diploidy, or the presence of more than the normal number of chromosomes in leukemic cells , is considered a good prognostic factor in ALL, associated with a better response to treatment and improved outcomes.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830-4832.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830-4832.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
In a pediatric clinic, a 3-year-old child with distinctive facial features, including a mongoloid slant of eyes and what appears to be gingival hyperplasia, is brought in by his mother due to recurrent nosebleeds and general fatigue. Upon examination, the child also exhibits intellectual disability and characteristic sandal gap toes. A complete blood count reveals anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukocytosis with a predominance of 75% blast cells. Considering the child's physical presentation and blood findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture4.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: In case of Acute lymphoblastic leukemia , anemia and thrombocytopenia are seen and is most common leukemia in children but it is not associated with gum hypertrophy.
• Option C: Hodgkins Lymphoma presents with continuous lymph node involvement and is not associated with gum hypertrophy.
• Option D: Chronic myeloid leukemia, anemia and thrombocytopenia are seen but is most prevalent in elderly patients and is not a ssociated with gum hypertrophy.
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of distinctive facial features consistent with Down syndrome, recurrent bleeding, fatigue, and the CBC findings of anemia, thrombocytopenia, and high blast cell count is suggestive of acute myeloid leukemia (AML). AML is more common in children with Down syndrome, and the specific clinical presentation of gingival hyperplasia can be associated with the monocytic subtypes of AML, such as acute myelomonocytic leukemia (AMML) or acute monoblastic leukemia (AMoL), also classified as M4 or M5 in the French American-British (FAB) classification, respectively.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 6-year-old child from Africa is brought by his parents for medical consultation, presenting with pronounced jaw swelling and ulceration as evident in the provided image. The child also exhibits difficulty breathing due to partial airway obstruction. A biopsy is performed, and histopathological analysis confirms a diagnosis of B-cell Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL). Based on the clinical presentation and biopsy findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and which chromosomal translocation is associated with this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture23.jpg, https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture10.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Burkitt’s lymphoma, especially the classic presentation is of jaw swelling in African children , and its associated with the chromosomal translocation t(8;14).
• Nelson 21 st Edition page number 6904
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page number 6904

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 5-year-old child is evaluated for intellectual disability, a mongoloid slant of the eyes, sandal gap toes, and distinctive facial features indicative of a certain genetic condition. Given these clinical signs, which type of myeloid lineage leukemia is most commonly associated with this condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture2.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Acute erythroid leukemia: This subtype, involving the proliferation of erythroid precursors , is less common in the general population and not specifically associated with Down syndrome .
• Option C: Acute monocytic leukemia: While this subtype (M5 under the FAB classification) involves the proliferation of monocyte precursors, it is not the most common in children with Down syndrome.
• Option D: Acute myelomonocytic leukemia: This subtype (M4 under the FAB classification) involves both monocyte and granulocyte precursors but is not the most prevalent myeloid leukemia in Down syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Children with Down syndrome have an increased incidence of leukemia, particularly Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) and Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), with Acute MegakaryoblasticLeukemia (AMKL) being the most common AML subtype.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615 and Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No 610-615 and Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 16-year-old boy presents with weight loss, fever, night sweats, and lethargy. Physical examination reveals a few firm, rubbery lymph nodes. A lymph node biopsy shows the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells, and subsequent PET CT identifies the involvement of four additional lymph nodes on the same side of the diaphragm as shown in the image. Suspecting Hodgkin's lymphoma, you proceed to stage the disease for treatment planning. Based on the information and evaluation provided, what is the stage of the lymphoma in this patient?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture5.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Stage 1: This stage is characterized by the involvement of a single lymph node region , which does not match the patient's findings.
• Option C: Stage 3: Involvement on both sides of the diaphragm or additional extralymphatic organ involvement , which is not indicated here.
• Option D: Stage 4: Diffuse or disseminated involvement of one or more extralymphatic organs , which is not indicated in the provided clinical scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• Based on PET CT staging is done.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4835-4840.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4835-4840.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 34-year-old mother brought her 2-month-old infant to your pediatric clinic, who reports that the baby developed a fever on Sunday, followed by the eruption of a distinctive rash on Friday. The mother is concerned and suspects that the rash might be related to her sister-in-law, who frequently kisses the child on the cheeks and playfully pinches and slaps him. Upon examination, the child presents with bright red erythema on both cheeks, giving a "slapped" appearance, without significant involvement of the nasal bridge and circumoral region. Based on the clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture11_Dug4UFj.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Kissing disease: Also known as infectious mononucleosis , it is caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). It typically presents with fever, pharyngitis, and lymphadenopathy, not primarily with a cheek rash.
• Option C: Battered baby syndrome: This term refers to physical abuse of infants, leading to multiple injuries. The described scenario and rash do not fit with this diagnosis .
• Option D: Juvenile SLE: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) can affect children, but the classic malar rash associated with SLE spares the nasolabial folds, which is different from the described presentation.
• Educational Objective:
• The "Slapped Cheek" appearance is characteristic of Parvovirus B19 infection , also known as erythema infectiosum or Fifth disease; commonly affects children and presents with a distinctive facial rash that resembles the appearance as if the cheeks have been slapped . The rash can subsequently spread to the body, forming a lacy pattern.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1570-1588
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1570-1588

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother brings her 6-year-old child to the pediatric clinic with complaints of a spreading rash. The rash, characterized by small, itchy, fluid-filled blisters, started on the face and has now covered the trunk and limbs. Accompanying symptoms include fever and fatigue. Notably, the rash exhibits lesions at various stages of development. Given these symptoms, what is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Impetigo , is a bacterial skin infection that typically presents with honey-colored crusts on the skin. It does not usually cause a rash consisting of fluid-filled blisters.
• Option B: Measles , is a viral infection that can cause a rash, fever, and other symptoms. However, the rash in measles typically starts on the head and then spreads to the rest of the body. The rash consists of larger, flat, red spots that may merge together.
• Option D: Hand, Foot and Mouth disease (HFMD) is a viral infection caused by coxsackie A16 virus that typically causes a rash on the hands, feet, and in the mouth . It does not usually cause a rash on other parts of the body.
• Educational Objective
• Fever accompanied by a pruritic (itchy) rash that starts on the face and spreads to the trunk and limbs , with lesions in various stages of development (including fluid-filled blisters and crusted lesions), is highly indicative of Varicella, also known as chickenpox, which is caused by the Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV).
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1579-1585.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1579-1585.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 10-year-old child is brought to the clinic by their mother due to pain and swelling below the ears and near the jawline, accompanied by fever and difficulty swallowing. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes swollen and tender salivary glands, leading to a diagnosis of Mumps. Given this diagnosis, which of the following is the most common complication of mumps in pediatric patients?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Orchitis: While orchitis, an inflammation of the testicles , is a recognized complication of mumps , it primarily affects post-pubertal males and is less common in pre-pubertal children.
• Option B: Oophoritis: Oophoritis, or inflammation of the ovaries, can occur in females as a complication of mumps but is less frequent than aseptic meningitis.
• Option D: SSPE: Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare , progressive brain disorder related to measles infection , not mumps, and is therefore not relevant to this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Mumps, a viral infection primarily affecting the parotid salivary glands, can also impact the central nervous system, l eading to symptoms such as headache, fever, and neck stiffness indicative of meningitis . However, most cases of aseptic meningitis related to mumps are self-limiting and resolve without long-term sequelae.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1552-1553.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1552-1553.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother is concerned about her 3-year-old son, who has had hearing loss, cataracts, and heart defects since birth. During a clinic visit, the child presents with a low-grade fever, rash, and lymphadenopathy. Considering the combination of congenital conditions and current symptoms, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Mumps: Primarily affects the salivary glands and can lead to complications such as orchitis, meningitis, and pancreatitis , but it is not associated with the congenital anomalies described.
• Option C: Measles (Rubeola): Characterized by a high fever, cough, conjunctivitis, and a rash that starts on the face and spreads downwards . It does not cause the congenital defects mentioned.
• Option D: Chickenpox (Varicella): Presents with an itchy rash that turns into f luid-filled blisters and eventually crusts over, but is not known to cause congenital heart defects, cataracts, and hearing loss.
• Educational Objective:
• Hearing loss, cataracts, and heart defects , are classic findings in Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS).
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1579-1585.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 1579-1585.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A new mother is concerned about her 1-week-old infant who has been experiencing fever, poor feeding, and a vesicular rash on the face and trunk. She reveals that she had a similar rash during her pregnancy and at the time of delivery. The Tzanck smear of the lesion is shown below. Based on the clinical presentation of the infant and the mother's history, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture1_NxXQn9o.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. C) Congenital Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) Infection
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Congenital Rubella Syndrome: Typically presents with cataracts, heart defects, and hearing loss, and not with the vesicular rash seen in herpes infections.
• Option B: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection: Can cause a variety of symptoms, including hearing loss, microcephaly, and hepatosplenomegaly . While CMV can cause a petechial rash known as "blueberry muffin" spots, it does not cause the vesicular rash seen in herpes infections.
• Option D: Congenital Toxoplasmosis: Often presents with chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and intracranial calcifications , and does not present with vesicular rash characteristic of herpes infections.
• Educational Objective:
• The Tzanck smear image showing multinucleated giant cells is indicative of herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection. The presence of a vesicular rash in both the mother during pregnancy a nd the newborn , alongside the mother's history and the Tzanck smear findings, strongly suggests congenital herpes simplex virus infection . HSV can be transmitted to the infant during delivery if the mother has active genital lesions , leading to neonatal herpes, which can present with fever, poor feeding, and vesicular rash.
• Nelson 21 st Edition, page no 13283
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, page no 13283

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 28-year-old woman visits a clinic in an urban area of Mumbai, India, with a distinctive rash that initiated on her face before spreading to her trunk and limbs. She describes experiencing a low-grade fever and notes palpable lymph node enlargement in her cervical region. During the oropharyngeal examination, the healthcare provider observes small, rose-colored lesions on the palate. The patient is counseled about potential long-term risks associated with the infection, including the rare but serious condition of progressive rubella panencephalitis. Considering the clinical presentation and potential future complications discussed, what is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Measles: Measles can present with a rash , but the rash typically starts on the face and spreads to the trunk and extremities . Measles rash is typically accompanied by high fever, cough, and conjunctivitis.
• Option C: Chickenpox: Chickenpox can present with a rash , but the rash typically starts on the trunk and spreads to the face and extremities . Chickenpox rash is typically accompanied by itching and fever.
• Option D: Scarlet fever: Scarlet fever can present with a rash , but the rash typically presents as a fine, sandpaper-like rash that starts on the trunk and spreads to the extremities . The rash is typically accompanied by a strawberry tongue and peeling of the skin. Scarlet fever rash does not typically start on the face.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical manifestations of rubella , includes facial rash spreading to the trunk and extremities , low-grade fever , lymphadenopathy , and Forchheimer spots , and rare long-term neurological complications like progressive rubella panencephalitis .
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1548-1551
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1548-1551 .

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Vijay, a 5-year-old boy from a village near Chennai, is brought to the pediatric department of a District Hospital. His medical history is notable for hereditary spherocytosis. The mother reports that Vijay has been looking increasingly pale and lethargic. The exacerbation of anemia is suspected, and upon examination, a significant decrease in reticulocyte counts is observed, suggesting an inadequate response to anemia. Given these clinical findings, a bone marrow examination is performed. What findings are most likely to be observed in Vijay's bone marrow?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture23_wUsbZw4.jpg]
options: Acellular bone marrow with reduction of all cell lines
Erythroid hyperplasia as a result of JAK-2 mutation.
Reduced erythropoiesis with giant erythroblasts and intranuclear inclusions
“Owl eye” appearance, due to CMV infection in erythroid precursors

explanation.short: Ans. A) Acellular bone marrow with reduction of all cell lines
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In a patient with hereditary spherocytosis who presents with an exacerbation of anemia and decreased reticulocyte counts , the findings are suggestive of an aplastic crisis with human parvovirus B19 . This condition is characterized by a temporary cessation of red blood cell production , resulting in an acellular bone marrow with a reduction in all cell lines .
• Patients usually recover in 4-6 weeks.
• Option B. Erythroid hyperplasia as a result of JAK-2 mutation : JAK-2 mutations are typically associated with myeloproliferative disorders , not hereditary spherocytosis, and would not result in decreased reticulocyte counts.
• Option C. Reduced erythropoiesis with giant erythroblasts and intranuclear inclusions : These findings are indicative of disorders such as megaloblastic anemia , not hereditary spherocytosis.
• Option D. “ Owl eye” appearance, due to CMV infection in erythroid precursors : This finding is associated with CMV infection , not hereditary spherocytosis, and would not account for the sudden decrease in reticulocytes typical of an aplastic crisis.
• Educational Objective:
• The presentation of an aplastic crisis in a patient with hereditary spherocytosis , characterized by acute worsening of anemia and low reticulocyte counts . Bone marrow biopsy would typically show an acellular marrow with reduction of all cell lines . Understanding this complication is crucial for the appropriate management of patients with hereditary hemolyticanemias.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No-337.
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No-337.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Match the following haematological conditions along with their inheritance pattern:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture28_AqsoM9D.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. D) A3 B4 C1 D2
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition page 4837
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 4837

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old child is brought to a pediatric clinic by their mother due to the appearance of petechiae and a notable degree of pallor. The clinical signs raise suspicion for a microangiopathic hemolyticanemia. As the evaluation progresses, it becomes clear that the child is also suffering from severe acute renal failure. The child presents with neither fever nor neurological symptoms during the physical examination. Given the symptoms of petechiae, pallor indicative of hemolytic anemia, and acute renal failure without fever or neurological involvement, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Autoimmune HemolyticAnemia: This typically does not include renal failure as a primary component.
• Option B: Disseminated intravascular coagulopathy: DIC is a secondary condition that results from another underlying process and is often accompanied by bleeding and clotting simultaneously . It could show similar lab findings but is usually associated with an identifiable cause of widespread coagulation activation, such as sepsis or malignancy, and would typically present with fever if infectious in nature.
• Option D: Autoimmune thrombocytopenic purpura: ITP primarily involves a low platelet count leading to bleeding manifestations and does not typically include hemolyticanemia or renal failure.
• Educational Objective:
• Hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS) is characterized by the triad of microangiopathic hemolyticanemia , thrombocytopenia , and acute renal failure . It often occurs after a gastrointestinal infection , particularly with strains of E. coli that produce Shiga toxin . The absence of fever or neurological manifestations helps to differentiate HUS from other similar conditions, such as thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), which often includes neurological symptoms.
• Ref; nelson 21 st edition page 2718
• nelson 21 st edition page 2718

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 2-year-old boy with diagnosed Down syndrome, characterized by intellectual disability, a mongoloid slant of the eyes, and sandal gap toes, is brought in by his parents for evaluation. The parents, who had not sought further medical advice post-prenatal diagnosis due to the child's gender, express concerns about the risk of leukemia, specifically asking about Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL). Addressing their concerns, you also consider the association of Down syndrome with myeloid leukemias. You then turn to your junior with a teaching question, "What is the most common leukemia of myeloid lineage seen in children with Down syndrome?"

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Acute erythroid leukemia: This is a rare form of acute myeloid leukemia characterized by the proliferation of erythroid precursors, not specifically associated with Down syndrome.
• Option C: Acute myelomonocytic leukemia: While this is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia , it is not the most common in children with Down syndrome.
• Option D: Acute monocytic leukemia: This leukemia subtype involves the proliferation of monocytes and is not the most common myeloid leukemia in children with Down syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• The increased risk of leukemia in children with Down syndrome , with a specific focus on Acute MegakaryoblasticLeukemia (AMKL) as the most common myeloid lineage leukemia in this population. Recognizing the association between genetic syndromes and specific types of leukemia can aid in the early diagnosis and management of these conditions.
• Ref; Nelson 20 th Edition , Page  No 610-615 and Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832.
• Nelson 20 th Edition , Page  No 610-615 and Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4832.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A worried mother came to pediatric department along with her 3-year-old kid with complaints of recurrent infection and dilated veins over the chest. During physical examination of the child, pallor, petechial hemorrhage and hepatosplenomegaly were found. Peripheral blood findings show the presence of anemia, thrombocytopenia and WBC <50,000/cu.mm. You suspect it as a case of ALL. What will you find in the bone marrow aspirate of this child to confirm the diagnosis?

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old child is brought to a government hospital in rural Odisha, India, with a 3-day history of high fever peaking at 39°C, associated with chills, headache, and episodes of vomiting. The mother reports that the child was previously healthy and has no significant medical history. Physical examination reveals pallor and an enlarged spleen. Laboratory investigations including a complete blood count confirm anaemia. A peripheral blood smear is positive for Plasmodium falciparum parasites. Given the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, what is the most appropriate treatment option for this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Chloroquine tablets : While chloroquine was once widely used to treat malaria, it is no longer effective against Plasmodium falciparum due to the development of widespread resistance. As such, it is not a recommended treatment option for this case.
• Option C: Primaquine tablets : Primaquine is typically used as a second-line treatment for malaria, particularly to target the hypnozoite stage of the parasite in the liver. However, it is not effective against Plasmodium falciparum, which is the parasite causing the current case. Therefore, it is not an appropriate treatment option in this case.
• Option D: Symptomatic management . As this is a case of severe malaria, Symptomatic management can’t be used as a treatment modality.
• Educational Objective:
• Treat Plasmodium falciparum malaria with the appropriate first-line therapy, which is Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy (ACT), particularly in regions with chloroquine resistance. Recognize that specific anti-malarial treatment is necessary alongside supportive care in managing acute malaria.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1712-1719.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1712-1719.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old boy who has completed treatment for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) is brought in for a follow-up visit six months post-therapy. Despite completing the treatment regimen, his bone marrow aspirate reveals a concerning 35% presence of lymphoblasts. Given this finding, which indicates a relapse of the disease, what would be the next appropriate step in managing this child's condition?
options: High dose methotrexate and high dose cytarabine
Low dose methotrexate and high dose cytarabine

explanation.short: Ans. A) High dose methotrexate and high dose cytarabine
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Low-dose methotrexate only: This would be insufficient for treating relapsed ALL, as it typically requires a more aggressive treatment regimen.
• Option C: High-dose methotrexate only: While high-dose methotrexate is a component of reinduction therapy, it is usually combined with other chemotherapeutic agents for greater efficacy in the case of relapse.
• Option D: Low-dose methotrexate and high-dose cytarabine: Low-dose methotrexate alone is not adequate for relapsed ALL and is not typically combined with high-dose cytarabine for this purpose.
• Educational Objective:
• Management of relapsed Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia in a pediatric patient - A combination of high-dose methotrexate and high-dose cytarabine is part of the standard reinduction therapy to achieve a second remission in patients with relapsed ALL .
• Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter-161, Page No-4830

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 3-year-old child presents to a pediatric outpatient department with a 3-day history of high fever, runny nose, red eyes, and small white spots inside the mouth. On examination, a red, blotchy rash is noted on the skin. The child's immunization history is incomplete. Based on the clinical presentation, which of the following is considered a pathognomonic sign of the suspected disease?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture10.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Nagayama spots: These are small, red papules found on the soft palate and uvula, typically seen in cases of Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), caused by human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6), not measles.
• Option C: Forchheimer spots : Petechiae on the soft palate, often associated with rubella (German measles) , not the classic measles described in the scenario.
• Option D: Conjunctivitis: While conjunctivitis can be a symptom of measles, it is not pathognomon ic as it can be present in various viral and bacterial infections.
• Educational Objective:
• Koplik spots are considered as a hallmark feature in diagnosing measles, especially in the context of the clinical presentation, including fever, rhinorrhoea, conjunctivitis, and a characteristic rash.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1542-1547.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1542-1547.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother visits a pediatric clinic with her 5-year-old child, who has been suffering from fever and a rash for the past few days. Upon examination, the pediatrician notes the presence of fluid-filled vesicles and lesions at various stages of crusting. Based on these findings, a diagnosis of chickenpox is made. Which of the following statements about chickenpox is NOT true?
options: The causative organism responsible is varicella virus.
The lesions will be pruritic in nature.
Different stages of rash is present at the same time.
The lesion start appearing on the palm & sole and spread to the face and scalp.

explanation.short: Ans. D) The lesion start appearing on the palm & sole and spread to the face and scalp.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Chicken pox is caused by varicella booster virus .
• Option B: The initial exanthem consists of intensely pruritic erythematous macules that evolve through the papular stage to form clear, fluid-filled vesicles.
• Option C: the simultaneous presence of lesions in various stages of evolution is characteristic of varicella
• Educational Objective:
• Chickenpox , caused by the varicella-zoster virus , typically starts with lesions on the face, scalp, and trunk, spreading outward to the limbs, not starting from the palms and soles. The lesions can indeed be pruritic (itchy), and one of the hallmark features of chickenpox is that lesions at different stages of development (papules, vesicles, crusts) can be present simultaneously on the body.
• O p ghai 9 th edition page number 213

subject: Paediatrics
question:
An anxious mother brings her 8-year-old child with complaints of fever, sore throat, and a rash. On asking history, the child explains that the rash started on the chest and then spread to the rest of the body. On examination the rash consisted of small, red bumps that felt like sandpaper. The child also complains of a headache, nausea, and vomiting. On examination, the tongue is found to be red and swollen, the image is given below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on the clinical findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture14_lwLHS2W.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Streptococcal pharyngitis, is a bacterial infection of the throat caused by group A streptococcus bacteria . It can cause a sore throat, fever, and other symptoms , but is not typically associated with a rash.
• Option B: Rocky Mountain spotted fever, is a bacterial infection transmitted by ticks. It can cause fever, headache, and a characteristic spotted rash , but is not typically associated with a sore throat or the rash described in the case.
• Option D: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome , is a bacterial infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus that produces a toxin causing the skin to blister and peel. It typically presents with fever, a red, scalded-looking rash, and other symptoms, but is not typically associated with a sore throat or the rash described in the case.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of fever, sore throat, rash starting on the chest and spreading, rash texture described as sandpaper-like, along with headache, nausea, vomiting, and a red, swollen tongue (often referred to as "strawberry tongue")—i s indicative of Scarlet fever.
• Scarlet fever is a condition that arises as a result of an infection with group A Streptococcus, which produces a toxin leading to the characteristic rash.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1328

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother is concerned about her 3-week-old infant who is presenting with a rash, nasal discharge, and swollen lymph nodes. Upon further inquiry, the mother reveals she had a painless ulcer with a firm base during her pregnancy. The diagnosis was confirmed through darkfield microscopy. Based on the infant's clinical presentation and the mother's history, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Congenital Rubella Syndrome: Typically presents with cataracts, heart defects, and hearing loss . The mother's symptoms and the diagnostic method do not align with rubella.
• Option B: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection: Can lead to a variety of symptoms including hearing loss, microcephaly, and hepatosplenomegaly , but the mother's specific symptom and darkfield microscopy are not indicative of CMV.
• Option D: Congenital Toxoplasmosis: Often presents with chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and intracranial calcifications . The mother's symptom and the diagnostic method used do not suggest toxoplasmosis.
• Educational Objective:
• The chancre is a hallmark of primary syphilis in the mother, and the use of darkfield microscopy to visualize Treponema pallidum (the causative agent of syphilis) is a classic diagnostic technique. Congenital syphilis can manifest in newborns as rash, nasal discharge (snuffles), and lymphadenopathy, among other symptoms.
• Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1470-1477.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1470-1477.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
In a pediatric clinic in West Bengal, India, a mother brings her 9-month-old infant who recently experienced a high fever lasting three days, which then abruptly subsided. Following the resolution of the fever, the infant developed a rash that originated on the trunk and has now spread to the extremities. The rash is non-itchy, pink, and maculopapular. The infant appears in good health otherwise, with no significant irritability or feeding difficulties. Given the clinical course and the characteristic presentation of the rash, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/29/picture20_W2iAxpC.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Measles: Measles presents with a high fever, cough, conjunctivitis, and coryza, followed by a rash that usually starts on the face and behind the ears before spreading to the rest of the body. The presentation in this case does not fit the classic measles progression.
• Option B: Rubella: Rubella, or German measles, presents with a mild fever and a rash that also starts on the face and spreads downwards. However, rubella rashes tend to be finer and the disease is often accompanied by lymphadenopathy, which is not described in this case.
• Option D: Scarlet fever: Scarlet fever, caused by Streptococcus pyogenes, presents with a sore throat, high fever, and a sandpaper-like rash that typically starts on the chest and abdomen. The sequence of fever followed by rash in the absence of sore throat or the characteristic sandpaper rash makes scarlet fever less likely in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Roseola infantum in infants is characterized by a high fever followed by a rash that starts on the trunk and spreads to the extremities after the fever resolves. This distinguishes it from measles, rubella, and scarlet fever, where the rash has different characteristics and is often accompanied by other specific symptoms.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No-1594-1597.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 12-year-old child is brought to the outpatient department of a tertiary care hospital in New Delhi, India, with a chief complaint of dark-colored urine and noticeable yellowing of the skin. The child's parents also report that the child tires easily. Upon clinical examination, the healthcare provider observes clear signs of jaundice. To further investigate, a complete blood count (CBC) and a peripheral blood smear are ordered. The blood smear results show the presence of schistocytes (fragmented red cells) and an increased number of reticulocytes (immature red blood cells), indicative of active red blood cell production. Given the combination of clinical symptoms and laboratory findings, what is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Iron deficiency anemia : This is the most common type of anemia globally , often presenting with microcytic hypochromic anemia , but it does not typically lead to hemolysis, schistocytes, or an elevated reticulocyte count.
• Option C. Sickle cell anemia : While sickle cell anemia is a form of hemolyticanemia , it has distinct clinical and laboratory findings, such as sickle-shaped red blood cells on the smear, which were not mentioned in this case.
• Option D. Thalassemia : Thalassemia can cause hemolyticanemia but is characterized by specific morphological changes in red blood cells such as target cells and microcytosis, and the presence of schistocytes is not typical.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical and laboratory features of hemolyticanemia , including symptoms like dark urine and jaundice , along with laboratory findings such as schistocytes and elevated reticulocyte counts , which differentiate it from other types of anemia such as iron deficiency anemia, sickle cell anemia, and thalassemia.
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343
explanation.ref: OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 339-343

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 9-year-old child is taken to the pediatrician with complaints of unexplained pallor, fatigue, and shortness of breath. After a detailed clinical evaluation, the peripheral blood smear is examined, revealing the presence of bite cells and Heinz bodies within the red blood cells. Based on these findings, the physician diagnoses the child with Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency. Regarding G6PD deficiency, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked recessive disorder
The condition is more common in females than males
Patients may experience hemolysis after exposure to certain medications and infections
Treatment may include avoiding triggers and blood transfusions

explanation.short: Ans. B) The condition is more common in females than males
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. G6PD deficiency is an X-linked recessive disorder : True , the G6PD gene is located on the X chromosome, and the deficiency typically manifests in males.
• Option C. Patients may experience hemolysis after exposure to certain medications and infections: True , individuals with G6PD deficiency may have episodes of hemolysis when exposed to oxidative stress from various triggers.
• Option D. Treatment may include avoiding triggers and blood transfusions: True , the mainstay of treatment in G6PD deficiency is to avoid known triggers, and blood transfusions may be necessary for severe cases.
• Educational Objective:
• G6PD deficiency is an X-linked recessive disorder that affects males more commonly than females , can lead to hemolytic anemia upon exposure to certain triggers, and is managed by avoiding these triggers and, in some cases, with blood transfusions.
• Ref; OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 338
• OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 338

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 7-year-old child is evaluated for persistent respiratory tract infections and a notable history of abnormal bleeding, exhibiting excessive bleeding from minor injuries. A physical examination also reveals albinism, and an ophthalmological assessment indicates the presence of nystagmus. Laboratory tests, including a CBC, show normal platelet levels, prolonged BT, but normal CT, PT, and aPTT. Given the combination of recurrent infections, albinism, nystagmus, abnormal bleeding, and the specific laboratory findings, which genetic disorder should be suspected?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Bernard-Soulier syndrome may also present with similar symptoms but it doesn’t has the presence of pulmonary fibrosis and albinism.
• Option C. Hemophilia A : This is a clotting factor disorder (factor VIII deficiency) and would typically present with increase aPTT and is not associated with albinism.
• Option D. Hemophilia B : Also a clotting factor disorder (factor IX deficiency) with similar presentations to Hemophilia A and not associated with albinism or recurrent infections.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of frequent infections , abnormal bleeding , albinism , and nystagmus suggested Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome . This syndrome's unique constellation of symptoms, including platelet dysfunction without affecting the platelet count or coagulation cascade, sets it apart from other bleeding disorders such as Bernard-Soulier syndrome, Hemophilia A, and Hemophilia B.
• Ref; Nelson 21 st Edition page 3142
• Nelson 21 st Edition page 3142

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother with a noted history of polyhydramnios during pregnancy is brought in with the onset of labor pains. Upon delivery, the newborn is observed to have a notably large abdomen characterized by hepatosplenomegaly and ascites, in addition to skin edema. These clinical findings lead to a diagnosis of hydrops fetalis. Regarding hydrops fetalis, particularly in the context of hemoglobinopathies, which of the following statements is accurate?
options: Pentamers of excess beta globin chains
Pentamers of excess alpha globin chains
Tetramers of excess gamma globin chains

explanation.short: Ans. D) Tetramers of excess gamma globin chains
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Deletion of two alpha genes results in alpha-thalassemia trait or minor , which is generally asymptomatic or associated with only mild anemia, not hydrops fetalis.
• Option B: Pentamers of excess beta globin chains and Option C. Pentamers of excess alpha globin chains are not recognized configurations in hemoglobinopathies related to hydrops fetalis . Hemoglobin in these conditions forms abnormal tetramers, not pentamers.
• Educational Objective:
