• Option A: Physiological jaundice usually appears on the 2nd or 3rd day of life , peaking around the 4th to 5th day , but it would not typically cause significant weight loss or be the sole reason for concern in an otherwise healthy infant.
• Option B: Breast milk jaundice tends to appear later than breastfeeding jaundice , usually at the end of the first week into the second week of life and is associated with lipoprotein lipase enzyme in the mother's breast milk that can increase bilirubin levels . It wouldn't be the leading cause of jaundice in a 5-day-old infant, especially with significant weight loss.
• Option D: Cholestatic jaundice , which results from impaired bile flow , would not typically present this early without other signs of liver dysfunction and would be associated with pale or acholic stools due to the lack of bile excretion. (increased conjugated bilirubin)
• Educational Objective
• Exclusively breastfed infants have a different pattern of physiological jaundice as compared to artificially fed babies.
• Jaundice in breastfed babies usually appears between 24-72 hr of age , peaks by 5-15 days of life and disappears by the third week of life . One-third of all breastfed babies are detected to have mild clinical jaundice in the third week of life , which may persist into the 2nd to 3rd month of life in a few babies. This increased frequency of jaundice in breastfed babies is not related to characteristics of breast milk but rather to inadequate breastfeeding (breastfeeding jaundice). Ensuring optimum breastfeeding would help decrease this kind of jaundice.
• Ref - OP Ghai 8 th Edition Page No- 172

subject: Paediatrics
question: An eight-day-old male infant is brought to the emergency department in a state of dehydration and shock. Physical examination reveals generalized hyperpigmentation but normal external genitalia. Laboratory tests show hypoglycemia, hyponatremia with a sodium level of 123 mEq/L, and hyperkalemia with a potassium level of 7 mEq/L. Based on the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Acute gastroenteritis with dehydration: While this can cause dehydration and shock , it would not typically result in the combination of symptoms observed.
• Option C: Adrenal hemorrhage and shock: Adrenal hemorrhage could cause acute adrenal insufficiency , leading to shock, but it is less likely to present with the mentioned symptoms.
• Option D: Hyperaldosteronism: This condition would lead to hypertension, hypokalemia , and metabolic alkalosis , which is the opposite of what is observed in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH)
• A group of autosomal recessive defects in steroid synthesis , is characterized by deficiency of adrenocortical hormones on one hand and excess of steroid precursors on the other . CAH is the commonest adrenal disorder in childhood . 21-hydroxylase deficiency is the commonest form of CAH accounting for over 90% of all cases. This disorder is associated with diminished synthesis of the cortisol and aldosterone. Classic presentation of dehydration, shock, electrolyte imbalances, and hyperpigmentation in a neonate.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 11618

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her newborn to the hospital for a procedure, as shown in the image. The child has a history of hyperbilirubinemia. The infant is placed under a type of light as part of the treatment. Which mechanism is chiefly responsible for the reduction in the levels of serum bilirubin during the procedure depicted?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/09/picture1_OVQZJf1.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Photooxidation: This is a minor reaction , where, photoproducts are excreted in urine .
• Option B. Photoisomerization: This term refers to the light-induced conversion of bilirubin to a different isomer , specifically converting the Z, Z-bilirubin to the more polar E,Z-bilirubin and E,E-bilirubin isomers . These isomers are more water-soluble and can be excreted without conjugation. It is is one of the mechanisms by which the phototherapy acts whereas the main mechanism is Structural isomerization.
• Option D. Conjugation: Conjugation is a liver process where bilirubin is made water-soluble by adding a glucuronic acid molecule , allowing it to be excreted in bile . This is not the process used by phototherapy to reduce bilirubin; rather, it is the natural metabolic pathway that may be deficient in newborns, leading to hyperbilirubinemia.
• Educational objective:
• Phototherapy remains the mainstay of treating hyperbilirubinemia in neonates. It acts by converting insoluble bilirubin (unconjugated) into soluble isomers that can be excreted in urine and feces . The bilirubin molecule isomerizes to harmless forms under blue-green light (460-490 nm). Structural isomerization is mainly responsible for phototherapy induced decline in TSB .
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 171.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A pediatric resident is reviewing neonatal reflexes with a medical student. They observe four different infants demonstrating various reflexes as shown in images labeled A, B, C, and D. The resident asks the student to match the reflexes to the images shown. Which of the following is the correct match of the neonatal reflexes to their corresponding images? Asymmetrical tonic neck reflex Palmar Grasp Reflex Parachute reflex Moro’s Reflex

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/09/picture3_20mbyRT.png]

explanation.short: Ans. D) A4 B3 C1 D2
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• A4 - Moro’s Reflex: Image A displays an infant with arms outstretched and crying , which is characteristic of the Moro reflex . This startle reflex is typically elicited by a sudden loss of support , causing the infant to react with arms flinging outwards and then curling in .
• B3 - Parachute Reflex: Image B shows an infant being held horizontally face down , appearing to extend the arms outward as if to break a fall , indicative of the parachute reflex . This reflex develops later, usually around 6 to 7 months of age, and is not present at birth.
• C1 - Asymmetrical Tonic Neck Reflex: Image C illustrates an infant lying with head turned to one side , the arm and leg on the face side are extended (fencer's position), and the opposite arm and leg are flexed , which is indicative of the asymmetrical tonic neck reflex.
• D2 - Palmar Grasp Reflex: Image D shows an infant grasping an adult's finger , demonstrating the palmar grasp reflex . This reflex is present at birth and fades around 5 to 6 months of age.
• Educational objective:
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No-141.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
On your first day of pediatric rotation, you observe a mother engaged in a care practice with her newborn. She holds the baby close to her chest, providing warmth and comfort. As a part of this care strategy, which of the following is NOT typically included?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/09/picture1_68K2Arb.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Skin-to-skin contact - Early , continuous , and prolonged skin-to skin contact
• Option B: Exclusive breast feeding - Promotes lactation and facilitates feeding , provides adequate nutrition.
• Option D: Early discharge and follow up care - desired weight gain helps in early discharge and regular follow up care to identify problems for early intervention.
• Educational Objective
• Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC ) is a special way of caring for low-birth-weight babies . It fosters their health and well-being by promoting.
• Effective thermal control Breastfeeding Infection prevention and Bonding.
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition page 388

subject: Paediatrics
question: Saheli recently gave birth to a full-term neonate who, at birth, was observed to have a noticeable swelling on the right parietal region of the head. The swelling is characterized as soft, fluctuant, and notably confined by the suture lines, not extending beyond them. Given these clinical findings, what is the most likely diagnosis for Saheli's child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Caput succedaneum is an edematous swelling that is often present at birth and crosses suture lines . It is usually caused by prolonged labor or fetal malposition and resolves within days without intervention.
• Option B: Subgaleal hematoma is a bleeding between the galea aponeurosis and the periosteum of the skull . It is often associated with significant neonatal morbidity and mortality due to its potential for massive hemorrhage and hypovolemic shock.
• Option D: Intracranial hemorrhage is a serious condition that occurs due to bleeding within the cranial cavity . It may present with seizure, apnea, or altered level of consciousness and requires immediate medical attention. Unlike cephalohematoma, it is not limited by suture lines and may lead to significant neurological deficits if left untreated.
• Educational Objective:
• Cephalohematoma , characterized by a soft, fluctuant swelling that does not cross suture lines , is a common neonatal condition resulting from birth trauma , involving blood collection between the skull and its periosteum. It differs from caput succedaneum , which can cross suture lines , subgaleal hematoma , which spreads widely , and intracranial hemorrhage , which presents with systemic symptoms . Cephalohematoma typically resolves spontaneously as the blood is reabsorbed over time.
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 139.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A neonate is delivered after a prolonged labor and noted to have a boggy, soft, and puffy swelling that covers the entire scalp. The mother had an uneventful pregnancy but experienced a lengthy second stage of labor. On examination, the swelling is not well-demarcated and crosses the cranial suture lines. Neonate is otherwise healthy with stable vital signs. Based on the clinical presentation and the accompanying image, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2023/05/09/picture1_WuxdcR2.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Cephalohematoma is a birth injury that occurs due to bleeding underneath the periosteum of the skull bone . It presents a soft, fluctuant swelling on the head that is limited by suture lines and may take longer to resolve than caput succedaneum.
• Option C: S ubgalea hematoma is a bleeding between the galea aponeurosis and the periosteum of the skull . It is often associated with significant neonatal morbidity and mortality due to its potential for massive hemorrhage and hypovolemic shock.
• Option D: Intracranial hemorrhage is a serious condition that occurs due to bleeding within the cranial cavity . It may present with seizure, apnea, or altered level of consciousness and requires immediate medical attention. Unlike caput succedaneum, it is not limited by suture lines and may lead to significant neurological deficits if left untreated.
• Educational objective:
• Caput succedaneum is a diffuse swelling of the scalp that can cross suture lines and is common after prolonged labor . It is important to distinguish this benign and self-limited condition from other scalp conditions like cephalohematoma , which does not cross suture lines , subgaleal hematoma , which is a more serious condition requiring prompt diagnosis and management, and intracranial hemorrhage , which presents with neurological symptoms and is a medical emergency .
• Ref - OP Ghai 9 th Edition Page No- 139.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-year-old child is present to the emergency department in shock, displaying signs of circulatory collapse. Despite immediate resuscitation efforts, the emergency team is unable to establish intravenous access. Considering the critical condition and the urgent need for vascular access to deliver fluids and medications, what is the next best step in the management of this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Intracardiac infusion: This is a rarely used , high-risk procedure that is considered only when other methods of access are not available or have failed, and the patient is in extremis. It carries significant risks and is not the first alternative when intravenous access cannot be obtained.
• Option C. Thoracotomy: This is a surgical procedure that is not indicated for the purpose of vascular access and is not appropriate in the context of shock without evidence of conditions such as a traumatic cardiac arrest requiring open cardiac massage.
• Option D. Intra-arterial access: While possible, intra-arterial access is not typically used for resuscitation purposes and is more complex to achieve than intraosseous access . It is not the preferred method in an emergency, especially in pediatric patients.
• Educational objective:
• Intraosseous cannulation is the recommended method for gaining rapid vascular access when intravenous attempts fail , especially in an emergency setting involving pediatric patients . The proximal tibia is the most common site for insertion . This method is relatively safe, fast, and effective for resuscitation in critical situations.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 9232

subject: Paediatrics
question: On the second day of life, a neonate is rushed to the emergency department with signs of severe respiratory distress and excessive frothing at the mouth. There were no significant events reported during the prenatal period, and the birth was at full term with no immediate postnatal complications until this episode. Given the acute presentation and the associated clinical signs, which syndrome is most likely to be associated with this child's condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CHARGE syndrome is an acronym for coloboma , heart disease , atresia of the choanae , retarded growth and mental development , genital anomalies , and ear malformations and hearing loss.
• Option B. Denys-Drash syndrome (DDS) is a rare disorder consisting of the triad of congenital nephropathy , Wilms tumor, and intersex disorders resulting from mutations in the Wilms tumor suppressor (WT1) gene.
• Option D. Russell-Silver syndrome is a rare disorder characterized by intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), poor growth after birth , a relatively large head size , a triangular facial appearance , a prominent forehead, body asymmetry and significant feeding difficulties.
• Educational Objective:
• The neonate with EA typically has frothing and bubbling at the mouth and nose after birth as well as episodes of coughing , cyanosis , and respiratory distress . Feeding exacerbates these symptoms , causes regurgitation , and can precipitate aspiration . Aspiration of gastric contents via a distal fistula causes more damaging pneumonitis than aspiration of pharyngeal secretions from the blind upper pouch . The infant with an isolated TEF in the absence of EA (“H-type” fistula) might come to medical attention later in life with chronic respiratory problems, including refractory bronchospasm and recurrent pneumonias.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition -Page NO- 7661-7667.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pediatrician evaluates the primitive reflexes of a newborn during a routine checkup. The physician is specifically assessing the Moro reflex. Which sequence correctly identifies the stages of the Moro reflex in the order they occur? Extension and abduction of upper limbs Anterior flexion of upper limb Opening of hands

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The Moro reflex , also known as the startle reflex , is a primitive reflex seen in newborns . The correct sequence of the Moro reflex:
• Opening of hands (28 weeks) and Extension & Abduction of upper limbs (32 weeks) and followed by Adduction of upper limbs 37 weeks .
• This reflex is typically present at birth and fades around 4 to 6 months of age .
• Note - The most common cause of depressed or absent Moro reflex is a HIE stage3 (hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy), Down syndrome & kernicterus. An asymmetrical Moro reflex is indicative of brachial plexus injury.
• Educational Objective:
• The sequential appearance of Moro’s reflex –
• sequential appearance of Moro’s reflex –
• Ref - OP Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 141.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A concerned mother presents her infant who exhibits signs consistent with aplastic anemia. After a thorough clinical evaluation, the decision is made to proceed with a bone marrow biopsy to further investigate the cause of the anemia. In infants, from which bone should the bone marrow sample be taken for biopsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Sternum is not preferred in child because of the associated pain .
• Option C: Posterior superior iliac crest is used for bone marrow biopsy in older children .
• Option D: I liac crest (Posterior superior iliac crest) is the most commonly used bone for bone marrow biopsy in older children and adults.
• Educational Objective:
• In infants and very young children , the tibia is the preferred site for bone marrow biopsy . This is because, in this age group , the bone marrow of the tibia is readily accessible and provides a safe site for aspiration. The sternum is generally avoided due to the risk of injury to underlying structures, and the iliac crest is typically used in older children and adults .
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 2601

subject: Paediatrics
question: A neonatal resuscitation team is attending to a 30-week preterm infant in the delivery room who requires resuscitation, including intravenous access for medication and fluid administration. Given the gestational age and the delicate nature of the infant, which size IV cannula is appropriate for use in this scenario?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: 18 G is too large and not appropriate for a preterm infant , as it may cause damage to the veins.
• Option B: 22 G is appropriate for preterm infants weighing more than 1000 grams , but may be too large for infants less than 1000 grams.
• Option C: 14 G is too large and not appropriate for a preterm infant , as it may cause damage to the veins.
• Educational Objective:
• 24 G and 26 G for infants less than 1000 grams, and 22 G for infants weighing more than 1000 grams.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 2600

subject: Paediatrics
question: A term male neonate, born to a 22-year-old primigravida mother via vaginal delivery, exhibits immediate postnatal cyanosis, respiratory distress, and a scaphoid abdomen. On physical examination, the heart sounds are notably heard on the right side, and there is diminished breath sound on the left side of the chest. Considering the clinical presentation, what is the most appropriate initial step in the resuscitation of this newborn?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Intubate with an endotracheal tube
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: IV bicarbonate cannot be used in this case , As the initial management is to maintain the airway.
• Option B: Bag-and-mask ventilation is contraindicated in case of diaphragmatic hernia.
• Option D: The initial management is to secure the airway followed by initiating the chest compressions.
• Educational Objective:
• Initial management Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia - The management of CDH is required to provide early , appropriate respiratory support . In the delivery room , infants with respiratory distress should be rapidly stabilized with endotracheal intubation . Prolonged mask ventilation in the delivery room , which enlarges the stomach and small bowel and thus makes oxygenation more difficult , must be avoided and a naso- or orogastric tube placed immediately for decompression . Gentle ventilation with permissive hypercapnia reduces lung injury , need for ECMO , and mortality . Factors that contribute to pulmonary hypertension (hypoxia, acidosis, hypothermia) should be avoided.
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131; Nelson 21 st edition pg 4063

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 35-year-old woman delivers a male infant at 38 weeks of gestation. The delivery process proceeds without complications, but the newborn appears lethargic immediately after birth and presents with bradycardia. Initial resuscitative efforts include oxygenation via bag and mask followed by endotracheal intubation. Despite these interventions, the infant's heart rate remains at 40 beats per minute. Based on the current clinical status, what is the most appropriate subsequent step in the management of this neonate?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Administer IV atropine: An anticholinergic medication used to treat bradycardia in adults and older children , primarily when it's thought to be due to vagal stimulation . In neonates , the most common cause of bradycardia is hypoxia , and the primary treatment is effective ventilation, not atropine.
• Option C. Administer IV epinephrine: Epinephrine is used in neonatal resuscitation if the heart rate remains very low (below 60 bpm) despite 60 seconds of effective chest compressions combined with adequate ventilation . It is not the first step in management but is considered if initial resuscitative efforts (ventilation and chest compressions) fail to increase the heart rate.
• Option D. Administer IV calcium chloride: Used in specific situations such as hypocalcemia , hyperkalemia , and calcium channel blocker overdose . It is not typically used in standard neonatal resuscitation, especially not as an initial step.
• The first actions in neonatal resuscitation are ensuring effective ventilation and, if necessary, chest compressions . Calcium chloride would only be considered under very specific circumstances and usually after other more common causes of neonatal distress have been addressed.
• Educational Objective:
• In neonatal resuscitation , If the HR is less than 60 bpm despite PPV with 100% oxygen , then chest compressions are given for 60 seconds .
• If the HR is still less than 60 bpm , then drug therapy (usually epinephrine) is indicated.
• Neonatal Resuscitation Algorithm-2015 update
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A term infant, delivered vaginally following an uneventful pregnancy, presents with an unusual pattern of respiratory distress. The healthcare team observes that the infant appears well and pink while crying, but exhibits significant respiratory distress and becomes dusky when not crying. This pattern suggests a specific type of airway obstruction that is relieved by crying. Based on this clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Diaphragmatic hernia: This condition involves the abnormal protrusion of abdominal organs into the thoracic cavity through an opening in the diaphragm . It typically presents with severe respiratory distress shortly after birth due to lung compression but is not characterized by the specific pattern of distress improving with crying.
• Option C. Meconium aspiration: This occurs when a newborn inhales a mixture of meconium and amniotic fluid into the lungs around the time of delivery , which can lead to breathing difficulties . The respiratory distress associated with meconium aspiration is not typically relieved by crying.
• Option D. Pneumothorax: A pneumothorax in a newborn is an accumulation of air in the pleural space , which can lead to collapsed lung and respiratory distress . Like the other conditions listed, the symptoms of pneumothorax are not relieved by crying.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation of choanal atresia - the distinctive pattern of respiratory distress that is temporarily relieved by crying. Prompt diagnosis and management of this congenital condition , which requires immediate intervention to establish a secure airway for the affected infant .
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Neonate is delivered via emergency cesarean section due to fetal distress, with the presence of meconium-stained amniotic fluid noted during the delivery. Immediately post-delivery, the neonatal team observes that the infant does not initiate crying, displays hypotonia, and has a heart rate measured at 90 beats per minute. The initial assessment reveals a term infant with some respiratory effort but reduced muscle tone and a heart rate persistently below the normal range for neonates. Given the clinical presentation of meconium exposure, lack of spontaneous crying, hypotonia, and bradycardia, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention by the neonatal resuscitation team to manage this infant's condition?
options: Suctioning the residual meconium from the hypopharnyx
Intubating the trachea and suctioning meconium from lower airway

explanation.short: Ans. B) Starting positive pressure ventilation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Drying and stimulation: While drying and providing tactile stimulation are important initial steps in neonatal resuscitation , they may not be sufficient for an infant with significant respiratory distress and bradycardia . Such a neonate requires more immediate and active intervention to support breathing and circulation.
• Option C. Suctioning the hypopharynx: The latest neonatal resuscitation guidelines have moved away from routine immediate suctioning of meconium-stained amniotic fluid in the airway , especially in infants who are not vigorous . The emphasis is now on ensuring adequate ventilation and oxygenation as the first-line intervention.
• Option D. Intubating and suctioning the lower airways: This approach is reserved for infants who are not vigorous and have thick meconium staining . However, the immediate priority is to establish effective ventilation with PPV, which can often obviate the need for immediate intubation solely for suctioning purposes.
• Educational Objective:
• As per latest NRP guidelines , there is no role of routine tracheal suction and stimulation in non-vigorous baby born through meconium-stained liquor .
• Positive pressure ventilation is the cornerstone of management for infants with inadequate respiratory effort and bradycardia , taking precedence over other interventions such as tactile stimulation or meconium suctioning .
• Ref- O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: Upon arrival in the operating theater, you are immediately briefed on a 1-minute-old neonate who has experienced apnea and has been receiving bag and mask ventilation for the past 30 seconds. Your initial assessment reveals a heart rate of 110 beats per minute and observable spontaneous breathing efforts by the neonate. Given this clinical scenario, what is the most appropriate course of action to take for this neonate's management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Continue ventilation for 30 sec more & B. Continue ventilation for 60 sec more: Continuing bag and mask ventilation might be considered if the neonate was still showing signs of respiratory distress or had a heart rate below 100 beats per minute . In this scenario, however, with the neonate demonstrating spontaneous breathing and an adequate heart rate, continued positive pressure ventilation is unnecessary and could potentially be harmful.
• Option C. Intubate and start ventilation: Intubation and mechanical ventilation are indicated for neonates who do not respond adequately to initial resuscitation efforts , including bag and mask ventilation , or those with specific conditions requiring advanced airway management. Given the neonate's improved condition, this intervention is not warranted at this time.
• Educational Objective:
• As per NRP guidelines , heart rate above 100 per min and spontaneous breathing efforts are an indication to stop ventilation and give post resuscitation care .
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: On your first day in the pediatric department, the consulting physician decides to test your knowledge with a series of questions. You confidently answer most of them until you're presented with a particularly challenging one regarding neonatal resuscitation. The question is about identifying the absolute contraindication for using bag and mask ventilation. Which of the following conditions is considered an absolute contraindication for the application of bag and mask ventilation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Tracheoesophageal fistula: While bag and mask ventilation can be challenging in neonates with a tracheoesophageal fistula due to the risk of gastric distension and potential worsening of respiratory distress , it is not considered an absolute contraindication. These neonates require careful airway management, often with early intubation to secure the airway and prevent complications.
• Option B. Meconium aspiration: In cases of meconium aspiration syndrome , the initial steps of resuscitation might include suctioning of the airways before ventilation . However, bag and mask ventilation is not absolutely contraindicated and may be necessary if the baby is not breathing effectively after initial clearing of the airway.
• Educational Objective:
• Congenital diaphragmatic hernia is an absolute Contraindication for bag and mask ventilation
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 130.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A newborn infant, delivered at 38 weeks of gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery, is observed to have inadequate respiratory effort and a heart rate below 100 beats per minute immediately post-delivery. The infant is also noted to be cyanotic and not breathing effectively. Upon being alerted, the neonatal resuscitation team arrives quickly at the scene. Considering the urgent need for neonatal resuscitation, what is the most appropriate initial intervention to undertake for this infant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Chest compressions are indicated in cases where the newborn has a heart rate of less than 60 beats per minute despite adequate ventilation . This is not the most appropriate initial step in the clinical case presented, as the heart rate is between 60-100 beats per minute .
• Option C. Epinephrine administration is indicated in cases where the newborn has a heart rate of less than 60 beats per minute despite adequate ventilation and chest compressions . This is not the most appropriate initial step in the clinical case presented, as the heart rate is between 60-100 beats per minute.
• Option D: Umbilical vein catheterization is not an initial step in neonatal resuscitation . It may be indicated later in the resuscitation process if intravenous access is needed for medications or fluids.
• Educational Objective:
• Neonatal Resuscitation Algorithm-2015 update
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 130.

subject: Paediatrics
question: While preparing for your upcoming Viva examination, you engage in a discussion with your peers about the key differences between adult and pediatric resuscitation. Which of the following statements accurately reflects a distinction between resuscitation procedures in adults compared to those in children?
options: More ventilation to be given compared to chest compression
Infant myocardium more refractory to hypoxia because of congenital heart disease
Ventricular dysrhythmias are uncommon in children
Dissolved aortic oxygen concentration is more in pre-attack state

explanation.short: Ans. C) Ventricular dysrhythmias are uncommon in children.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Hypoxia induced bradycardia are more important cause and that is why rescue breaths are more important in pediatric resuscitation .
• Option A and D: More ventilation is given in children is because most of the cardiac arrest in pediatric age group are due to respiratory failure and not due to primary cardiac event.
• So, more ventilation has better outcome.
• Educational Objectives:
• The fundamental differences between adult and pediatric resuscitation , particularly the etiology of cardiac arrest . Recognizing that ventricular dysrhythmias are uncommon in pediatric cardiac arrest compared to adults is crucial for appropriate resuscitation efforts and interventions.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 1275

subject: Paediatrics
question: In the neonatal intensive care unit, a premature newborn presenting with respiratory distress requires immediate resuscitation. The infant's weight is recorded at 1,500 grams. Given the critical need for precise and controlled ventilation in such a delicate patient, selecting the correct size of the Ambu bag (manual resuscitator) is essential. Which of the following Ambu bag sizes is most appropriate for the resuscitation of this premature infant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: A 150 mL Ambu bag is too small for a premature infant weighing 1,500 grams . This size would not deliver enough tidal volume to effectively ventilate the infant.
• Option C and D: A 500 mL and 750 ml Ambu bag may be too large for a premature infant weighing 1,500 grams . This size may deliver too much tidal volume, which can be harmful to the infant.
• Educational objective:
• The importance of selecting the appropriate equipment size in neonatal resuscitation to ensure effective and safe ventilation . Using a 250 mL Ambu bag for a premature infant weighing 1,500 grams is appropriate to provide the necessary respiratory support without causing injury to the infant's delicate respiratory structures .
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a routine newborn examination, a full-term infant is suddenly found unresponsive and without a detectable pulse. Immediate preparations for cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) are made. As you review the neonatal CPR protocol in your mind, you recall several key principles. However, one of the following statements is incorrect regarding neonatal CPR. Identify the incorrect statement:
options: The appropriate sequence of steps for neonatal CPR is airway, breathing, circulation
In neonates, the airway should be managed first, followed by chest compressions if the heart rate is less than 60 beats per minute despite adequate ventilation
Chest compressions should be performed after the airway has been managed in neonatal CPR
In neonatal CPR, the recommended compression-to-ventilation ratio is 2:1

explanation.short: Ans. D) In neonatal CPR, the recommended compression-to-ventilation ratio is 2:1.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: This statement is true . The appropriate sequence of steps for neonatal CPR is airway, compressions, breathing.
• Option B: This statement is true . In neonates, the airway should be managed first, followed by chest compressions if the heart rate is less than 60 beats per minute despite adequate ventilation.
• Option C: This statement is true . Chest compressions should be performed after the airway has been managed in neonatal CPR.
• Educational Objective:
• In neonatal CPR , the recommended compression-to-ventilation ratio is 3:1 for a single provider and 9:3 for two providers .
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A newborn girl is delivered via emergency cesarean section to a 34-year-old mother whose pregnancy was marked by hypertension and concerning fetal heart rate patterns noted during prenatal monitoring. At birth, the baby is observed to be coated in thick green meconium. Upon a thorough examination, the infant is found to be apneic, bradycardic, and displaying poor muscle tone. As the pediatrician on duty, you are tasked with initiating resuscitation. What is the first step you should take in the resuscitation process for this neonate?
options: Suction the mouth & nose and start bag & mask ventilation

explanation.short: Ans. B) Suction the mouth & nose and start bag & mask ventilation.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: IV bicarbonate cannot be used in this case , As the initial management is to maintain the airway.
• Option C: Bag-and-mask ventilation can be initiated once the airway is cleared .
• Option D: Chest compressions can be initiated only after the clearance of the airway .
• Educational Objective:
• Clearing the airway of meconium through suctioning before initiating positive pressure ventilation is crucial to prevent meconium aspiration syndrome , followed by respiratory support as needed to stabilize the infant's condition .
• Ref - O P Ghai 9th Edition Page No- 129-131.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her child with a history of narrow stream of urine for long days. She complaints that he often gets infected because of this. On examination, a suspicion of urethral stricture is made. All of the following are complications of urethral stricture except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Urethral Calculi : Urethral strictures can lead to urinary retention and stasis , predisposing to the formation of urethral calculi (stones).
• Option C. Bilateral Hydronephrosis : Urethral obstruction caused by strictures can result in back pressure on the kidneys , leading to bilateral hydronephrosis due to dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces.
• Option D. Periurethral Abscess : Urinary stasis caused by urethral strictures can predispose to the development of periurethral abscesses due to bacterial infection in the surrounding tissues.
• Educational objective :
• Urinary Incontinence is not a complication of urethral stricture, whereas urinary retention can be a complication of it.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 559 Page No- From 11076.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her 6-month-old male child with complaints of dribbling of urine and recurrent urinary tract infections. During her prenatal visits, the doctor mentioned abnormal radiographic findings, but she forgot about it due to the long gap since her last pregnancy. Upon reviewing her old medical file, you find the following ultrasound (USG) image. Based on the clinical features and the image provided, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/23/picture4_ROz1eXV.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ureterocele : While ureterocele is a congenital anomaly involving the distal ureter, it typically presents with a cystic dilatation at the ureteric orifice within the bladder, rather than causing obstruction in the urethra. There will be an adder head or Cobra head appearance on IVP .
• Option C. Urethral stricture : Urethral stricture refers to narrowing of the urethra due to scar tissue or inflammation. While it can cause urinary flow obstruction, it typically presents differently and is less common in infants.
• Option D. Neurogenic bladder : Neurogenic bladder dysfunction is often associated with conditions affecting the central or peripheral nervous system , leading to abnormal bladder function. While it can cause urinary symptoms, the clinical presentation and imaging findings in this case are more suggestive of posterior urethral valves. This will show the Christmas tree appearance not the keyhole appearance.
• Educational objective :
• The clinical presentation of dribbling of urine and recurrent urinary tract infections in a male child , along with the history of prenatal radiographic findings suggestive of an abnormality , raises suspicion for posterior urethral valves (PUV).
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 555 Page No- From 10988.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 5-month-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother due to recurrent fever without a clear source. This is his fourth episode since birth. On examination, abdominal masses are palpable bilaterally, which resolve after the child urinates. A urinalysis reveals the presence of white blood cells (WBCs), suggestive of a urinary tract infection (UTI). What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this child?
options: Urine culture for UTI, USG and MCU for Ureterocele.
Urine culture for UTI, USG and MCU for PUV
Urine culture for UTI, USG and MCU for Hydroureteronephrosis
Urine culture for UTI, USG and MCU for Urethral stricture.

explanation.short: Ans. B)  Urine culture for UTI, USG and MCU for PUV
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . Urine culture for UTI, USG, and MCU for ureterocele: Ureterocele is a cystic dilatation of the distal ureter , which may cause urinary obstruction . However, the presentation described is more suggestive of PUV rather than ureterocele.
• Option C . Urine culture for UTI, USG, and MCU for hydroureteronephrosis : Hydroureteronephrosis refers to dilation of the ureter and renal pelvis due to urinary obstruction . While it can be associated with PUV, the specific evaluation for PUV with VCUG is warranted in this case.
• Option D . Urine culture for UTI, USG, and MCU for urethral stricture : Urethral stricture is an uncommon cause of urinary obstruction in infants and is less likely given the clinical presentation described.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation suggestive of posterior urethral valves (PUV) in an infant with recurrent fever and urinary symptoms , and to determine the appropriate diagnostic evaluation, including urine culture for UTI , ultrasonography (USG) of the kidneys and bladder , and voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) to evaluate for PUV.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 555 Page No- From 10988.

subject: Paediatrics
question: On your first day in the pediatric department, while exploring, you encounter a child with urogenital abnormalities. A third-year postgradate (PG) resident passes by and asks you which congenital anomaly is associated with an increased risk of bladder carcinoma. What will be your reply?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Ureterocele : Ureterocele is a congenital anomaly involving the dilatation of the distal ureter within the bladder . While it can lead to complications such as urinary obstruction and vesicoureteral reflux , it is not typically associated with an increased risk of bladder carcinoma.
• Option C. Right-sided renal agenesis : Right-sided renal agenesis refers to the absence of the right kidney. This condition is not directly associated with an increased risk of bladder carcinoma.
• Option D. Duplication ureter : Duplication ureter is a congenital anomaly where the ureteric bud bifurcates during embryonic development , resulting in two ureters draining a single kidney . While it can lead to complications such as urinary tract obstruction and vesicoureteral reflux, it is not typically associated with an increased risk of bladder carcinoma.
• Educational Objective
• Bladder exstrophy is associated with an increased risk of bladder carcinoma and is important for the management and follow-up of patients with this condition.
• Ref - Nelson 21 st Edition page 11019

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother in India brings her 2-day-old son to the pediatric clinic with concerns of his condition at birth. She reports that her son has had constant leakage of urine from the lower abdominal region since delivery. On examination, the infant has a wide separation of the pubic bones and a visibly externalized bladder through the abdominal wall, as shown in the accompanying image. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/23/picture6_Y9LYUkO.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Posterior Urethral Valves : This condition is an obstructive developmental anomaly in male infants , presenting with a distended bladder , difficulty with urination , and potentially renal damage . It would not present with an externalized bladder or pubic diastasis and typically leads to a range of symptoms from urinary tract infections to renal failure.
• Option B. Vesicoureteral Reflux : It involves the backflow of urine from the bladder into the ureters and potentially the kidneys . While it can present with recurrent UTIs and may be associated with other congenital abnormalities of the urinary tract, it does not cause the anatomical changes seen in the image, such as an everted bladder and diastasis of the pubic symphysis.
• Option D. Prune Belly Syndrome : Also known as Eagle-Barrett syndrome , it is characterized by a triad of abdominal muscle deficiency , cryptorchidism (undescended testes), and urinary tract anomalies . The abdominal wall is thin and wrinkled, resembling a prune, which is not consistent with the exposed bladder in the image. It does not present with bladder exstrophy but may include a range of urinary tract malformations and symptoms related to the urinary system.
• Educational Objective:
• Bladder exstrophy is a rare congenital anomaly where the bladder is open and exposed on the lower abdominal wall . It is due to failure of fusion of the lower abdominal wall and bladder during embryonic development.
• Clinical features include a wide pubic diastasis, everted bladder neck, and urine leakage from the lower abdominal wall. Surgical reconstruction is the mainstay of treatment.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 556 Page No- 11019 to 11022.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 16-year-old female presents to a rural health clinic, with a 1-day history of stabbing pain in her left groin, accompanied by white vaginal discharge and mild dysuria that has persisted for the past week. She denies experiencing any abnormal vaginal bleeding, noting her last menstrual period was about 3 weeks ago. The patient has no history of sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) and has been sexually active for the past year, relying on oral contraceptives for birth control. She mentions that her sexual partners sporadically use condoms. Upon examination, she is found to be afebrile, but exhibits tenderness in the left lower quadrant and suprapubic area during deep palpation. In the context of the Indian health system, what is the most appropriate next step in her management?
options: Perform urinalysis and urine pregnancy testing
Perform pelvic examination and Pap smear

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Reassure : It would be premature to reassure the patient without further investigation, given her symptoms and sexual history.
• Option C. Perform Ultrasonography : Ultrasonography could be useful later for diagnostic clarification but is not the immediate next step before confirming UTI or pregnancy status.
• Option Perform Pelvic Examination and Pap Smear : While a pelvic exam might be necessary for a comprehensive evaluation, it should follow initial urine tests. A Pap smear is not indicated in this acute setting and is generally not recommended for individuals under the age of 21.
• Educational Objective:
• Performing urinalysis and urine pregnancy testing is the initial step to be taken in all sexually active female with complaints of UTI.
• STD is a concern in this patient with pyuria and abdominal pain. In addition to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), possible etiologies include UTI, ovarian torsion, ovarian cyst, and ectopic pregnancy. So, the first step in evaluation should always be urinalysis and pregnancy testing.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 553 Page No- From 10943.

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-year-old boy is brought to your CHC in a suburban area of India by his mother. She reports her son has been experiencing irritation and itching of the penis, along with a burning sensation during urination. The child has not reported an increase in the frequency of urination or changes in the appearance of his urine. Upon doing laundry, his mother observed a whitish-yellow stain on his underwear. The boy is uncircumcised, and his medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, there is significant erythema of the glans penis and a whitish exudate is noted upon retraction of the foreskin. A urinalysis does not show any abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Gonococcal urethritis : Caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae , it typically presents with purulent discharge and can be confirmed with gram stain or culture . Urinalysis can sometimes show signs of infection.
• Option C. Nongonococcal urethritis : Usually caused by Chlamydia trachomatis or Mycoplasma genitalium , it presents with mucopurulent or clear discharge , but urinalysis would often show signs of infection such as white blood cells.
• Option D. Herpes infection : Genital herpes can cause painful vesicular lesions on the glans or shaft of the penis. It is less likely to present with whitish exudate and would not typically result in an unremarkable urinalysis if lesions were present.
• Educational Objective
• Candidal balanitis is an infection of the glans penis that is commonly caused by the fungus Candida albicans , particularly in uncircumcised males . The symptoms described, such as itching and burning of the penis , along with the whitish exudate upon retraction of the foreskin , are characteristic of this diagnosis. The unremarkable urinalysis helps rule out bacterial infections that would typically present with pyuria or bacteriuria.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her male child with clinical features of recurrent Urinary tract infection. On examination, the child was febrile. An Ultrasound examination reveals the presence of bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. A diagnosis of posterior urethral valve is suspected. What is the investigation of choice for posterior urethral valve?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anterograde urethroscopy is used for posterior urethral pathologies .
• Option B. Cystourethroscopy has less or no significance in diagnosis of PUV.
• Option D. Retrograde urethroscopy is used for anterior urethral pathologies
• Educational Objective
• Voiding cystourethroscopy (VCUG) is the investigation of choice for confirming the diagnosis of posterior urethral valves (PUV). PUV is a congenital anomaly characterized by abnormal membranous folds within the posterior urethra , leading to obstruction of urinary flow .
• VCUG allows direct visualization of the urethra and bladder during voiding , enabling identification of the obstructive valves . It also provides information on the severity of obstruction and associated bladder abnormalities.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 555 Page No- From 10988.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A pregnant woman presents for routine ultrasound at 28 weeks gestation. On examination, oligohydramnios was noted. A 4D USG reveals the findings of flattened nose, low-set ears, and limb deformities. The baby was delivered after a few weeks and the image of the baby is given below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on the history and clinical features?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/23/picture7_W1JCVeh.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Trisomy 21 : The clinical features mentioned in this case is not consistent with Down Syndrome . In case of it there will be clinical features of intellectual disability, Mongoloid slant of eyes, large tongue and single simian crease will be mentioned.
• Option C. Patau syndrome - trisomy 13 it also shows features of cleft lip and palate and VSD whereas the other symptoms are only characteristic of Potters syndrome.
• Option D. The clinical features mentioned in this case is not consistent with Turner Syndrome. In case of it there will be clinical features of short stature, widely placed nipples, shield type chest will be seen.
explanation.ref: Nelson 20 th Edition, Page No- 2554.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother brings her 3-year-old girl child with complaints of fever and crying during micturition. On examination, the left side loin of the child has a mass which resolves after urination. On routine urine analysis, UTI was suspected. On asking history, the mother admits to multiple episodes of UTI. USG shows hydronephrosis and the following appearance. What is the diagnosis based on the clinical features and radiological findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/23/picture5_iy5W8S8.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Vesicoureteric reflux : Vesicoureteric reflux involves the retrograde flow of urine from the bladder into the ureters , often leading to recurrent UTIs and, in severe cases, hydronephrosis. However, it typically does not present with a mass in the loin that resolves after urination.
• Option B. Hydronephrosis : Hydronephrosis refers to the dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces due to obstruction of urinary flow . While it can be associated with ureterocele, it is not the primary diagnosis in this case.
• Option D. Posterior urethral valve : Posterior urethral valve is a condition affecting male infants , leading to obstructive uropathy and features such as dribbling of urine, recurrent UTIs , and characteristic imaging findings. It is less common in female children.
• Educational Objective:
• Ureterocele is a congenital abnormality where the distal end of the ureter balloons within the bladder , leading to obstructive uropathy . This can result in hydronephrosis and recurrent UTIs. The characteristic ultrasound (USG) appearance often shows a cystic dilatation at the ureteric orifice within the bladder.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 10987

subject: Paediatrics
question: An 11-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with complaints of severe pain and swelling in his scrotum. On examination, his right testicle is found to be tender, high-riding, and horizontal. A preliminary diagnosis of testicular torsion was made. Which of the following clinical features is most commonly associated with testicular torsion in children?
options: Pain that improves with elevation of the affected testicle

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pain that improves with elevation of the affected testicle : This is not typically observed in testicular torsion . Elevation of the testicle may worsen the pain due to further compromise of blood flow.
• Prehn’s sign positivity which is seen in case of Epididymoorchitis.
• Option B. Nausea : While nausea can occur with testicular torsion due to severe pain , they are not specific to this condition and can be seen in various other causes of acute scrotal pain.
• Option D. Scrotal erythema and warmth : Scrotal erythema and warmth may be present in cases of epididymitis or orchitis , which are inflammatory conditions of the scrotum, but they are not typically seen in testicular torsion.
• Educational objective:
• Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency characterized by the twisting of the spermatic cord , leading to compromised blood flow to the testicle .
• Clinical features commonly associated with testicular torsion include sudden onset severe testicular pain, swelling, tenderness, and a high-riding, horizontal orientation of the affected testicle.
• The cremasteric reflex , which normally causes elevation of the testicle upon stroking the inner thigh , is typically absent in cases of testicular torsion due to the compromised blood supply.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21st Edition, Chapter 560 Page No- From 11092- 11096.

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A mother seeks medical attention for her 2-month-old son, who has been experiencing abdominal distension, constipation, and difficulty urinating since birth. During the physical examination, the physician notes the infant's abdomen is significantly distended and there is an apparent absence of abdominal muscles. Additionally, the child presents with an empty scrotum due to bilateral undescended testes. Based on the clinical presentation and the physical features observed, what is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/23/picture8_6E0qKof.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Bladder exstrophy : It involves the abnormal development and exposure of the bladder to the outside of the body , not typically associated with the absence of abdominal muscles or undescended testes.
• Option C. Hypospadias : A congenital condition where the opening of the urethra is on the underside of the penis , not at the tip. It does not involve abdominal distension, absence of abdominal muscles, or undescended testes.
• Option D. Posterior urethral valves : A condition that causes obstruction of urine flow in male infants , but it is not associated with the absence of abdominal muscles or the abdominal findings described.
• Educational Objective:
• Prune belly syndrome/ Eagle-Barrett syndrome , is characterized by a triad that includes the absence of abdominal muscles, undescended testes , and urinary tract anomalies .
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition page 2749

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to a pediatric outpatient department in a tertiary care hospital in India by his mother, who is concerned that her son has never passed urine from the tip of his penis. The child has otherwise been healthy with no significant medical history. On physical examination, the urethral orifice is not located at the usual position at the tip of the penis. The rest of the genital examination shows normal testicular descent and no other abnormalities. Which among the following is the most common congenital urethral anomaly?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Epispadias : This is a less common condition where the urethral opening is on the upper surface of the penis . It often presents with more severe anatomical anomalies and can be associated with bladder exstrophy.
• Option B. Urethral stenosis : This involves a narrowing of the urethra , which can lead to difficulty urinating but does not involve abnormal placement of the urethral opening.
• Option C. Bladder exstrophy : A rare congenital condition where the bladder and associated structures are exposed outside the abdomen . It is usually noticeable immediately after birth due to the visible bladder tissue and associated abnormalities.
• Educational Objective:
• Hypospadias is the most common congenital urethral anomaly and management involves surgical repair.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Page No- 1063.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A mother brings her 8-year-old boy with complaints of bedwetting. On asking history, she informs that he does about 3 to 4 times per week. On further evaluation, he has a normal urine stream, daytime urine continence, and no history of UTIs. His physical examination is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Use of enuresis alarm
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ultrasound of abdomen : Routine ultrasound of the abdomen is not indicated in the initial evaluation of primary nocturnal enuresis without other concerning symptoms or signs.
• Option B. Reassurance : While reassurance is an important aspect of management, it alone may not be sufficient for addressing primary nocturnal enuresis.
• Option D. Desmopressin acetate : Desmopressin acetate, a synthetic form of antidiuretic hormone (ADH), is another treatment option for primary nocturnal enuresis , particularly for children who do not respond to behavioral interventions such as enuresis alarms or who need additional help in achieving dry nights . However, it is typically reserved for cases where behavioral therapy has failed.
• Educational Objective:
• Nocturnal enuresis occurs in 15% of 5-year-olds with a resolution rate of 15% per year . Males are more frequently affected, and family history is common . Initial evaluation includes a history of wetting pattern, prior UTI, and developmental, social, and emotional history.
• The enuresis alarm has a success rate of 70% to 90% and requires parental support. Pharmacologic interventions include nighttime doses of imipramine or oral desmopressin acetate. Following use of desmopressin acetate, fluid intake is restricted to avoid hyponatremia. Pharmacologic treatment usually is reserved for special occasions.
explanation.ref: Nelson 21 st Edition, Chapter 558 Page No- From 11044.

subject: Paediatrics
question: ​​​​​ A 36-year-old male presents to clinic for a routine health check-up. He has no significant past medical history and leads an active lifestyle. During his respiratory examination, you note the following parameters: His respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute, tidal volume is measured at 400 mL, and dead space volume is estimated to be 150 mL. What is the patient's alveolar ventilation per minute?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The alveolar ventilation per minute can be defined as the total volume of new air entering the alveoli in one minute . The alveolar ventilation per minute can be expressed as Alveolar ventilation per min = (Tidal volume - dead space) x rate of respiration Here, tidal volume = 400 mL, respiratory rate = 16/min Dead space volume = 150 mL Alveolar ventilation /min = (400 - 150) x 16 = 250 x 16 = 4000 mL
• #Extra edge: Types of Dead Spaces –
• #Extra edge: Types of Dead Spaces
• Educational Objective:
• Understand and accurately calculate alveolar ventilation rates using patient respiratory parameters .
• Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 2 nd South Asia Edition, Pg No: 34
explanation.ref: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 2 nd South Asia Edition, Pg No: 34

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 42-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a four-week history of progressive breathlessness. He reports episodes of wheezing, especially at night and early in the morning. His past medical history includes occasional allergic rhinitis but no prior episodes of similar symptoms. On physical examination, you note expiratory wheezing bilaterally. He denies any history of smoking. A spirometry test is conducted diagnosis of bronchial asthma was made. Based on the clinical presentation and diagnosis, which of the following spirometry findings is most likely in this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Decreased FEV1, Decreased FEV1/FVC
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize decreased FVC and decreased FEV1/FVC ratio in spirometry as indicative of bronchial asthma in symptomatic patients.
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg No: 630

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 30-year-old woman presents to your clinic for a routine physical examination. She is a non-smoker and has no significant past medical history. During her respiratory assessment, you decide to evaluate her lung volumes and capacities as part of a complete pulmonary function test. What is the expected normal Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) in this Healthy Adult Female?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) is a term used in pulmonary physiology to describe the additional amount of air that can be inhaled after a normal tidal inhalation. Essentially, it's the extra volume of air that a person can draw into their lungs above the regular , relaxed inhalation. IRV is one component of lung volumes , which are typically measured during pulmonary function tests . In healthy adults, the IRV can vary based on factors such as age , gender , body size , and physical condition . On average: For adult males , the IRV is typically around 3000 to 3300 milliliters (mL) . For adult females , the IRV is usually slightly less , ranging from about 1900 to 2100 mL.
• Educational Objective
• Determine the normal range of Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) in healthy adult females, recognizing 3000 mL as a typical upper value.
• Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg No: 629
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg No: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question: A pulmonologist is assessing the respiratory function of a 30-year-old male athlete and measures the maximal voluntary ventilation (MVV). The doctor asks his medical students to estimate the normal value of MVV in a healthy adult male. During the assessment, subject moves 40 liters of air in and out of her lungs with maximum effort over 15 seconds. What is her MVV (in liters per minute)? Which of the following is the correct value?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The maximal voluntary ventilation can be defined as the largest volume of air that can be moved inside and outside the lungs in one minute by means of voluntary effort of respiration
• In the given example
• Air moved in 15 seconds = 40 L
• Hence, in one minute the MVV = 40 L/15 sec × 60 =160 L/min
• Among the options given, Option D. 160 L/min will be the appropriate one , rest all Option A, Option B and Option C are incorrect.
• The normal range for Maximal Voluntary Ventilation (MVV) varies depending on factors like age, sex, and body size, exercise or physical training but generally falls within the range of 50–250 L/min. A healthy, fit young male adult might have an MVV of around 140 - 170 L/min.
• Educational Objective:
• The objective of this question is to understand the concept of maximal voluntary ventilation (MVV) and its normal value in a healthy adult male, which is important in assessing lung function and respiratory muscle strength .
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25th Edition, Pg No: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question: During a respiratory physiology class, a medical professor presents a clinical scenario to test the students' understanding of lung volumes. He asks, "Which of the following equations correctly represents tidal volume?
options: Inspiratory Reserve Volume – Expiratory Reserve Volume

explanation.short: Ans. C) Inspiratory Capacity – Inspiratory Reserve Volume
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Inspiratory Capacity (IC) includes Tidal Volume ( TV ) and Inspiratory Reserve Volume ( IRV ). Subtracting Expiratory Reserve Volume ( ERV ) from IC does not yield TV.
• Option B. Subtracting ERV from IRV does not give TV. TV is the volume of air inhaled or exhaled in a normal breath .
• Option D. Subtracting Residual Volume (RV) from ERV does not provide TV. RV is the volume of air remaining in the lungs after a maximal exhalation .
• Tidal Volume = Inspiratory Capacity – Inspiratory Reserve Volume (TV = IC – IRV)
• #Extra Edge: Definitions and normal values of various lung volumes and capacities:
• Educational Objective:
• The objective of this question is to assess understanding of lung volumes and capacities , particularly the calculation of tidal volume within the context of respiratory physiology.
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg no: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 45-year-old male with a history of smoking for 10 years presented to OPD for regular checkup. He is asymptomatic but concerned about his lung health due to his smoking history. A physiciandecides to conduct a pulmonary function test to assess his lung volumes and capacities. Which lung volume measurement represents the total amount of air that can be exhaled after a maximal inhalation.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The vital capacity (VC) can be defined as the maximal volume of air expired from maximal inspiratory level and will be equal to the sum of tidal volume (TV), inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) and expiratory reserve volume (ERV). Lung volumes and capacity measurements. Lung volumes recorded by a spirometer . Lung capacities are determined from volume recordings .
• Educational Objective:
• Identify Vital Capacity as the lung volume measurement indicating the total amount of air exhaled after a maximal inhalation .
• Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg no: 629
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg no: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 30-year-old male presents to your clinic for a routine physical examination. He is a non-smoker with no significant past medical history. As part of the examination, you perform a spirometry test. The results show a tidal volume of 460 mL and an inspiratory capacity of 3380 mL. He also has a known expiratory reserve volume of 1060 mL. Calculate his IRV.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The additional volume of air that can be inspired above the tidal respiration is termed as Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) The Inspiratory Capacity (IC) can be defined as the sum of the tidal volume ( TV ) and the inspiratory reserve volume (IRV)
• Here, tidal volume = 460 mL,
• inspiratory capacity = 3380 mL
• IC = TV + IRV
• IRV = IC – TV = 3380 - 460 = 2920 mL
• # Lung volumes and capacity measurements . Lung volumes recorded by a spirometer . Lung capacities are determined from volume recordings .
• Educational Objective:
• Inspiratory Capacity (IC) can be defined as the sum of the Tidal Volume (TV) and the Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg no: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 45-year-old male with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) undergoes spirometry to assess his lung function. Based on the spirometry results, the physician asks the medical students to calculate a specific lung capacity or volume. Which of the following represents the correct calculation or interpretation of lung volumes and capacities in this clinical context?
options: Vital Capacity = Tidal Volume + Inspiratory Reserve Volume + Expiratory Capacity
Total Lung Capacity = Vital Capacity + Expiratory Reserve Volume
Functional Residual Capacity = Expiratory Reserve Volume + Residual Volume
Inspiratory Capacity = Total Lung Capacity - Residual Volume

explanation.short: Ans. C. Functional Residual Capacity = Expiratory Reserve Volume + Residual Volume
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . incorrect as the Vital Capacity = Tidal Volume + Inspiratory Reserve Volume + Expiratory Reserve Volume ( Not Expiratory Capacity): Vital Capacity (VC) is the maximum volume of air a person can exhale after a maximum inhalation, which is indeed the sum of TV, IRV, and ERV.
• Option B . incorrect as the Total Lung Capacity = Vital Capacity + Residual Volume (Not Expiratory Reserve Volume): Total Lung Capacity (TLC) is the total volume of air in the lungs after a maximal inspiration, which includes VC and RV. This statement is also correct.
• Option D . incorrect as the Inspiratory Capacity = Total Lung Capacity - Functional Residual Capacity ( Not Residual Volume): Inspiratory Capacity (IC) is the maximum volume of air that can be inhaled from the end-expiratory position, not TLC minus FRC.
• Educational Objective:
• The objective of this question is to test understanding of lung volumes and capacities, particularly in a clinical setting involving a patient with a respiratory condition like COPD.

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 54-year-old male presents to the medicine outpatient department with persistent cough, breathlessness, and recent unexplained weight loss. His medical history is significant for long-term exposure to environmental pollutants. After thorough evaluation, he is diagnosed with pulmonary fibrosis. Which of the following pulmonary function test findings is most likely to be observed in this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Decreased FVC, Normal FEV1/FVC
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This is a case of pulmonary fibrosis , which is a restrictive lung disease FVC refers to forced vital capacity, i.e. i.e. the total amount of air coming out of the lung in during quick and forceful expiration after maximum inspiration FEV refers to forced expiratory volume and FEV1 refers to forced expiratory volume in the first second i.e. amount of air coming out of the lung in 1st second during quick and forceful expiration after maximum inspiration. In restrictive lung diseases, the FVC will be decreased, but the ratio of FEV1/FVC will be normal or even increased, especially in severe condition due to drastic reduction in the FVC This can be used to differentiate between the obstructive and restrictive lung diseases . Lung disease measurements:
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that pulmonary fibrosis typically presents with decreased Forced Vital Capacity (FVC) and normal Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second ( FEV1 )/ FVC ratio in pulmonary function tests .
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg no: 630

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A 27-year-old man is breathing quietly. He then inhales as much air as possible and exhales as much air as he can, producing the spirogram shown in the previous figure. What is his Expiratory Reserve Volume (in Liters)?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/26/02.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) is the maximum extra volume of air that can be expired by forceful expiration after the end of a normal tidal expiration. ERV is equal to the difference between the Functional Residual Capacity (FRC, 3 L) and the Residual Volume (RV, 1 L).
• ERV = FRC – RV
• = 3L – 1L    = 2L
• # Lung volumes and capacity measurements. Lung volumes recorded by a spirometer . Lung capacities are determined from volume recordings .
• Educational Objective:
• The objective of this question is to test understanding of lung volumes and capacities , particularly the corelation of FRC, ERV and RV in a graph format.
explanation.ref: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 2 nd South Asia Edition, Pg no: 342

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 63-year-old male on ventilator support with a long history of smoking and chronic bronchitis is undergoing evaluation for respiratory difficulties. His spirometry shows a significant reduction in forced expiratory volume, and a CT scan reveals emphysematous changes in the lungs. In this scenario, what would be the expected changes in both anatomical and alveolar dead space?
options: Increased Anatomical Dead Space, Decreased Alveolar Dead Space
Decreased Anatomical Dead Space, Increased Alveolar Dead Space
Increased both Anatomical and Alveolar Dead Space
Decreased both Anatomical and Alveolar Dead Space

explanation.short: Ans. C) Increased both Anatomical and Alveolar Dead Space.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Inaccurate in this context. Chronic bronchitis and emphysema, particularly when the patient is on ventilator support , typically lead to an increase in both anatomical and alveolar dead space . Ventilation doesn't decrease alveolar dead space.
• Option B: Not relevant here. The conditions described, along with ventilator use , are more likely to increase both types of dead space rather than decrease anatomical dead space .
• Option D: Unlikely in this clinical scenario. Both anatomical and alveolar dead spaces tend to increase in COPD and with ventilator support.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand how chronic respiratory conditions like COPD and the use of mechanical ventilation can lead to increased anatomical and alveolar dead space , affecting gas exchange efficiency .
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg. no: 641

subject: Paediatrics
question:
A patient with chronic respiratory symptoms is undergoing a diagnostic workup. The attending pulmonologist is reviewing Weibel's classification of the bronchial tree with the medical team to assist in the localization of the pathology. Match the correct generation of bronchioles to their defining characteristics as per Weibel's model: Match the following:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/26/screenshot-2024-02-26-145827.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Statement A: Generation 7 - Main Bronchi (2) : This generation, consisting of the main bronchi, has cartilaginous rings and is part of the purely conducting airway , indicating the option for the main bronchi .
• Statement B: Generation 12 - Terminal bronchioles (1) : As the final segment of the conducting airways, the terminal bronchioles are correctly matched with generation 12, which is immediately prior to the respiratory portion of the airways .
• Statement C: Generation 17 - Respiratory bronchioles (3) : The transitional bronchioles at generation 17, where the first alveoli appear , match the respiratory bronchioles , marking the beginning of the respiratory zone .
• Statement D: Generation 23 - Alveolar sacs (4) : This generation is well within the respiratory zone and includes the alveolar sacs , where extensive gas exchange takes place, making it the correct match for the alveolar sacs .
• Educational Objective:
• The aim is to challenge the learner’s ability to integrate complex anatomical knowledge of the bronchial tree with clinical concepts, using Weibel's classification for precise localization of pulmonary lesions .
• (Refer : Ganong’s Review of Medical physiology, 25th edition, pg. no : 610, 611)

subject: Paediatrics
question: A 65-year-old female with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) presents to your clinic for a Follow-Up visit. She is currently on a maintenance Inhaler and reports moderate improvement in her symptoms. At which Lung Volume do the Elastic Recoil Forces of the lungs balance exactly with the Elastic Recoil Forces of the Chest Wall, a state particularly relevant in the management of patients with COPD?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Understand the significance of Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) as the lung volume where elastic recoil forces of the lungs balance with those of the chest wall , particularly in COPD management .
explanation.ref: Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 2 nd South Asia Edition, Pg. no: 342

subject: Paediatrics
question: Calculate the Expiratory Reserve Volume, if the Vital Capacity is 4380 mL, Tidal Volume is 480 mL and Inspiratory Reserve Volume is 2840 mL?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The Vital Capacity (VC) can be defined as the maximal volume of air expired from maximal inspiratory level The vital capacity will be equal to the sum of tidal volume (TV), inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) and expiratory reserve volume (ERV)
• VC = TV + IRV+ ERV
• ERV = VC – (TV + IRV)
• = 4380 – (480 + 2840)
• = 4380 – 3320 = 1060 mL
• # Lung volumes and capacity measurements. Lung volumes recorded by a spirometer . Lung capacities are determined from volume recordings .
• Educational Objective:
• To understand and calculate various lung volumes and capacities , which is essential for assessing pulmonary function and diagnosing respiratory conditions .
explanation.ref: Ganong’s Review of Medical Physiology, 25 th Edition, Pg. No: 629

subject: Paediatrics
question:
Difference in the Course of Inspiratory Loop and the Expiratory Loop in the Curve which leads to Hysteresis in the Lung compliance curve is due to:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/02/26/04.jpg]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Difference in Concentration of Surfactant during Inspiration and Expiration
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alveolar pressure does change between inspiration and expiration , contributing to the mechanics of breathing . However, this change doesn't directly explain hysteresis in the lung compliance curve, which is more related to surfactant dynamics.
